paragraphs: Adjectives + Prepositions
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 1 Grammar

In this grammar lesson, you will learn how adjectives and prepositions work together to make your sentences clearer and more interesting. By the end, you will be able to choose the right preposition for each adjective and use these combinations confidently in your writing and speaking.
What Are Adjectives + Prepositions?

An adjective is a word that describes a noun or a pronoun. It tells us about a quality, feeling, or characteristic. For example: proud, anxious, afraid, excited, interested, good, sorry, angry, kind, grateful, jealous, keen.
A preposition is a small word that shows the relationship between the adjective and the thing it refers to. Common prepositions include: of, about, in, at, for, with, to, on.
When an adjective needs more information to complete its meaning, it uses a preposition to connect to the noun or idea that follows. This is called an adjective + preposition combination.
For example:
I am proud of my work. (proud + of + my work)
She was anxious about the test. (anxious + about + the test)
They are interested in science. (interested + in + science)
Without the preposition, the sentence would feel incomplete. You would not say “I am proud my work” — you need the word of to connect the adjective to the noun.
How to Use Adjectives + Prepositions
Follow these four steps to use adjective + preposition combinations correctly:
Step 1: Identify the Adjective
Find the describing word in your sentence. Ask yourself: “Which word tells me about a feeling, quality, or opinion?”
Example: Safiya felt anxious. → The adjective is anxious.
Step 2: Choose the Correct Preposition
Each adjective has one or two prepositions that go with it. Here is a reference table of the most common combinations:
Step 3: Complete the Phrase
After the preposition, add the noun, pronoun, or idea that the adjective refers to.
anxious + about + the journey → “She was anxious about the journey.”
proud + of + his ancestor → “He was proud of his ancestor.”
interested + in + marine life → “She is interested in marine life.”
Step 4: Build a Full Sentence
Make sure your sentence has a subject, a verb, and the adjective + preposition phrase.
Pattern: Subject + verb + adjective + preposition + noun
Tendo was proud of her role at the courthouse.
The children were excited about the school trip.
My brother is afraid of spiders.
Special Cases and Exceptions
Most adjectives pair with just one preposition, but there are some special cases to be aware of.
Adjectives with More Than One Preposition
Some adjectives can use different prepositions depending on the meaning of the sentence:
angry with (a person) → “She was angry with her friend.”
angry about (a situation) → “She was angry about the decision.”
sorry for (feeling sympathy) → “I am sorry for your loss.”
sorry about (apologising) → “I am sorry about the mess.”
grateful for (a thing) → “I am grateful for the food.”
grateful to (a person) → “I am grateful to my teacher.”
Adjectives That Do Not Need a Preposition
When an adjective comes directly before a noun, it does not need a preposition:
“The noble elder spoke wisely.” (noble describes elder directly)
“A misty morning greeted the village.” (misty describes morning directly)
Prepositions are only needed when the adjective comes after the verb in the sentence. Compare these two patterns:
Before the noun (no preposition): “a proud father”
After the verb (preposition needed): “The father was proud of his son.”
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Using the Wrong Preposition
WRONG: “I am afraid from the dark.” → RIGHT: “I am afraid of the dark.”
WRONG: “She is good in maths.” → RIGHT: “She is good at maths.”
WRONG: “He was proud for his work.” → RIGHT: “He was proud of his work.”
Mistake 2: Forgetting the Preposition
WRONG: “She was proud her team.” → RIGHT: “She was proud of her team.”
WRONG: “I am interested science.” → RIGHT: “I am interested in science.”
Mistake 3: Adding an Unnecessary Preposition
WRONG: “The noble of elder spoke.” → RIGHT: “The noble elder spoke.” (No preposition when the adjective is directly before the noun.)
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Correct Preposition
Complete each sentence with the correct preposition.
The students were excited _______ the school play.
My mother is very good _______ baking cakes.
Tendo was proud _______ her job at the courthouse.
I am interested _______ learning about marine species.
The little girl was afraid _______ the loud rumble of thunder.
We should all be responsible _______ keeping our environment clean.
He was anxious _______ his first day at a new school.
She was angry _______ her brother for breaking her pencil.
The teacher was kind _______ every student in the class.
We are grateful _______ the food we eat every day.
Exercise 2: Match the Adjective to Its Preposition
Write the letter of the correct preposition next to each adjective.
worried ___         a) at
keen ___             b) of
jealous ___          c) for
bad ___              d) about
sorry ___            e) on
Exercise 3: Spot and Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence below has an error with the adjective + preposition. Rewrite the sentence correctly.
I am interested about dinosaurs.
She was afraid from the big dog.
We are proud for our school.
He is good in football.
The children were excited for the Eid celebration.
They are keen about swimming.
Exercise 4: Complete the Sentences
Finish each sentence using the adjective + preposition given. Add your own ideas to make the sentence complete.
Exercise 5: Write Your Own Sentences
Write one complete sentence for each adjective + preposition combination below. Try to make your sentences about your own life, your family, or your community.
afraid of
excited about
kind to
responsible for
sorry for
angry with
Answer Key
Exercise 1 Answers
about
at
of
in
of
for
about
with
to
for
Exercise 2 Answers
worried — d) about
keen — e) on
jealous — b) of
bad — a) at
sorry — c) for
Exercise 3 Answers
I am interested in dinosaurs.
She was afraid of the big dog.
We are proud of our school.
He is good at football.
The children were excited about the Eid celebration.
They are keen on swimming.
Exercise 4: Sample Answers
Your answers will be different from these — that is perfectly fine! Here are some examples:
I am proud of my family and our traditions.
My friend is interested in reading books about animals.
Our teacher is good at explaining difficult things simply.
I was anxious about my first day at a new school.
We should be grateful for clean water and good food.
End of Grammar Lesson — Adjectives + Prepositions
tables: She was proud of her team.
He was anxious about the exam.
The child was afraid of the storm.
They were excited about the trip.
I am sorry for the mistake.
She was angry with her brother.
He is responsible for the garden.
She was jealous of her friend.
We are grateful for your help.
Mum is worried about the weather.

💡  Memory Tip
Think of each adjective as having a 'best friend' preposition.
When you learn a new adjective, write it with its preposition in your notebook:
proud + of, anxious + about, interested + in, good + at
Review your list regularly and soon it will feel completely natural!

paragraphs: Unit 1: There’s a Lesson in That
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson

Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit, you will explore stories that teach us important lessons about life. You will read a fable, learn new vocabulary, practise grammar, and write your own short story with a moral. Every story has something to teach us — let’s find out what!
Section 1: What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a fable and identify its moral lesson
Recognise features of fables and folklore, including animal characters and moral teachings
Use adjectives + prepositions correctly in sentences (e.g., proud of, anxious about)
Build your vocabulary with words about stories, community, and the world around you
Write your own short fable with a clear beginning, middle, and ending
Discuss stories and share your opinions with others using clear, respectful language
Tell the difference between literal and figurative language
Identify what makes a character behave in a certain way in a story

Fables and folktales are part of every culture in the world. In Somali culture, traditional stories called sheeko have been passed down for generations. They are told to teach children important values such as honesty, courage, and respect for elders. In this unit, you will see how storytelling connects people everywhere.
Key Features of Fables
As you work through this unit, look out for the features that make fables special. Here is a checklist of things you will find in most fables:
Animal characters — the main characters are usually animals who can talk, think, and feel like humans
A short, simple story — fables are not long novels; they tell their story quickly and clearly
A problem or conflict — something goes wrong that the characters must deal with
A moral at the end — the story finishes with a clear lesson about how to behave or what matters in life
Stereotypes — each animal usually represents a particular human trait, such as a fox being clever or a tortoise being patient

Fables are different from other stories you may have read. Unlike a novel or a fairy tale, a fable keeps things simple so the reader can focus on the lesson it teaches. The characters are not complicated — they usually have one main personality trait, which helps us understand the moral more easily.
Folklore Around the World

Folklore means the traditional stories, beliefs, and customs of a group of people. Every culture has its own folklore. In East Africa, stories are often told around a fire in the evening, passed down from grandparents to grandchildren. These stories carry wisdom that has been collected over hundreds of years.
Here are some examples of folklore traditions from around the world:
Somali sheeko — traditional stories that teach values like generosity, bravery, and wisdom
Aesop's Fables — a famous collection from ancient Greece, including 'The Tortoise and the Hare' and 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf'
Anansi stories — West African tales about a clever spider who uses his wits to solve problems
Panchatantra — ancient Indian fables about animals who teach lessons about friendship, trust, and leadership
No matter where they come from, all these traditions share something in common: they use stories to teach important lessons about life. That is why this unit is called "There's a Lesson in That"!
Section 2: Key Vocabulary

Before we begin reading, let’s learn some important words you will meet in this unit. Study each word, its meaning, and the example sentence. Try to use these words in your own sentences as you work through the lesson.

Keep a vocabulary notebook as you work through this unit. Each time you meet a new word, write it down with its meaning and an example sentence of your own. This is one of the best ways to remember new vocabulary.
Section 3: Reading — The Camel and the Sparrow
Before You Read
Before you start reading the fable, think about these questions. You do not need to write your answers — just think about them quietly for a minute or two:
Have you ever been told you were too young or too small to do something? How did it make you feel?
What does the word contribution mean to you? Can a small action really make a big difference?
What animals live in dry, desert areas of East Africa? What qualities do those animals have?
Now read the fable below carefully. As you read, think about what lesson the story is trying to teach. Pay attention to how the characters behave and what happens because of their choices.
A Fable from the Dry Lands

In the dry lands near the edge of a great desert, there lived a tall, proud camel named Jamal and a tiny sparrow named Safiya. They shared the same region, near a small village surrounded by acacia trees and golden sand. The villagers were kind people who looked after one another, just as their ancestors had done for many generations.

One morning, a terrible sandstorm swept through the village. It roared and rumbled for hours, filling the air with dust and darkness. When the misty dawn arrived the next day, the people stepped outside and found their homes in rubble. Walls had crumbled. Roofs had blown away. The well was buried under sand. The village elder, a wise woman named Hawa, called everyone together.
“We must rebuild,” she said firmly. “Every creature and every person has a role to play.”

Jamal the camel stepped forward proudly. “I am the strongest,” he declared. “I will carry the heavy stones. I will drag the timber. No one works harder than me.” He began hauling large rocks on his broad back, and the villagers cheered. Jamal was proud of his strength and happy to show it off.
Little Safiya flew down and landed on a branch near Jamal. “I want to help too,” she chirped. “What can I do?”

Jamal laughed loudly. “You? You are far too small! What could a tiny bird possibly do? Go and sit on your branch and sing your little songs. Leave the real work to those of us who are big and strong.”

Safiya felt a wave of anxiety. Perhaps the camel was right. Perhaps she was too small to matter. She flew to a quiet branch and sat there, feeling sad. But then she remembered something her grandmother had once told her: “Even the smallest raindrop fills the river.”

So Safiya decided not to give up. She flew high above the village, higher than any camel could ever reach. From the sky, she could see things that no one on the ground could spot. She noticed a thin line of green far to the east — a hidden stream, tucked behind a ridge of rocks. Water! The village needed water desperately for survival, and there it was, waiting to be found.
She swooped down to tell Hawa. “There is water!” she cried. “Beyond the eastern ridge! I can see it from above!”

Hawa sent workers to the ridge, and they found the stream just as Safiya had described. Fresh, clean water flowed into the village. Without it, the people would have gone thirsty for days. The encounter with the hidden stream felt like a miracle to everyone.

That evening, Hawa gathered the whole village under the stars. “Jamal carried our stones,” she said, “and we are grateful for his great strength. But Safiya found our water. She saved us from thirst. Strength is noble, but so is the wisdom to see what others cannot.”
Jamal lowered his great head. “I was wrong,” he said softly to Safiya. “You were not too small. You were exactly the right size — for exactly the right job.”
Safiya smiled. “Every creature has a role,” she said. “We just need to find it.”
Moral: No contribution is too small. Everyone has something important to offer.
Section 4: Understanding the Text
Now that you have read the fable, answer the following questions. Write your answers in full sentences. Look back at the story if you need to check any details.
Part A: Comprehension Questions
Where does the story take place? Describe the setting in your own words.
What happened to the village at the beginning of the story?
What did the village elder, Hawa, tell everyone to do after the storm?
What did Jamal say when Safiya offered to help? How did this make Safiya feel?
How did Safiya discover the hidden stream?
What did Hawa say about Safiya’s contribution at the end of the story?
What is the moral of this fable? Write it in your own words.
Part B: Thinking Deeper
These questions ask you to think beyond what is directly written in the story. Use clues from the text and your own ideas to answer them.
Why do you think the author chose a camel and a sparrow as the two main characters? What qualities does each animal represent?
Jamal told Safiya she was “too small” to help. Have you ever felt that someone thought you were too young or too small to do something important? What did you do about it?
Safiya’s grandmother once told her: “Even the smallest raindrop fills the river.” What does this figure of speech mean? Is it literal or figurative? Explain your answer.
If you could add a third animal character to this fable, which animal would you choose and what role would it play in the story?
How does this fable connect to real life? Can you think of a time when someone made a small but important contribution to your family, school, or community?
Part C: Vocabulary in the Fable

Several vocabulary words from this unit appear in the fable. Find each word listed below in the story and write the sentence where it appears. Then explain what the word means in that sentence, using your own words.
region — Find it in the first paragraph. What does it mean here?
ancestor — Where does this word appear? Why is it important to the villagers?
rumbled — What rumbled in the story? What picture does this word create in your mind?
rubble — What was in rubble? How does this word help you understand how bad the storm was?
survival — Why was survival important in the story? What did the villagers need to survive?
encounter — What encounter happened in the story? Was it a good or bad encounter?
Section 5: Grammar Focus — Adjectives + Prepositions

In English, many adjectives need a special word called a preposition to complete their meaning. When we combine an adjective with a preposition, we can express feelings, opinions, and descriptions more clearly. Let’s learn how this works!
What Are Adjectives and Prepositions?
An adjective is a word that describes a noun or pronoun. For example: proud, anxious, interested, afraid, excited, good, sorry, angry, kind, grateful.
A preposition is a small connecting word that links the adjective to the noun or idea it refers to. Common prepositions include: of, about, in, at, for, with, to.
When we put them together, we get an adjective + preposition combination. Here are some of the most useful ones:
proud of — She was proud of her work.
anxious about — He felt anxious about the test.
interested in — They are interested in learning new things.
good at — Amina is good at drawing.
afraid of — The little boy was afraid of the dark.
excited about — We are excited about the school trip.
sorry for — She felt sorry for the lost kitten.
angry with — My brother was angry with me.
responsible for — Everyone is responsible for keeping the classroom clean.
kind to — Always be kind to others.
How to Use Them (Step by Step)
Find the adjective. Look for the describing word in your sentence. Example: The adjective in “She was anxious” is anxious.
Choose the right preposition. Each adjective has a preposition that goes with it. For anxious, the preposition is about.
Add what comes after. Complete the phrase with the noun or idea that follows. Example: anxious about the exam.
Build a full sentence. Put it all together with a subject and verb. Example: Fatima was anxious about the exam.
Reference: Common Adjective + Preposition Pairs
Here is a helpful table of common combinations. Try to memorise these:
Examples from the Fable
Look at how adjective + preposition combinations appear in our fable, “The Camel and the Sparrow”:
Jamal was proud of his strength. (proud + of)
Safiya felt anxious about being too small. (anxious + about)
The villagers were grateful for the water Safiya found. (grateful + for)
Hawa said that strength is noble, but wisdom is also important. (Here, noble is used without a preposition because it describes strength directly, before the noun.)
Watch Out for Common Mistakes!
Many learners use the wrong preposition with an adjective. Here are some mistakes to avoid:
WRONG: She was proud for her work. → RIGHT: She was proud of her work.
WRONG: I am interested about science. → RIGHT: I am interested in science.
WRONG: He was afraid from the dark. → RIGHT: He was afraid of the dark.
WRONG: We are excited for the trip. → RIGHT: We are excited about the trip.
WRONG: She is good in maths. → RIGHT: She is good at maths.
Section 6: Grammar Practice
Now it’s time to practise! Complete the exercises below. Try to do them without looking back at the rules first. If you get stuck, you can check Section 5 for help.
Exercise 1: Fill in the Correct Preposition
Choose the correct preposition to complete each sentence.
She was excited _______ the school concert. (about / of / in)
The children were afraid _______ the thunder. (about / of / at)
My father is very good _______ cooking. (in / of / at)
I am interested _______ reading fables. (in / about / at)
We are all responsible _______ keeping our school clean. (of / for / about)
Jamal was proud _______ carrying the heaviest stones. (of / for / about)
Safiya felt anxious _______ being too small to help. (for / with / about)
The elder was kind _______ everyone in the village. (with / for / to)
Exercise 2: Match the Adjective to Its Preposition
Write the letter of the correct preposition next to each adjective. Then write a sentence of your own using each pair.
grateful …………  a) in
jealous …………… b) to
interested ………  c) for
kind ………………… d) of
sorry ……………… e) about
worried …………… f) for
Exercise 3: Spot the Mistake
Each sentence below has the wrong preposition. Find the mistake and rewrite the sentence correctly.
Ahmed is afraid from spiders.
My sister is good in singing.
We are excited for the holidays.
The teacher was angry to the noisy students.
I am proud for my best friend.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Sentences
Write a sentence using each adjective + preposition combination below. Make your sentences about things that are real or important to you.
Answers for Section 6
Exercise 1 Answers:
about
of
at
in
for
of
about
to
Exercise 2 Answers:
grateful — c) for
jealous — d) of
interested — a) in
kind — b) to
sorry — f) for
worried — e) about
Exercise 3 Answers:
Ahmed is afraid of spiders.
My sister is good at singing.
We are excited about the holidays.
The teacher was angry with the noisy students.
I am proud of my best friend.
Section 7: Writing Workshop — Write Your Own Fable
Now it’s your turn to be the author! In this section, you will plan and write your own short fable. Your fable should be about 150–200 words long and must include a clear moral at the end.
What Makes a Good Fable?
Before you start writing, remember the key features of a fable:
Animal characters who talk and act like humans (this is called anthropomorphism)
A short, simple plot with a clear beginning, middle, and ending
A problem or conflict that the characters must face and try to solve
A moral (lesson) at the end that teaches something about how to behave
Dialogue between characters to make the story come alive
A setting that your reader can picture in their mind
Step 1: Plan Your Fable
Use the planning frame below to organise your ideas before you start writing. You do not need to write in full sentences here — just notes and key ideas are fine.
Title: What will your fable be called?
Setting: Where and when does your story take place? Think of places you know well — a village, a river, a market, a farm, a forest, a coastline.
Characters: Choose two or three animal characters. Give each one a name and a personality. For example: a lazy hyena, a clever tortoise, a brave eagle, a generous goat.
Problem: What goes wrong? What challenge do the characters face? For example: a drought, a lost treasure, a broken bridge, a quarrel between neighbours.
Events: What happens in the middle of the story? How do the characters try to solve the problem? Does anything go wrong along the way?
Ending: How is the problem solved? What does each character learn from the experience?
Moral: What lesson does your story teach? Write it in one clear sentence. For example: “Honesty is always the best path.”
Step 2: Write Your First Draft
Now write your fable using the plan you made above. Here are some tips to help you write a strong story:

Start with the setting. Describe where and when the story happens. Use adjectives to paint a picture for your reader. Example: “In a quiet forest beside a shining river, there lived a clever tortoise named Abdi.”

Introduce the characters. Tell us who they are and what they are like. This is a great place to use adjective + preposition phrases! Example: “Abdi was proud of his shell, but he was also kind to every creature he met.”
Create a problem. Something must go wrong to make the story interesting. Example: “One day, a fierce storm washed away the bridge, and no one could cross the river.”
Show how the characters respond. Use dialogue with speech marks to let your characters talk to each other. Example: “‘We must find another way across,’ said Abdi thoughtfully.”
End with the moral. After the problem is solved, state the lesson clearly. Example: “Moral: Patience and teamwork can solve even the biggest problems.”
Step 3: Check and Improve Your Writing
After writing your first draft, read it again carefully. Use this checklist to make sure your fable is as good as it can be:
Does my fable have animal characters who talk and act like humans?
Does it have a clear beginning, middle, and ending?
Have I used speech marks correctly for dialogue?
Have I used at least two adjective + preposition phrases?
Have I included a moral at the end?
Have I checked my spelling and punctuation carefully?
Is my fable between 150 and 200 words long?
Have I used interesting vocabulary from this unit?
Section 8: Listening and Speaking
Good communication is not just about reading and writing. It is also about listening carefully and speaking clearly. In this section, you will practise both of these important skills.
Activity 1: Discussion Questions

Discuss these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor. Listen carefully to their answers and share your own ideas. There are no wrong answers — the important thing is to explain your thinking clearly.
What is your favourite story that teaches a lesson? Why do you like it?
Do you think fables are a good way to teach children about right and wrong? Why or why not?
In the fable “The Camel and the Sparrow,” Jamal told Safiya she was too small to help. Why do you think some people look down on others? How should we respond when this happens to us?

Can you think of a proverb or saying from your own culture that teaches a lesson? Share it and explain what it means. For example, the Somali proverb “Aloow aqoon la’aan waa iftiin la’aan” reminds us that knowledge is like light.
If animals could really talk, which animal do you think would give the wisest advice? Why did you choose that animal?
Activity 2: Role Play

Choose one of the role-play activities below and act it out with a partner or family member. Try to use clear, expressive voices and good vocabulary. Use adjective + preposition phrases in your dialogue where you can.

Role Play 1: The Apology. Act out the scene from “The Camel and the Sparrow” where Jamal tells Safiya she is too small to help. Then act out the ending where Jamal apologises. Use phrases like “I am sorry for what I said” and “I was wrong about you.”

Role Play 2: The News Report. Imagine you are a news reporter. Interview Hawa, the village elder, after the village is rebuilt. Ask her about what happened and how the community worked together. Use questions like “What were you most anxious about?” and “What are you most proud of?”
Role Play 3: Your Own Scene. Create your own scene with two animal characters from any fable. Act out a conversation between them that includes at least one moral lesson.
Activity 3: Retell a Story

Choose a fable, folktale, or traditional story that you know well. It could be a Somali sheeko, an East African folktale, or any story with a moral lesson. Practise retelling it aloud by following these steps:
Think about the main events of the story. What happens first, next, and last?
Practise telling the story in your own words. Do not try to memorise it word for word — just tell it naturally, as if you are sharing it with a friend.
Use expression in your voice. Change your tone for different characters. Speak louder for exciting parts and softer for quiet, thoughtful moments.
End by clearly stating the moral of the story. Explain what the story teaches us.
If possible, tell your story to a family member or friend and ask them what they thought of it.
Section 9: Check Your Learning

Well done for completing Unit 1! Before you move on, use this checklist to see how much you have learned. Be honest with yourself — if there is something you are not sure about, go back and review that section of the lesson.
Read each statement and tick the box if you can do it confidently:
I can explain what a fable is and describe its key features.
I can read a fable and identify the moral of the story.
I understand what anthropomorphism means and can give an example.
I know at least ten new vocabulary words from this unit and can use them in sentences.
I can use adjective + preposition combinations correctly (e.g., proud of, anxious about, interested in).
I can spot the wrong preposition in a sentence and correct it.
I can tell the difference between literal and figurative language.
I have written my own short fable with animal characters and a clear moral.
I have used speech marks correctly when writing dialogue.
I can retell a story aloud using clear, expressive language.
I have discussed the themes of the unit with someone and shared my own opinions respectfully.

If you ticked most of the boxes, you are ready to move on to the next unit. If you need more practice with any area, go back to that section of the lesson and try the activities again. You can also ask your AI tutor for extra practice exercises on any topic you found difficult.

Remember, learning takes time and effort, and every bit of practice makes you stronger. The most important thing is to keep trying, keep reading, and keep asking questions. Just like Safiya the sparrow in our fable, you might be surprised by how much you can achieve when you believe in yourself and never give up.
End of Unit 1 — Self-Paced Lesson
tables:
💡  What is a Fable?
A fable is a short story that teaches a lesson, called a moral.
Fables often have animals as main characters who talk and act like humans.
This is called anthropomorphism — giving human qualities to animals.
Famous fables include those by Aesop, such as 'The Ant and the Grasshopper'.
Having good qualities like honesty and kindness
The noble elder always helped his neighbours.
The part someone plays in a story or in real life
Everyone has a role to play in the community.
To meet or come across someone by chance
She had a surprising encounter with a fox near the river.
A feeling of worry or nervousness
He felt anxiety before speaking in front of the class.
Continuing to live, especially in hard conditions
Water is essential for the survival of all living things.
A family member who lived a long time ago
My ancestor built the first house in our village.
Broken pieces of stone or brick from a building
After the storm, there was rubble on the streets.
When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
The morning was misty and cool.
Their residence is a small house near the market.
To make a deep, loud sound like thunder
We could hear the rumble of thunder in the distance.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are unsure about any word, try asking your AI tutor:
"Can you explain the word 'noble' in a simple way?"
"Can you use the word 'encounter' in three different sentences?"
"What is the opposite of 'rural'?"

⭐  Did You Know?
Fables have been told in East Africa for thousands of years.
In Somali culture, stories called sheeko are told to teach children important values.
Many fables use animals because their qualities remind us of human behaviour.
The camel is respected across the Horn of Africa as a symbol of endurance.

📖  Reading Strategies
Good readers use these strategies when reading a new text:
- Skim the text first to get the main idea before reading in detail
- Use context clues to work out unfamiliar words from the sentence around them
- Re-read important or tricky parts slowly to make sure you understand
- Ask yourself questions as you read: Who? What? Why? How?
- Picture the setting and characters in your mind as you read

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Stuck on a question? Try asking:
"Can you help me understand what 'figurative language' means?"
"What clues in the story tell us how Safiya is feeling?"
Remember: your AI tutor can guide you, but the answers should be in your own words!

💡  Did You Know?
When you find an unfamiliar word in a text, you can often work out its
meaning from the context — the words and sentences around it.
This is called using 'context clues' and it is a very useful reading strategy.
Try it next time you meet a word you do not know!
She was proud of her brother.
He was anxious about the journey.
The cat is afraid of loud noises.
I am sorry for being late.
We are responsible for our actions.
He was jealous of his friend.
We are grateful for this food.

💡  Memory Tip
Think of adjectives and prepositions as best friends.
Each adjective has a favourite preposition that it likes to go with.
When you learn a new adjective, always learn its preposition partner too!
Try making a flashcard: adjective on one side, preposition + example on the other.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want to check your Exercise 4 sentences? Ask your AI tutor:
"Are my adjective + preposition sentences correct?"
"Can you give me more practice with adjectives and prepositions?"

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
After writing your fable, you can ask your AI tutor to help you improve it:
"Can you check my fable for grammar mistakes?"
"Does my story have a clear moral?"
"How can I make my fable more interesting?"
"Have I used adjective + preposition phrases correctly?"

🌟  Writing Challenge (Optional)
Can you write a second version of your fable from a different point of view?
For example, if your first fable was told by the narrator (third person),
try rewriting it as if one of the animal characters is telling the story (first person).
This is a great way to practise narrative voice!

🎤  Speaking Tip
When speaking in front of others, remember to:
- Stand or sit up straight and look at your audience
- Speak slowly and clearly so everyone can hear you
- Use interesting vocabulary to keep your listeners engaged
- Pause at important moments for effect
- Smile! It helps you relax and connects you with your audience.

🎉  Congratulations!
You have completed Unit 1: There's a Lesson in That.
Remember: every story has a lesson, and every person has a role to play.
Keep reading, keep writing, and keep learning. You are doing brilliantly!

paragraphs: The Silence After the Rumble
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 1 Story

The courthouse was the only building still standing in the middle of the broken town. Around it, there was nothing but rubble. Walls had fallen. Roads were cracked. Yet one room — the courtroom — still held its shape.

Tendo lived in a small residence nearby with her grandmother. They had survived a civil war that left many people without homes. Others had moved to different regions of the country. But Tendo and her grandmother had stayed. “This is where your ancestor planted trees and built homes,” Grandma said. “It is our place.”
Tendo walked to the courthouse every morning. Her role was simple — sweep the steps and make sure there was water for the judge’s bench. It was not a big job, but it made her proud.
She moved slowly and carefully, always checking the door hinges, still bent from the last explosion. Some said the building would fall soon. But to her, it was sacred.
One misty morning, she arrived early. The courtroom was silent. Then, a deep rumble shook the floor. Tendo froze. “Is it another bomb?” she whispered.
But it was not. A delivery truck had passed too close to the damaged road.
She exhaled slowly and climbed the steps.
That day, a native elder named Mzee Wekesa came to speak to students about justice.
“When I was a boy,” he said, “we followed continental rules — brought by outsiders. But now we shape our own laws.”
One boy asked, “What if laws are broken? What happens?”
Mzee Wekesa replied, “The law must help people — not hurt them. A nation is measured by how it treats even the smallest citizen.”
Tendo listened. She felt a strange anxiety in her chest — the kind that grows when you are about to speak.
She raised her hand.
“Yes, child?” the elder asked.
“Do I have a role?” she asked softly. “I just sweep.”
The elder looked at her with kind eyes. “Of course. You help the court open. Without you, justice begins late. Never doubt your part.”
Later that week, a trial began. A businessman was accused of stealing food during the war — food meant for children.

The courtroom was full. Tendo stood near the back, beside a woman in a silvery shawl who had come from an urban centre far away. She whispered, “My child got sick during the war. There was no nutrition. If that man took food, it cost lives.”
The judge entered. The people sat down. The accused shuffled to the front. He wore clean clothes and held a folded paper in his pouch.
As the trial began, witnesses spoke. Some voices shook. Others spoke clearly and slowly, trying to remember.
Then came a surprise.
A boy, just eleven, came to speak. “I saw him,” the boy said. “He hid boxes behind the artificial wall in the storehouse.”
“What’s the difference,” someone whispered, “between this trial and ones from before the war?”
Tendo knew the answer: survival. This time, people spoke. This time, the court had power.
After the trial, the accused was found guilty. The judge said, “The court will ensure the food is returned. People in rural towns need it most.”
Outside, the sun had dipped low. A pot of tea boiled over a coal stove in the courtyard. Women from different regions shared small cups, speaking of the verdict.
One woman turned to Tendo. “You came to every trial. Why?”
“I want to know how nations work,” she said. “How laws are made. What justice means.”
The woman smiled. “You might make a noble lawyer someday.”
Tendo laughed. “Maybe. But I like being early. I like unlocking the doors. It feels like opening a promise.”

That evening, she walked home past burnt fences and empty plots where shops once stood. Her shoes crunched the gravel. From far off, she heard a soft rumble — thunder this time. The sky was grey and misty.
At home, her grandmother stirred soup over a fire. “The road may be cracked,” she said, “but we walk it anyway.”
“Yes,” Tendo said. “I walked it again today.”
They ate slowly. She saw her reflection in the metal spoon — dark eyes, tired but full of something new. Confidence maybe.
Later, she helped hang clean clothes behind their home. They flapped like flags in the cool air.
Suddenly, she encountered a boy her age. He had come to see her grandmother about a land document. “Do you work at the court?” he asked.
“Not really,” she said. Then she paused. “Actually, I do. I unlock the doors.”
The boy looked at her like she was important.
And maybe, she finally believed it too.
Think About the Story

After reading “The Silence After the Rumble,” take some time to think about the questions below. You can write your answers in your notebook, discuss them with someone you trust, or share your thoughts with your AI tutor.
Tendo’s job was to sweep the courthouse steps and fill the water jug. Why do you think this small job was so important to her? What does it tell us about her character?
Mzee Wekesa said, “A nation is measured by how it treats even the smallest citizen.” What do you think this means? Do you agree with this idea? Why or why not?
The story is set in a town that is recovering from war. How does the setting affect the way the characters think, feel, and behave? Find at least two examples from the text to support your answer.
At the end of the story, Tendo says, “Actually, I do. I unlock the doors.” Why is this moment important? What has changed about how Tendo sees herself?

The moral of this story is: “Every citizen, no matter how small, plays a role in rebuilding justice — even when the world feels broken.” How does this moral connect to your own life? Can you think of a small thing you do that makes a difference in your family or community?
End of Story — The Silence After the Rumble
tables:
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you want to explore the story further, try asking:
"What are the main themes in this story?"
"Can you help me find examples of figurative language in the text?"
"What other stories deal with similar themes of justice and community?"

tables: A black rock burned for heat and energy
We use coal to help make electricity for our homes.
A small bag or pocket that can hold things
He kept his coins in a small leather pouch.
To meet or come across someone by chance
She had an unexpected encounter with an old friend at the market.
Areas or parts of a country with something in common
There are many different regions in our country, each with its own culture.
When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
The morning was misty, and we could barely see the path ahead.
A feeling of worry or nervousness
She felt a lot of anxiety before the big test.
Related to the sea or ocean
Marine animals like dolphins and whales live in the ocean.
A person who was born in a particular place
She is a native of the town and knows every street.
A group of living things that are similar
There are many species of birds in the forest.
Having a shiny, silver colour like metal
The silvery fish darted through the clear water.
Continuing to live, especially in hard conditions
Finding water was essential for their survival in the desert.
The artificial flowers looked so real that everyone was fooled.
Food and drink that help our bodies grow and stay healthy
Eating fruits and vegetables is important for good nutrition.
Related to the countryside, not the city
The rural village had many farms and open fields.
Related to a large land area, like a continent
The continental weather was very different from the coastal weather.
Related to a city or town
The urban area was busy with traffic and tall buildings.
A family member who lived a long time ago
My ancestor came to this land many generations ago.
Their residence is a small house with a big garden.
To have something inside or hold something
The box can contain many books and toys.
Something very special that is hard to explain
It was a miracle that the lost cat found its way home.
Broken pieces of stone or brick from a fallen building
After the earthquake, there was rubble everywhere.
Having good qualities like honesty and kindness
The noble leader always put her people first.
The part someone plays in a story or in real life
In the school play, I have the role of the wise owl.
To make a deep, loud sound like thunder
We could hear the rumble of the truck passing by.
To move slowly dragging your feet, or to mix things up
He began to shuffle the cards before the game.

paragraphs: Indefinite Pronouns
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 2 Grammar

This grammar guide will help you understand and use indefinite pronouns with confidence. Work through each section carefully, practise the activities, and check your answers using the answer key at the end.
What Are Indefinite Pronouns?

Indefinite pronouns are special words that we use when we want to talk about people or things in a general way, without being specific. They are called 'indefinite' because they do not point to a definite or particular person or thing.
For example, instead of saying:
"Ayele and Kiya and Nuru all cooperated during the experiment."
We can say:
"Everyone cooperated during the experiment."
The word everyone replaces the specific names and refers to all the people in the group. This makes our writing shorter, smoother, and more flexible.
Indefinite pronouns are very important because they help us:
Talk about people or things when we do not know exactly who or what they are
Make general statements about groups
Ask questions about unknown people or things
Make negative statements clearly and naturally
Types of Indefinite Pronouns
Pronouns for People
These indefinite pronouns are used when we are talking about people:
Pronouns for Things
These indefinite pronouns are used when we are talking about things:
Pronouns for Quantity
These indefinite pronouns tell us about how many or how much:
Important Rules to Remember
Rule 1: Singular Verb Agreement
Most indefinite pronouns that refer to people or things (everyone, somebody, nothing, etc.) are treated as singular. This means they need a singular verb (is, has, was, does, knows, etc.).
Correct examples:
Everyone is ready for the presentation.
Nobody knows where the book went.
Something was glowing in the field.
Everything has been checked twice.
Common mistakes:
Everyone are happy. --> Everyone is happy.
Nobody know the answer. --> Nobody knows the answer.
Something were strange. --> Something was strange.
Rule 2: Someone vs. Anyone
Use someone / somebody / something in positive sentences.
Use anyone / anybody / anything in questions and negative sentences.
Examples:
Someone called you this morning. (positive)
Did anyone call me? (question)
I did not hear anything. (negative)
Something is wrong with the computer. (positive)
Is anything wrong? (question)
Rule 3: No One and Nothing Are Already Negative
The pronouns no one, nobody, and nothing already carry a negative meaning. Do not add another negative word (like 'not') to the same sentence, or you will create a double negative.
Correct:
Nobody was late for class.
There is nothing in the box.
Incorrect (double negative):
Nobody was not late for class.
There is not nothing in the box.
Rule 4: Using 'Their' with Singular Indefinite Pronouns
In modern English, it is common and accepted to use their to refer back to a singular indefinite pronoun like everyone, someone, or nobody. This avoids awkward phrases like 'his or her'.
Everyone should bring their own lunch.
Somebody left their backpack in the classroom.
Nobody wanted to miss their chance to present.
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun to complete each sentence.
__________ wants to go outside for break. (Everyone / Anyone)
Does __________ know where the library book is? (someone / anyone)
__________ was missing from the science kit. (Something / Anything)
__________ in the class passed the test -- it was too difficult. (No one / Anyone)
We looked everywhere, but we could not find __________. (something / anything)
__________ of the students finished early. (Few / Nothing)
__________ is more important than working together. (Nothing / Anything)
Has __________ seen my notebook? (somebody / anybody)
__________ knew the answer except the teacher. (Nobody / Anybody)
__________ must bring their permission slip by Friday. (Everyone / Anyone)
Exercise 2: Correct the Sentences
Each sentence has a mistake. Rewrite it correctly.
Everyone are invited to the school fair.
Nobody don't know the answer.
Does someone have a spare pen?
Everything have been packed for the trip.
I can't find nothing in my bag.
Somebody have eaten my lunch.
Exercise 3: Someone, Anyone, or No One?
Fill in each blank with someone, anyone, or no one.
__________ should check the equipment before we start.
Is __________ coming to the meeting today?
__________ remembered to bring the map.
I don't think __________ noticed the mistake.
__________ volunteered to clean the classroom.
__________ in the group has ever visited a space centre.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own
Write six sentences of your own, each using a different indefinite pronoun from the list below. Make sure your sentences are about school, science, or your community.
Pronouns to use: everyone, something, nobody, anything, many, few
Exercise 5: Story Hunt
Go back to the story The Burrow Discovery and find five examples of indefinite pronouns. For each one:
Write the sentence from the story.
Underline the indefinite pronoun.
Explain whether the pronoun refers to a person, a thing, or a quantity.
Answer Key

Exercise 2 Answers

Answers will vary. Check that each sentence uses the correct indefinite pronoun and that the verb agrees in number (singular verb for everyone, something, nobody; plural context for many, few).
Exercise 5 Answers
Answers will vary. Examples from the story include:
"...everyone in Year 5 had to present..." (everyone = people, singular)
"Unless someone objects..." (someone = people, singular)
"No one will..." (no one = people, singular)
"Not everyone agrees yet..." (everyone = people, singular)
"...not everything in science goes fast." (everything = things, singular)
tables: Everyone is welcome at the fair.

💡  Memory Trick
Think of everyone, someone, and nobody as ONE person standing in for a group.
One person = singular verb. This trick works every time!

💬  Well Done!
