Keep practising these pronouns in your own writing. The more you use them, the more natural they will feel!

paragraphs: Unit 2: Exploring Space
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit, you will explore the exciting world of space and discovery. You will read about how people study the stars, build amazing projects, and work together to achieve great things. Along the way, you will learn new vocabulary, practise important grammar skills, and develop your writing and speaking abilities.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use and understand 25 new vocabulary words related to systems, science, and social values
Identify and use indefinite pronouns such as everyone, somebody, nothing, and anything
Read and understand an informative passage about space exploration
Write a blog post using informal language and personal reflections
Distinguish between facts and opinions in a text
Use adverbs and adverbial phrases to add detail to your sentences
Practise speaking and listening skills through role-play and discussion
Understand the difference between biographies and autobiographies
Key Vocabulary

Before you begin reading, take a moment to look at the important words you will meet in this unit. Knowing these words will help you understand the texts and complete the activities. Read through the table below, say each word aloud, and try to use it in a short sentence of your own.
Reading: The Night Sky Above Us
Read the passage below carefully. Think about the main ideas and any new words as you read.

For thousands of years, people have looked up at the night sky and wondered what lies beyond the clouds. In ancient times, astronomers in the Muslim world made brilliant discoveries. Scholars in Baghdad, Cairo, and Timbuktu built observatories, mapped the stars, and invented instruments to observe the heavens more clearly. Their work became the source of much of the knowledge we use in modern astronomy today.

One of the most important early astronomers was Al-Sufi, who lived in Persia over a thousand years ago. He wrote a famous book called the Book of Fixed Stars, which described the positions and brightness of hundreds of stars. His observations were so accurate that everyone who studied the sky after him used his work as a guide. Nobody in Europe had created anything like it at the time.

Centuries later, the dream of exploring space became real. In 1957, the first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched into orbit around the Earth. This was a booming moment in history, and it changed everything. Scientists began to cooperate across borders, sharing ideas and working together to reach the Moon. In 1969, astronauts landed on the Moon for the first time. Someone once said that this was the greatest achievement of the twentieth century.

Today, the International Space Station (ISS) orbits our planet. Astronauts from many countries live and work there. They conduct experiments, grow food, and study how the human body reacts to living in space. The towering rockets that carry them into orbit are marvels of engineering. Everything on the station must be carefully planned because there is no shop in space! If anything breaks, the crew must fix it themselves.

Space exploration is not just about science. It teaches us the value of cooperation, patience, and hard work. It shows us that when people avoid conflict and work towards a shared goal, they can achieve brilliant results. Whether you dream of becoming an astronomer, an engineer, or a teacher, the stars above remind us that there is always something new to discover.
As the famous saying goes, the sky is not the limit; it is only the beginning.
Understanding the Text
Now that you have read the passage, answer the following questions. Write your answers in full sentences.
Comprehension Questions
What did ancient Muslim astronomers build to study the sky?
Who was Al-Sufi, and why was his book important?
What happened in 1957 that changed the history of space exploration?
What do astronauts do on the International Space Station?
Why does the passage say that space exploration is not just about science?
Think Deeper
The passage says that scientists began to cooperate across borders. Why do you think cooperation is important in science?
The author writes that the stars remind us there is always something new to discover. Do you agree? Give a reason for your answer.
Find one fact and one opinion in the passage. Write them down and explain how you can tell the difference.
Vocabulary in Context
Go back to the reading passage and find the words listed below. For each word, explain what it means based on the way it is used in the passage. Write your answer in a full sentence.
brilliant (paragraph 1): What kind of discoveries did the Muslim astronomers make?
source (paragraph 1): What does it mean to say their work was the source of modern knowledge?
cooperate (paragraph 3): How did scientists cooperate to reach the Moon?
towering (paragraph 4): What does this word tell you about the size of the rockets?
avoid (paragraph 5): Why is it important to avoid conflict when working together?
Understanding Facts and Opinions
When you read a text, it is important to be able to tell the difference between facts and opinions. This is a key skill that will help you become a better reader and thinker.
What Is a Fact?

A fact is a statement that can be proven to be true. You can check a fact by looking it up in a reliable source, such as an encyclopaedia, a textbook, or a trusted website. Facts do not change depending on who says them.
Examples of facts:
Sputnik 1 was launched in 1957.
The International Space Station orbits the Earth.
Al-Sufi wrote the Book of Fixed Stars.
The Moon is about 384,400 kilometres from the Earth.
What Is an Opinion?

An opinion is a statement that expresses what someone thinks, feels, or believes. Opinions cannot be proven true or false because they are personal views. Different people can have different opinions about the same topic.
Examples of opinions:
Space travel is the most exciting thing in the world.
Everyone should learn about astronomy.
Stars are the most beautiful things in the sky.
Exploring Mars would be the ideal next step for humanity.
How to Tell Them Apart
Here are some helpful clues:
Facts often include dates, numbers, names, and events that can be verified.
Opinions often include words like 'best', 'worst', 'most', 'should', 'I think', or 'I believe'.
Ask yourself: Can this be checked and proven? If yes, it is likely a fact. If it depends on personal feelings, it is an opinion.
Grammar Focus: Indefinite Pronouns

In the reading passage, you saw words like everyone, nobody, something, and anything. These are called indefinite pronouns. They are very useful because they let us talk about people or things in a general way, without naming anyone or anything specific.
What Are Indefinite Pronouns?

An indefinite pronoun is a word that replaces a noun when we do not need to be specific about who or what we are talking about. Instead of saying, 'Ayele and Kiya and Nuru all worked hard,' we can simply say, 'Everyone worked hard.' This makes our sentences shorter and more natural.
Here are the most common indefinite pronouns, grouped by what they refer to:
Indefinite Pronouns for People
everyone / everybody -- all people in a group
someone / somebody -- an unknown or unspecified person
anyone / anybody -- any person at all (often used in questions and negatives)
no one / nobody -- not a single person
Indefinite Pronouns for Things
everything -- all things
something -- an unknown or unspecified thing
anything -- any thing at all (often used in questions and negatives)
nothing -- not a single thing
Indefinite Pronouns for Quantity
many -- a large number (countable)
few -- a small number (countable)
several -- more than two but not very many
some -- an unspecified amount or number
none -- not any
all -- the total amount or number
Important Rules
There are two important rules to remember when using indefinite pronouns:
Rule 1: Most indefinite pronouns for people and things are singular.
This means they use singular verbs. Look at these examples:
Everyone is excited about the science fair. (NOT: Everyone are excited.)
Nobody knows the answer yet. (NOT: Nobody know the answer.)
Something was glowing in the dark. (NOT: Something were glowing.)
Everything has been packed for the trip. (NOT: Everything have been packed.)
Rule 2: Use 'somebody' and 'something' in positive sentences. Use 'anybody' and 'anything' in questions and negative sentences.
Somebody left a book on the desk. (positive)
Did anybody see my pencil? (question)
I cannot find anything in this room. (negative)
There is nothing left in the box. (negative with nothing)
Indefinite Pronouns in Action
Let us look at how indefinite pronouns appear in real sentences. Notice how each one replaces a specific noun:
Everyone cooperated during the experiment. (Everyone = all the students)
Nobody wanted to miss the science fair. (Nobody = not one student)
Something strange happened during the test. (Something = an unknown event)
Is there anything I can help with? (Anything = any task at all)
Many enjoyed the presentation about space. (Many = a large number of people)
Nothing is more important than safety in the lab. (Nothing = no thing at all)
Few had seen a real telescope before. (Few = a small number of students)
Somebody left their backpack in the classroom. (Somebody = an unknown person)

Did you notice something interesting in sentence 8? We wrote 'Somebody left their backpack.' Even though 'somebody' is singular, we often use 'their' as a pronoun to refer back to it. This is common and accepted in modern English.
Grammar Practice
Now it is time to practise what you have learned about indefinite pronouns. Try each activity carefully and check your answers at the end.
Activity 1: Choose the Correct Pronoun
Fill in each blank with the best indefinite pronoun from the box below.
Word box: everyone, somebody, nothing, anything, everything, nobody, something, few
__________ in our class enjoys reading about space.
Is there __________ you would like to ask the teacher?
__________ left a glowing object near the field.
There is __________ wrong with the experiment. It worked perfectly!
__________ must be packed before we leave for the trip.
__________ has ever been to the Moon from our village.
I think __________ mysterious is happening in the lab.
Only a __________ of the students had seen a real rocket.
Activity 2: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence below has a mistake with the indefinite pronoun or the verb. Rewrite the sentence correctly.
Everyone are excited about the science fair.
Nobody know the answer to the question.
Everything have been cleaned in the lab.
Someone have left their lunch in the cafeteria.
Do anyone want to help with the project?
Activity 3: Write Your Own Sentences
Write a sentence using each of the following indefinite pronouns. Make sure your sentence is about space, science, or discovery!
everyone
something
nobody
anything
many
Activity 4: Someone or Anyone?
Choose the correct pronoun to complete each sentence. Remember: use someone/somebody in positive sentences and anyone/anybody in questions and negatives.
__________ should check the sensor before the presentation. (someone / anyone)
Does __________ have a spare pencil I can borrow? (someone / anyone)
I do not think __________ noticed the mistake. (someone / anyone)
__________ has been using the school tablets today. (Someone / Anyone)
Can __________ explain how the satellite works? (someone / anyone)
Answer Key: Grammar Practice
Activity 1:
Everyone in our class enjoys reading about space.
Is there anything you would like to ask the teacher?
Somebody left a glowing object near the field.
There is nothing wrong with the experiment. It worked perfectly!
Everything must be packed before we leave for the trip.
Nobody has ever been to the Moon from our village.
I think something mysterious is happening in the lab.
Only a few of the students had seen a real rocket.
Activity 2:
Everyone is excited about the science fair.
Nobody knows the answer to the question.
Everything has been cleaned in the lab.
Someone has left their lunch in the cafeteria.
Does anyone want to help with the project?
Activity 3: Answers will vary. Check that each sentence uses the correct indefinite pronoun and makes sense.
Activity 4:
Someone should check the sensor before the presentation.
Does anyone have a spare pencil I can borrow?
I do not think anyone noticed the mistake.
Someone has been using the school tablets today.
Can anyone explain how the satellite works?
Writing Workshop
In this section, you will use what you have learned about vocabulary, grammar, and reading to produce your own piece of writing. Take your time and enjoy the creative process!
Writing Task: A Space Discovery Blog Post

Imagine that you are a young astronaut writing a blog post about your first week on the International Space Station. Your blog should be written in an informal, friendly tone -- as if you are chatting with your friends and family back home.
Your blog post should include:
At least five vocabulary words from this unit (underline them)
At least four indefinite pronouns (circle them or write them in bold)
Contractions to make your writing sound natural (e.g. I'm, we've, it's, you'll)
A description of something surprising you discovered in space
A short paragraph about how the crew cooperates to get things done
A closing sentence that encourages your readers to keep exploring and learning
Your blog post should be between 150 and 250 words long.
Planning Your Blog Post
Before you start writing, it helps to plan your ideas. Use these questions to guide your thinking:
What is the most exciting thing that happened during your first week?
What did you see when you looked out of the window?
How does the crew work together on the station?
What would you tell your friends and family about living in space?
What advice would you give to someone who dreams of being an astronaut?
Editing Checklist
After writing your first draft, use this checklist to improve your work:
Have I used at least five vocabulary words from this unit?
Have I included at least four indefinite pronouns?
Does my blog sound friendly and informal?
Have I used contractions?
Have I checked my spelling and punctuation?
Does my blog have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Model Blog Post
Here is an example to inspire you. Notice the informal tone, vocabulary words, and indefinite pronouns:
Day 5 on the ISS -- Halima's Space Blog

Salaams from space! You'll never guess what happened today. Everyone on the crew was busy running experiments when something unexpected floated past the window. It was a tiny piece of an old satellite! Nobody could believe it.

Living up here is brilliant but also quite strange. Everything floats, including your food, your toothbrush, and even the water you drink! The crew has to cooperate on every single task. If someone forgets to close a hatch, it could become a real issue. We avoid mistakes by checking everything twice.

I feel so drowsy by the end of the day because we work long hours. But when I look out of the towering window and see Earth below, I forget all my tiredness. It is the most ideal view you could ever imagine.
If anyone asks me whether space is worth it, I will say yes. Nothing compares to floating among the stars. Keep dreaming, keep exploring, and never let anything stop you!
Bonus Focus: Biography and Autobiography

In this unit, you have been reading informative texts and writing blog posts. Another important type of text you will encounter is the biography and its close cousin, the autobiography. Understanding the difference between them will help you become a more skilled reader and writer.

A biography is the story of a person's life written by someone else. It is written in the third person, using pronouns like he, she, and they. Biographies focus on facts, achievements, and important events in the person's life.
Example: She became the first woman to command the International Space Station. Her achievements inspired millions of people around the world.

An autobiography is the story of a person's life written by the person themselves. It is written in the first person, using pronouns like I, me, and we. Autobiographies often include personal feelings, reflections, and memories.
Example: I remember the first time I looked out of the space station window. My heart was full of wonder and gratitude.
Here is a quick comparison:
Biography: third person (he, she, they) + written by someone else + factual focus
Autobiography: first person (I, me, we) + written by the person + personal reflections
Quick Activity: Read the two short passages below and decide which is from a biography and which is from an autobiography.

Passage A: 'I was so nervous before my first spacewalk. My hands were shaking inside my gloves, but I told myself to stay calm. When I opened the hatch, I could see the Earth below, and everything felt peaceful.'

Passage B: 'Dr Amira Hassan graduated from university in 2005. She joined the space programme three years later and completed her training with flying colours. Her team described her as one of the most dedicated astronauts they had ever worked with.'
Answer: Passage A is an autobiography (first person: I, my). Passage B is a biography (third person: she, her).
Listening and Speaking
Good communication is not just about writing. In this section, you will practise your speaking and listening skills through fun activities.
Activity 1: Astronaut Interview Role-Play
Work with a partner or ask your AI Tutor to role-play an interview. One person plays a journalist and the other plays an astronaut who has just returned from a space mission.
The journalist should ask at least five questions. Here are some ideas:
What was the most brilliant discovery you made in space?
Was there anything that surprised you about living on the ISS?
How did the crew cooperate during difficult moments?
Did everyone on the crew have the same job?
What advice would you give to someone who wants to become an astronaut?
The astronaut should answer in full sentences, using vocabulary from this unit and at least three indefinite pronouns.
Activity 2: Fact or Opinion?

Your teacher or AI Tutor will read out a list of statements about space. After each statement, decide whether it is a fact (something that can be proven true) or an opinion (what someone thinks or believes). Hold up a card or say your answer aloud.
Here are some statements to get you started:
The Moon orbits the Earth.
Space travel is the most exciting thing in the world.
The ISS has been in orbit since 1998.
Everyone should learn about astronomy.
Sputnik 1 was launched in 1957.
Stars are the most beautiful things in the sky.
Astronauts exercise for about two hours every day on the ISS.
Nobody can resist the beauty of a sunset from space.
After the activity, discuss with your partner: How can you tell the difference between a fact and an opinion?
Activity 3: Group Presentation -- Our Space Mission
In small groups, plan a pretend space mission. Give your mission a name and decide:
Where will you go? (the Moon, Mars, or beyond?)
What will you study or discover?
What equipment will you need?
How will the team cooperate to make the mission a success?
Each group member should present one part of the mission plan to the class. Use at least three vocabulary words and two indefinite pronouns in your presentation.
Activity 4: Adverb Challenge

Adverbs and adverbial phrases add detail to our sentences by telling us when, where, how, or how much something happens. In this activity, you will practise adding adverbs to make your sentences more interesting.
Look at the plain sentences below. Rewrite each one by adding an adverb or adverbial phrase from the box. Then read your improved sentence aloud to a partner.
Adverb box: in 1969, carefully, on the Moon, with great effort, finally, every day, at the space centre, patiently
The astronauts landed. --> The astronauts landed __________.
Scientists study the stars. --> Scientists study the stars __________.
The crew checked the equipment. --> The crew checked the equipment __________.
The rocket launched. --> __________, the rocket launched.
They trained for years. --> They trained __________ for years.
Possible answers:
The astronauts landed on the Moon / in 1969.
Scientists study the stars every day / at the space centre.
The crew checked the equipment carefully / patiently.
Finally, the rocket launched.
They trained with great effort / at the space centre for years.
Now try making up three sentences of your own about space, each one using a different type of adverb (time, place, or manner). Share them with your partner or AI Tutor.
Word Building: Prefixes

A prefix is a group of letters added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning. Learning prefixes helps you understand and build new words. Many English prefixes come from Greek and Latin, and they appear often in science and space-related vocabulary.
Useful Prefixes for This Unit
Quick Activity: Use the prefix clues above to work out the meaning of these words:
astrodome (astro- = star/space + dome): What do you think this word means?
teletext (tele- = far + text): How might this word relate to communication?
autobiography (auto- = self + biography): How is this different from a biography?
triennial (tri- = three + ennial from 'year'): How often does something triennial happen?
resupply (re- = again + supply): Why would a space station need to be resupplied?
Check your answers with your AI Tutor or discuss them with a partner.
Check Your Learning

You have worked hard throughout this unit! Use the checklist below to see how much you have learned. Tick each statement that you feel confident about. If there are any areas where you are not yet sure, go back and review that section before moving on.
I can use and explain at least 15 vocabulary words from this unit.
I know what indefinite pronouns are and can use them correctly in sentences.
I can tell the difference between someone/somebody and anyone/anybody.
I know that most indefinite pronouns for people and things use singular verbs.
I can read a passage and find the main ideas and supporting details.
I can tell the difference between a fact and an opinion.
I understand the difference between a biography and an autobiography.
I can write a blog post using informal language, contractions, and vocabulary from this unit.
I can add adverbs and adverbial phrases to make my sentences more detailed.
I can break words into prefixes and root words to work out their meaning.
I can take part in a role-play and speak clearly in full sentences.
I understand how people cooperate to achieve great things, in science and in life.
tables:
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are unsure about any word or idea in this lesson, ask your AI Tutor.
Try saying: What does the word 'cooperate' mean? or Can you give me an example of an indefinite pronoun?
The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices
On her birthday, she felt proud of her independence because she could choose her own cake.
A problem or topic that people talk about or need to think about
We had a big issue with our school project, so we worked together to fix it.
A large area of land that is controlled by one ruler or government
The Roman Empire was very powerful and included many countries.
To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known
The teacher will expose the secret to the class during the lesson.
A very bright light that is hard to see in
The glare from the sun made it hard to see the road.
A place or thing where something comes from
The river is the source of the water for our town.
To work together with others to achieve something
We need to cooperate to finish the project on time.
When people decide not to buy or use something to show they disagree
The students decided to boycott the cafeteria food because they wanted healthier options.
Always being there for someone or something you care about
My dog is very loyal; he always stays by my side.
To completely ruin or make something unusable
The storm will destroy the old tree in our yard.
To stay away from something or not do something
I try to avoid eating too much candy.
We need to hoist the flag up the pole.
To tell people something important or new
The teacher will announce the winner of the contest tomorrow.
The towering tree in the park is taller than all the other trees.
Feeling sleepy and wanting to rest or sleep
After staying up late, I felt very drowsy in class.
A hole or tunnel that an animal makes in the ground to live in
The rabbit dug a burrow to keep safe from predators.
The state of having something that belongs to you
Having ownership of a pet means you take care of it and love it.
The music was booming at the party, and everyone was dancing.
When someone thinks something is good or okay
I felt happy when my teacher gave me her approval for my project.
A written agreement between two or more people or groups
We signed a contract to agree on the rules for our project.
To think that someone means something different than what they really mean
I misunderstood what my friend said, so I answered the wrong question.
I like to consume fruit for a healthy snack.
Something that you can see or touch, like a toy or a book
The ball is a round object that we can play with.
She had a brilliant idea for the science project.
The best or most perfect thing or situation
An ideal day at the beach includes sunshine and fun games.

💡  Vocabulary Tip
When you come across a new word while reading, try to work out its meaning from the words around it.
This is called using context clues. It is one of the best ways to grow your vocabulary!

⭐  Did You Know?
The word 'algorithm' comes from the name of the Muslim mathematician Al-Khwarizmi, who lived in Baghdad around 800 CE.
Many star names we use in English today, such as Aldebaran and Betelgeuse, come from Arabic!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you found any question difficult, try asking your AI Tutor for a hint.
Say: Can you help me find a fact and an opinion in the passage about space?

⭐  Did You Know?
Many texts contain a mixture of facts and opinions. Good readers can spot the difference!
When you write your own blog post later in this unit, you will include both facts and your own opinions.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are confused about when to use 'someone' versus 'anyone', try asking:
When do I use 'someone' and when do I use 'anyone' in a sentence?

⭐  Did You Know?
In everyday English, we often use 'they' and 'their' to refer back to singular indefinite pronouns like 'someone' or 'everyone'.
For example: Everyone should bring their own lunch. This avoids saying 'his or her' every time!

✍️  Writing Tip
Start your blog with an exciting opening line to grab your reader's attention.
For example: You won't believe what happened on my third day in space!
Remember to use contractions and a friendly tone -- this is a blog, not an essay!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Need help starting your blog? Try asking:
Can you help me write an opening sentence for a blog about living in space?

⭐  Did You Know?
The astronaut Samantha Cristoforetti kept a blog while living on the ISS. Her blog posts are like a mix of autobiography and informal journalism!
You can read about her experiences to see how real astronauts describe life in space.

🎙️  Speaking Tip
When you answer a question, try to explain your answer with a reason or an example.
This makes your response more interesting and shows that you are thinking carefully!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want to check your adverb sentences? Try saying:
Can you check whether I have used adverbs of time, place, and manner correctly?

💡  Word Building Tip
When you see a long or unfamiliar word, try breaking it into parts: prefix + root word.
This strategy works for hundreds of English words and will make you a stronger reader!

💬  Well Done!
You have completed Unit 2: Exploring Space. Be proud of what you have achieved!
Remember, every great discovery starts with curiosity. Keep asking questions and keep learning.
If there is anything you want to revisit, go back and read through the sections again. Your AI Tutor is always here to help!

paragraphs: The Burrow Discovery
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 2 Story
Read the story below carefully. As you read, look out for the vocabulary words you have been learning and notice how the characters speak and work together. Enjoy the adventure!
The Burrow Discovery

In a quiet highland village in Ethiopia, a small school sat between green hills and rocky paths. Inside the school, excitement buzzed. This was the week of the Science Systems Fair, and everyone in Year 5 had to present a working science display.

At the centre of the classroom, Ayele and her team stood beside their table. "Our project is about animal homes," she explained. "We'll build a model of a burrow, show how animals live inside it, and explain why it's important."
Nuru, her classmate, adjusted the small object on top of their model. "This is our soil sensor," he said proudly. "It tells us how moist the ground is."
"You mean when it works," teased Kiya. "It didn't work at all yesterday."
Nuru rolled his eyes. "We fixed it. You'll see."
Later that day, the teacher made an announcement. "Students, listen up. There's a booming science blog competition. You'll write about your project, and winners will present at the town library!"
"Brilliant!" said Kiya. "We'll win!"
Ayele smiled. "Only if everyone does their part."
They started typing their blog post using the school tablets. Ayele wrote:
"Our group of learners studied animal homes. We built a burrow model. It was hard work, but we cooperated well."
Kiya added:
"You'll want to avoid using cheap glue! Our tunnel collapsed twice!"
Nuru continued:
"We discovered a glowing object in the field while collecting materials. At first, we misunderstood what it was. We thought it was plastic, but it turned out to be part of a meteor!"
Ayele laughed. "Don't forget to clearly explain that part. It sounds like a science movie."
They revised and used contractions: you'll, we've, they're. The blog sounded fun and real.
Later, the class voted on the best blog post. Another group presented about wind energy. "Our towering windmill model produces light," they said. "The glare from the bulbs shows it works!"
"The glare hurts my eyes," someone whispered.
Nuru sighed. "Our project doesn't light up. Maybe we'll lose."
"You should relax," Ayele said. "We're not in an empire. This is school, not war."
Kiya giggled. "Still, a win would be nice."
When the fair day arrived, families from different regions came. The head teacher gave a speech. "Today, we celebrate cooperation, observation, and hard work."
"Unless someone objects," Kiya whispered.
"No one will," said Nuru. "Unless we destroy something."
They laughed.
Their model worked. The burrow stayed standing, and the sensor beeped at the right time.
After the presentations, a visitor approached their table. "I'm from the National Science Youth Council," she said. "Where did this source idea come from?"
"It started when we saw rabbits dig near the road," Ayele said. "We wanted to expose how animals create cool, dark places to rest -- especially in this hot season."
"I was also inspired by a film about energy," Nuru added. "And the sensor just came to me."
"You seem very loyal to your work," the visitor said. "That's important in science."
Later, the team gathered to hear the blog results. The teacher smiled. "The winning team is... Ayele, Kiya, and Nuru!"
They cheered.
"You'll present next week at the library," she said. "But you'll also join a local energy project. Your task is to reduce glare from public lights and consume less electricity."
"Another project?" Nuru asked, feeling drowsy.
"You mustn't nap yet," Kiya said. "This is a great chance!"
"Okay, okay," Nuru said. "But I want snacks first."
The next day, they were walking to the community hall when they saw students outside, holding signs.
"What's happening?" asked Ayele.
"They're starting a boycott against plastic cups in the lunchroom," someone said. "They want to protect the environment."
"Should we join?" asked Kiya.
"I don't know," said Nuru. "I'd rather research it first."
"We should support it," Ayele said. "But let's find out more."
So they talked to the group leader. He showed them the data -- hundreds of cups used weekly, none recycled.
"You've got our approval," said Ayele. "We'll help announce it next week."
That afternoon, they worked on their new blog:
"Today, we joined a group of students to start a boycott of plastic cups. We cooperated, shared ideas, and wrote a plan. Not everyone agrees yet, but we're working on it."
"We also learned: not everything in science goes fast. You must be patient. But when you mix loyalty, effort, and curiosity -- the results can be brilliant."
Think About the Story
Now that you have read The Burrow Discovery, take some time to reflect on the story. Answer the questions below in full sentences.
Comprehension Questions
Where is the story set, and what event is taking place at the school?
What was Ayele's team project about?
What did Nuru discover in the field while collecting materials?
What competition did the teacher announce, and what was the prize?
Why did the team decide to join the boycott against plastic cups?
Deeper Thinking
How do Ayele, Kiya, and Nuru show cooperation throughout the story? Give at least two examples.
The visitor said, 'You seem very loyal to your work.' What do you think she meant by this?
Nuru says, 'I'd rather research it first,' when asked about joining the boycott. Do you think this was a wise response? Why or why not?
What lesson does the story teach about patience and working together?
If you could join Ayele's team for their next project, what would you suggest they investigate? Explain your idea.
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of the following words in the story and explain what it means based on how it is used:
booming (paragraph 6)
misunderstood (Nuru's blog entry)
expose (Ayele's explanation to the visitor)
consume (the teacher's instructions about the energy project)
approval (Ayele's response to the group leader)
tables:
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want to explore the story further? Try asking:
What are the main themes in The Burrow Discovery?
Can you help me find all the indefinite pronouns used in the story?

paragraphs: Grammar — Unit 3: Reflections
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Comparatives & Superlatives

This grammar file focuses on comparatives and superlatives. Work through each section one at a time. Read the explanations, study the examples, try the practice exercises, and then check your answers at the end.
Section 1: What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?
Why They Matter

Comparatives and superlatives are special forms of adjectives. They help us compare people, places, things, and ideas. When we compare poems, stories, characters, or events, we use these forms to say which is bigger, better, more beautiful, or most exciting.
There are two types:
A comparative compares two things: 'A haiku is shorter than a ballad.'
A superlative compares three or more things: 'She is the best poet in our class.'
Section 2: Forming Comparatives
Rule 1: Short Adjectives (One Syllable)
Add -er to the end of the adjective.
tall → taller — 'The tree is taller than the house.'
fast → faster — 'Zinzi ran faster than Sizwe.'
old → older — 'The charter is older than the museum.'
Spelling note: If the adjective ends in one vowel + one consonant, double the last consonant: big → bigger, hot → hotter, sad → sadder.
Rule 2: Adjectives Ending in -y
Change the y to i and add -er.
happy → happier — 'She felt happier after the presentation.'
easy → easier — 'The second exercise was easier than the first.'
funny → funnier — 'His joke was funnier than mine.'
Rule 3: Longer Adjectives (Two or More Syllables)
Use more before the adjective. Do not add -er.
beautiful → more beautiful — 'The sunset was more beautiful than yesterday’s.'
inclusive → more inclusive — 'Our rules are more inclusive than the old ones.'
competitive → more competitive — 'The debate was more competitive this year.'
Always use than after the comparative when naming both things being compared:
'Poetry is more creative than a report.'
Section 3: Forming Superlatives
Rule 1: Short Adjectives (One Syllable)
Add -est to the end and put the before it.
tall → the tallest — 'She is the tallest girl in the class.'
fast → the fastest — 'He is the fastest runner in the school.'
old → the oldest — 'That is the oldest building in town.'
Spelling note: Same doubling rule applies: big → the biggest, hot → the hottest.
Rule 2: Adjectives Ending in -y
Change the y to i and add -est. Put the before it.
happy → the happiest — 'That was the happiest day of my life.'
easy → the easiest — 'This was the easiest question on the test.'
pretty → the prettiest — 'She wore the prettiest dress.'
Rule 3: Longer Adjectives (Two or More Syllables)
Use the most before the adjective. Do not add -est.
beautiful → the most beautiful — 'That is the most beautiful poem I have ever read.'
protective → the most protective — 'Our constitution is the most protective we have had.'
exciting → the most exciting — 'That was the most exciting presentation.'
Section 4: Irregular Comparatives and Superlatives
Some common adjectives do not follow the regular rules. You need to memorise them:
Examples in sentences:
'She is a better writer than I am.' (not 'gooder')
'That was the worst storm in years.' (not 'the most bad')
'He has more books than anyone.' (not 'mucher')
'She ate the least food at dinner.' (not 'the most little')
Section 5: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them

Double comparative: Do not use -er and more together.
• Wrong: 'She is more taller than her sister.'
• Correct: 'She is taller than her sister.'

Missing 'the' in superlatives: Always use 'the' before a superlative.
• Wrong: 'He is fastest runner.'
• Correct: 'He is the fastest runner.'

Using -est with long adjectives: Do not add -est to long adjectives.
• Wrong: 'This is the beautifulest poem.'
• Correct: 'This is the most beautiful poem.'

Wrong irregular form: Do not add -er/-est to irregular adjectives.
• Wrong: 'My drawing is gooder than yours.'
• Correct: 'My drawing is better than yours.'

Forgetting 'than' in comparatives: Include 'than' when naming both things.
• Wrong: 'She is taller me.'
• Correct: 'She is taller than me.'
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Choose the Correct Form
Circle or write the correct word in brackets:
A camel is (bigger / more bigger) than a goat.
That was (the most exciting / the excitingest) story I have ever read.
My sister is (more careful / carefuler) than I am.
This is (the worst / the baddest) meal I have ever tasted.
Her poem is (beautifuler / more beautiful) than mine.
He is (the fastest / the most fast) boy in our class.
Today is (hotter / more hot) than yesterday.
She is (the happiest / the most happy) person I know.
Exercise 2: Complete the Sentence
Write the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets:
This book is __________ (interesting) than the last one.
Mount Kilimanjaro is __________ (tall) mountain in Africa.
My brother's handwriting is __________ (neat) than mine.
She is __________ (good) at maths than at English.
That was __________ (bad) film I have ever watched.
A cheetah is __________ (fast) land animal.
This exercise is __________ (easy) than Exercise 1.
He told __________ (funny) joke in the whole school.
Exercise 3: Spot and Fix the Mistake
Each sentence has one mistake. Rewrite the sentence correctly:
'She is more smarter than her brother.'
'This is the most biggest building in our town.'
'He is fastest player on the team.'
'My cat is more friendlier than my dog.'
'That was the goodest day of my life.'
'The red car is expensiver than the blue one.'
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Sentences
Write one sentence for each instruction:
Use a comparative to compare two fruits.
Use a superlative to say which animal is the best.
Use the irregular comparative of good in a sentence.
Use the irregular superlative of bad in a sentence.
Use more + a long adjective to compare two places.
Use the most + a long adjective to describe your favourite poem.
Exercise 5: Comparatives and Superlatives in 'The Cracked Charter'
Read these sentences from the story. Underline the comparative or superlative and say which type it is:
'Modern policies are more inclusive than early ones.'
'The current constitution is the most protective we have had.'
'Our system must be fairer, clearer, and more competitive.'
'That is the most powerful kind of constitution.'
Answer Key
Exercise 1
1. bigger  2. the most exciting  3. more careful  4. the worst  5. more beautiful  6. the fastest  7. hotter  8. the happiest
Exercise 2
1. more interesting  2. the tallest  3. neater  4. better  5. the worst  6. the fastest  7. easier  8. the funniest
Exercise 3
'She is smarter than her brother.' (remove 'more')
'This is the biggest building in our town.' (remove 'most')
'He is the fastest player on the team.' (add 'the')
'My cat is friendlier than my dog.' (remove 'more')
'That was the best day of my life.' (good → best, not goodest)
'The red car is more expensive than the blue one.' (use 'more', not -er)
Exercise 4
Answers will vary. Check that each sentence uses the correct form. Ask your AI tutor to review your sentences.
Exercise 5
'more inclusive' – comparative (comparing modern and early policies).
'the most protective' – superlative (comparing all constitutions).
'fairer, clearer, more competitive' – comparatives (comparing the new system with the old).
'the most powerful' – superlative (saying this kind is the greatest).
tables:
💡  Memory Tip
Short word = -er / -est (tall, taller, tallest).
Long word = more / the most (beautiful, more beautiful, the most beautiful).
Ends in -y = change y to i, then -er / -est (happy, happier, happiest).
Irregular = just memorise them (good, better, best).

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my answers and explain any I got wrong?
Can you give me five more sentences to practise comparatives and superlatives?
Can you explain why we say 'better' instead of 'gooder'?

paragraphs: Unit 3: Reflections
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Unit 3 is called Reflections because it is all about looking closely at the world around you and expressing what you see, think, and feel through poetry and reflective writing. Poets use special tools to paint pictures with words, and by the end of this unit you will use those same tools yourself.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Identify and explain similes, metaphors, and personification in poems.
Read and understand different kinds of poetry, including haiku.
Count syllables and understand how poets use rhythm and sound.
Tell the difference between literal and figurative language.
Describe the theme and tone of a poem in your own words.
Use comparatives and superlatives correctly in sentences.
Write your own reflective poem or piece of reflective writing.
Perform a poem aloud with expression, and give helpful feedback to a classmate.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Read the Reading section below carefully, then answer the comprehension questions.
Work through the Grammar Focus on comparatives and superlatives, then do the practice.
Complete the Writing Workshop to create your own reflective poem.
Practise the Listening and Speaking activities with your AI tutor or a partner.
Read the Story file, 'The Cracked Charter', and answer the reflection questions.
Finish with Check Your Learning to see what you have achieved.
Key Vocabulary

Here are the most important words for this unit. You will meet them in the reading, the grammar, and the writing sections. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary companion file where you will find the full definition and example sentence.
Poetry and Language Words
Story and Unit Words
These words appear in 'The Cracked Charter' and throughout the unit:
charter – a special document giving rights or rules to a group
constitution – the set of rules that explains how a country works
fracture – a break or crack in something hard
tradition – a way of doing things passed down through generations
collaborative – working together with others
legislative – related to making laws or rules
architect – a person who designs buildings and structures
artifacts – objects made by people long ago
Reading: Poetry and Reflection

In this section you will read two poems and one short reflective text. As you read, look for the figurative language tools you learned about in your vocabulary: similes, metaphors, and personification. Read each poem at least twice — once silently, and once aloud.
Poem 1: The Sea
This poem uses personification and metaphor to describe the ocean. Read it aloud slowly, feeling the rhythm of the words.
The sea is a hungry dog,
Giant and grey.
He rolls on the beach all day.
With his clashing teeth and shaggy jaws
Hour upon hour he gnaws
The rumbling, tumbling stones,
And 'Bones, bones, bones, bones!'
The giant sea-dog moans,
Licking his greasy paws.
And when the night wind roars
And the moon rocks in the stormy cloud,
He bounds to his feet and snuffs and sniffs,
Shaking his wet sides over the cliffs,
And howls and howls and howls and howls.
But on quiet days in May or June,
When even the grasses on the dune
Play no more their reedy tune,
He lies on the sandy shores,
So quiet, so quiet, he scarcely snores.
— James Reeves
After reading, think about these questions:
The poet says 'The sea is a hungry dog.' Is this a simile or a metaphor? How do you know?
Find two examples of personification in the poem. What human actions does the sea perform?
What is the tone of the poem in the second stanza? Is it calm or wild? What words tell you?
In the last stanza the sea 'scarcely snores.' What does this tell us about the sea in May or June?
What is the theme of this poem? What is the poet trying to say about the sea?
Close Reading: How to Analyse a Poem
Before we move to our next poem, let us learn a step-by-step way to analyse any poem. You can use these steps every time you read poetry:
Read it through once without stopping. Just enjoy the sound and rhythm.
Read it again slowly. This time, underline or circle words you do not know.
Identify the subject: What is the poem about? What is the poet describing?
Look for figurative language: Are there similes, metaphors, or personification? Mark them.
Think about the structure: How many stanzas are there? Do the lines rhyme? Is there a pattern?
Consider the tone: What feeling does the poem give you? Is it joyful, sad, calm, wild, thoughtful, or playful?
Find the theme: What is the poet's main message or idea? What do they want us to think about after reading?
Form your opinion: Which part did you like best? What would you ask the poet if you could meet them?
Now practise these steps with 'The Sea' before you move on. Write one sentence for each step in your notebook.
Poem 2: Haiku Collection

A haiku is a very short Japanese poem with a strict syllable pattern: 5 syllables in the first line, 7 syllables in the second, and 5 syllables in the third. Haiku usually describe nature and end with a surprising image or comparison.
Haiku 1:
Pen-guins, black and white
Sur-vi-ving in i-cy seas
State-ly em-pe-rors
Haiku 2:
Boi-ling, hot la-va
Bub-bling from the moun-tain top
Ea-ger to be free
Haiku 3:
Cool morn-ing dew drops
Spar-kling on the spi-der's web
Na-ture's dia-mond ring
After reading, think about these questions:
Count the syllables in each line of Haiku 1. Does it follow the 5–7–5 pattern?
In Haiku 2, the lava is described as 'Eager to be free.' What kind of figurative language is this?
In Haiku 3, what is being compared to a diamond ring? Is this a simile or a metaphor?
Which haiku do you like best? Write one sentence explaining why.
Try clapping the syllables of each line. Which line was hardest to count?
Reading 3: A Reflective Passage — 'Looking at the Wind'
This short reflective text is inspired by the poem 'Wind' by Christina Rossetti. Read it carefully and think about how the writer uses descriptive language.

I remember the day the wind came to our neighbourhood like an uninvited guest. It arrived without warning, lifting the dust from the streets and hurling it against our windows. The old acacia tree in our courtyard trembled and bent, as though it were bowing to a powerful king. Sheets of laundry flew from the line like startled birds.

My little sister Halimo hid under the table, her eyes wide. 'Is the wind angry?' she whispered. I told her no, the wind is not angry — it is just doing its job, pushing clouds across the sky so the rain can follow. But secretly, I thought the wind did sound angry that day. It howled between the buildings and rattled every door.

When it stopped, the world was quiet in a way I had never noticed before. The leaves lay still. The dust settled. Everything looked washed and new, as if the wind had swept the neighbourhood clean. I stepped outside and breathed in the cool air. Hooyo called me back inside for supper, but I stood there a moment longer, feeling grateful. Even something wild and frightening can leave the world more beautiful than it found it.
After reading, answer these questions:
Find one simile in the passage and explain what two things are being compared.
Find one metaphor in the passage. What is being described as something else?
Find one example of personification. What human action is given to a non-human thing?
What is the theme of this reflective passage? What lesson does the writer learn?
The writer says the wind was 'like an uninvited guest.' What does this tell you about how the wind arrived?
Why do you think the writer calls this piece a 'reflection'? What is the writer reflecting on?
Understanding the Text
Now that you have read the poems and the reflective passage, let us go deeper. Complete the activities below to show your understanding.

Good readers do not just read the words on the page. They ask questions, make connections, and think about what the writer is really saying. This is called critical reading, and it is an important skill for Year 5 and beyond. The activities below will help you practise reading critically.
Activity 1: Figurative Language Hunt
Copy and complete this table in your notebook. Find one example of each type from the texts you have read:
Activity 2: Comparing Poems
Think about 'The Sea' and the haiku collection. Answer these questions in full sentences:
How is 'The Sea' different from a haiku in length and structure?
Which poem uses more figurative language? Give an example from each.
Both describe nature. How does the tone of 'The Sea' compare with the tone of Haiku 3?
Which style of poem would you prefer to write yourself, and why?
Activity 3: Theme and Tone Chart
Copy this chart into your notebook and fill in each row based on the texts you read:

After completing the chart, write a paragraph comparing the theme and tone of any two texts. Use at least one comparative in your paragraph, for example: 'The tone of The Sea is wilder than the tone of Haiku 3.'
Activity 4: Vocabulary in Context
Use context clues from the reading to explain what these words mean. Then check your Vocabulary file:
gnaws (from 'The Sea')
stately (from Haiku 1)
hurling (from 'Looking at the Wind')
trembled (from 'Looking at the Wind')
scarcely (from 'The Sea')
Grammar Focus: Comparatives and Superlatives

In this unit, your main grammar topic is comparatives and superlatives. These are special forms of adjectives that help us compare things. You will use them when you compare poems, characters, and ideas in your writing.
What Are Comparatives?
We use a comparative adjective to compare two things. It tells us that one thing has more or less of a quality than the other.
One-syllable adjectives: add -er. Example: tall → taller
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change y to i and add -er. Example: happy → happier
Longer adjectives (two or more syllables): use more before the adjective. Example: beautiful → more beautiful
Always use than after the comparative when you name both things:
The constitution is more powerful than a charter.
A haiku is shorter than a free verse poem.
Zinzi's idea was more creative than Sizwe's first suggestion.
What Are Superlatives?
We use a superlative adjective to say that one thing is the most or least in a group of three or more.
One-syllable adjectives: add -est. Example: tall → tallest
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change y to i and add -est. Example: happy → happiest
Longer adjectives: use the most before the adjective. Example: beautiful → the most beautiful
Always use the before a superlative:
That was the most competitive debate in the school.
This is the tallest tree in the courtyard.
She wrote the best haiku in the class.
Irregular Comparatives and Superlatives
Some adjectives do not follow the rules. You need to memorise these:
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Do not use -er and more together. Wrong: 'She is more taller than her brother.' Correct: 'She is taller than her brother.'
Do not forget the before a superlative. Wrong: 'He is fastest runner.' Correct: 'He is the fastest runner.'
Do not add -er or -est to long adjectives. Wrong: 'This is the beautifulest poem.' Correct: 'This is the most beautiful poem.'
Grammar Practice
Exercise 1: Fill in the Comparative
Complete each sentence with the correct comparative form of the adjective in brackets:
A haiku is __________ (short) than a ballad.
The second stanza of 'The Sea' is __________ (wild) than the third stanza.
Our new constitution is __________ (inclusive) than the old one.
Zinzi felt __________ (confident) after the presentation than before it.
Writing a metaphor is __________ (difficult) than writing a simile.
Exercise 2: Fill in the Superlative
Complete each sentence with the correct superlative form:
That was __________ (exciting) poem I have ever read.
She is __________ (good) poet in our school.
The wind was __________ (strong) force of nature that day.
This is __________ (beautiful) haiku in the collection.
He wrote __________ (bad) essay in the class, but he improved next time.
Exercise 3: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence has a mistake with comparatives or superlatives. Rewrite the sentence correctly:
'She is more taller than her sister.'
'He is fastest runner in the team.'
'This poem is the most beautifulest I have read.'
'My handwriting is gooder now than last term.'
'That was the baddest storm in ten years.'
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Comparisons
Write one sentence for each prompt using the correct comparative or superlative:
Compare two animals you know. Use a comparative.
Say which food is the best in your family. Use a superlative.
Compare your favourite poem from this unit with another. Use a comparative.
Describe the most beautiful place you have seen. Use a superlative.
Answer Key: Grammar Practice
Exercise 1: 1. shorter  2. wilder  3. more inclusive  4. more confident  5. more difficult
Exercise 2: 1. the most exciting  2. the best  3. the strongest  4. the most beautiful  5. the worst
Exercise 3:
'She is taller than her sister.'
'He is the fastest runner in the team.'
'This poem is the most beautiful I have read.'
'My handwriting is better now than last term.'
'That was the worst storm in ten years.'
Writing Workshop: Reflective and Poetry Writing

Now it is your turn to write! In this section you will create your own reflective poem and a short piece of reflective writing. Take your time, draft first, and then edit. Good writers always revise their work.

What is reflective writing? Reflective writing is when you describe an experience, a memory, or something you have observed, and then explain what you thought and felt about it. It is personal — you use the word 'I' and share your own honest reactions. The reflective passage 'Looking at the Wind' is a good example. The writer described the wind, told us how they felt, and then shared a bigger thought about how something scary can also be beautiful.

What makes a good poem? A good poem does not have to be long. It should choose its words carefully, create a picture or feeling in the reader's mind, and leave the reader thinking. Use figurative language to make your poem more powerful, but do not force it — every simile and metaphor should feel natural and true to what you are describing.
Task 1: Write a Haiku
Write your own haiku about something in nature. Remember the rules:
Line 1: 5 syllables — describe what you see (literal).
Line 2: 7 syllables — add context or action.
Line 3: 5 syllables — end with a surprising image or comparison (figurative).
Example topics: the sea, the wind, a sunset, a bird, rain, the moon, a tree.
Draft your haiku here, then count the syllables by clapping:
Task 2: Write a Simile Poem
Write a short poem (4–8 lines) about a topic you care about. Include at least two similes and one metaphor. Here is a frame to help you start:
The ______ is like ______,
And the ______ is a ______.
Possible topics: your home, your school, a storm, the market, your favourite season, the ocean, your neighbourhood.
Task 3: Reflective Writing

Write a short reflective paragraph (8–12 sentences) about a time when something in nature surprised you or made you think. It could be a storm, a sunset, an animal, or a change of season. Use these steps:
Set the scene: Where were you? When did it happen? What did you see?
Describe your feelings: How did you feel at first? Did your feelings change?
Use figurative language: Include at least one simile, one metaphor, or one example of personification.
Reflect: What did you learn or realise? Why does this moment stay in your memory?
Conclude: End with a sentence that tells the reader something meaningful.
After writing, read your paragraph aloud. Check: Does it have a clear beginning, middle, and end? Did you use at least one piece of figurative language? Did you use any comparatives or superlatives?
Task 4: Writing With Comparatives and Superlatives
Write a short paragraph (5-8 sentences) comparing two places, two seasons, or two animals. Your paragraph must include:
At least two comparatives (e.g. taller than, more colourful than).
At least one superlative (e.g. the most beautiful, the fastest).
At least one simile or metaphor to make your description vivid.
Here is an example to guide you:

The rainy season is more colourful than the dry season. When the rains come, the grass grows greener than an emerald. Flowers burst open everywhere — it is like a painting coming to life. The air feels cooler and fresher, and the sky is the most beautiful when the sun breaks through the clouds after a storm. I think the rainy season is the best time of year in our town.
Task 5: Peer Feedback (Optional)
If you are studying with a partner or in a group, swap your haiku or poem with a classmate. Use these questions to give helpful feedback:
Does the haiku follow the 5–7–5 syllable pattern?
Can you find a simile, a metaphor, or personification in their poem?
What is the strongest line in their poem? (Use a superlative!)
What is one thing they could improve to make the poem even more vivid? (Use a comparative!)
Listening and Speaking
Poetry is meant to be heard, not just read. In this section you will practise reading poems aloud, listening carefully, and having conversations about poetry.
Listening Activity 1: Hear the Poem
Ask your AI tutor to read 'The Sea' aloud for you. As you listen:
Close your eyes and picture the sea in your mind.
Notice where the reader's voice gets louder or softer. Why?
Listen for the words that rhyme. Can you name three pairs?
Now listen to the poem a second time, and this time follow along with the text. Were there any words you heard differently from how you expected?
Speaking Activity 1: Choral Verse Performance
Choose one of the three stanzas of 'The Sea'. Practise reading it aloud with expression. Think about:
Volume: When should your voice be loud? When should it be soft?
Speed: Should you read the stormy stanza quickly or slowly?
Tone: What feeling do you want the listener to have?
If you are in a group, divide the stanzas between you. Practise reading the whole poem together as choral verse — that means reading aloud as a group, like a choir.
Speaking Activity 2: Poetry Interview
Imagine you could interview the poet James Reeves. With a partner or your AI tutor, take turns being the interviewer and the poet. Here are some starter questions:
Why did you compare the sea to a dog?
Which stanza is your favourite, and why?
What advice would you give a young poet writing their first poem?
Is there a sea near where you grew up? How did it make you feel?
Speaking Activity 3: Present Your Poem

When your haiku or simile poem is finished, practise presenting it to an audience. This could be your family, your study partner, or your AI tutor. Before you present, prepare by thinking about these questions:
What is the title of your poem, and why did you choose it?
What inspired you to write about this topic?
Which line are you most proud of, and why?
What kind of figurative language did you use?

When you present, speak clearly and slowly. Make eye contact with your audience. After reading your poem aloud, invite questions. This is excellent practice for building confidence in English speaking.
Listening Activity 2: Compare Two Readings
Ask your AI tutor to read the first stanza of 'The Sea' in two different ways: first in a calm, quiet voice, and then in a loud, dramatic voice. After listening:
Which reading felt more like the poem? Why?
How does the way a poem is read change its tone?
Which style did you prefer? Explain your choice.
Listening Activity 3: Haiku Dictation
Ask your AI tutor to read one of the haiku aloud. Write down exactly what you hear, then check your version against the text. Did you get the spelling right? Did you remember the line breaks?
Check Your Learning

Well done — you have worked through the whole of Unit 3! Use this checklist to see what you have achieved. Tick 'Yes' or 'Not yet'. For every 'Not yet', go back to that section or ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can identify a simile in a poem and explain it.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can identify a metaphor in a poem and explain it.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can identify personification and say what human action is used.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can count syllables and check if a haiku is 5–7–5.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can explain the theme and tone of a poem.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use comparatives correctly (e.g. taller, more beautiful).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use superlatives correctly (e.g. the tallest, the most beautiful).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write my own haiku with the correct syllable pattern.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a reflective paragraph with figurative language.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can read a poem aloud with expression and the right tone.   Yes □   Not yet □
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you at any time. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: 'I don't understand similes. Can you explain them in a different way?'
Practise speaking: 'Can you listen to me read this poem and give me feedback?'
Get feedback: 'Can you check my haiku and tell me if the syllables are right?'
Ask for more practice: 'Can you give me more examples of comparatives and superlatives?'
Prepare for your teacher: 'What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?'
A Simple Daily Routine
Getting Ready for Unit 4
Make sure you know all the Unit 3 words on your Word Wall.
Keep reading and writing poetry — even one haiku a day builds your skill.
Tell your AI tutor: 'What will I learn in Unit 4? Can you help me get ready?'
Excellent work — you are becoming a reflective reader and writer. Every poem you read and write makes your English stronger. Keep going!
tables:
🧭  How to use this unit
This is your main lesson for Unit 3. It takes you through reading, grammar, writing, speaking and self-assessment.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (Comparatives and Superlatives), and the Story 'The Cracked Charter'.
Study a little every day. Read aloud, say your answers before you write them, and use your AI tutor whenever you need help. Bring your work to your teacher sessions.
A comparison using like or as
Her smile was as bright as the sun.
Saying one thing is another to compare
The sea is a hungry dog.
Giving human qualities to non-living things
The wind hurled dirt across the road.
A unit of sound with one vowel sound
The exact, ordinary meaning of a word
Literal meaning: the sky is blue.
Using words in an imaginative, non-literal way
Figurative meaning: she has a heart of gold.
The central idea or message of a text
The theme of the poem was nature's power.
The feeling or mood a writer creates
The tone of the haiku was calm and peaceful.
A word with a similar meaning to another word

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you test me on the difference between a simile and a metaphor?
Can you use the word personification in three new sentences for me?
What does figurative mean? Can you give me examples from everyday life?

📖  Before you begin reading
Have a pencil and notebook ready. Write down any words you do not know.
As you read each poem, try to picture what the poet is describing.
After each poem, pause and ask yourself: What did I see in my mind? How did the poem make me feel?
Remember: poetry is not a race. Take your time with every line.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me find all the figurative language in 'The Sea'?
What does the word 'gnaws' mean? Can you give me a synonym?
Can you explain what 'reedy tune' means in the last stanza?

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you walk me through the poem analysis steps using 'The Sea' as an example?
I found a simile in the poem but I am not sure. Can you check?
What is the difference between a stanza and a paragraph?

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me count the syllables in each haiku line by clapping them?
Why does a haiku have only three lines? What makes it special?
Can you show me how to write my own haiku about rain?

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me find all three types of figurative language in this passage?
What does the Somali word 'Hooyo' mean? Why did the writer include it?
Can you ask me questions to check if I understood the passage?
What is being described as what?
One word that shows the tone

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on figurative language with examples from the poems?
Can you explain the difference between theme and tone with a new example?
I am stuck on Activity 2. Can you help me compare the two poems step by step?
She is a better writer than her brother. She is the best writer in the class.
Today's weather is worse than yesterday's. It was the worst storm of the year.
The school is farther than the mosque. It is the farthest building.
She has more books than I do. She has the most books in the family.

💡  Memory Tip
Short adjective (one syllable) = add -er or -est (tall, taller, tallest).
Long adjective (two or more syllables) = use more or the most (beautiful, more beautiful, the most beautiful).
If the adjective ends in -y, change y to i first (happy, happier, happiest).

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor

paragraphs: Story — Unit 3: Reflections
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Cracked Charter”
The Cracked Charter

In a township near Cape Town, an old courthouse stood beside a crumbling museum. The walls were dusty, and the sign outside was faded. But inside, a class of Year 5 students stood in awe. They had come for a history project, but they were about to solve something unexpected.

Their teacher, Mr. Dlamini, led them into a quiet hall. “This building once held debates,” he said. “Here, people argued about policy, voted on rules, and wrote early drafts of our town’s constitution.”
Zinzi walked slowly across the tiled floor. “It smells like paper and time,” she whispered.
Nomusa pointed to a case in the centre of the room. “Look! There’s the original town charter.”
It was yellowed with age, sealed in glass.
Sizwe leaned closer. “There’s a fracture in it,” he said. “A line running right through the page!”
“That’s strange,” Mr. Dlamini said. “Let’s ask the curator.”
They met Mrs. Nkosi, the museum guide, in the lobby.
“Why is there a crack in the charter?” asked Zinzi.
Mrs. Nkosi smiled. “That’s part of the story. Some say the town council had a fracture in trust. A disagreement broke unity the day the charter was signed.”
Zinzi raised her hand. “Can we study it for our project?”
“You may,” she said. “But treat it with care — it’s an artifact, and very protective glass surrounds it.”
The students began to sketch the charter and read the labels around it.
Suddenly, Nomusa gasped. “This stamp… it’s not from our town!”
Mr. Dlamini’s eyes narrowed. “That’s right. This symbol is legislative — it belonged to the regional capital, not our township.”
“Why would their symbol be on our charter?” asked Sizwe.
Mrs. Nkosi nodded slowly. “Now that is a question worth asking.”
The next week, the students turned the mystery into a class mission.
They split into groups. One researched the history of the local government. Another studied the roles of architects who had helped design the early miniature courthouse.
“Look,” Zinzi said. “One of the architects lobbied for a bigger hall — he wanted more public voices in meetings.”
“But the mayor at the time preferred small rooms,” said Sizwe. “He believed public tension could lead to infection — social unrest.”
“That’s a weak argument,” said Nomusa. “Fear of expansion isn’t a reason to silence people.”
Their teacher nodded. “Use that in your report. That’s an opinion backed by comprehension of history.”
At the presentation day, the class decorated their wall with maps, notes, and a collaborative mural showing voices breaking through a cracked wall.
Nomusa explained, “We believe the fracture in the charter is both physical and symbolic. It shows the fight between voices wanting tradition and others asking for change.”
Sizwe added, “Some feared alternative policies. But over time, people function better when ideas are shared.”
They used comparatives to explain how modern policies are “more inclusive than early ones” and said the current constitution is “the most protective we’ve had.”
The room clapped.
That afternoon, the mayor visited. She smiled and said, “You’ve opened a new dimension to an old problem.”
Zinzi asked shyly, “Can we suggest ideas for a new youth charter?”
“You may,” the mayor said. “Write one. Let it reflect your generation’s hopes.”
Over the next week, the class wrote together.

Nomusa typed:
“We believe no one is too small to speak.
Our mission is to learn, listen, and lead.”

Zinzi added:
“We will value all traditions — but not let them block progress.”

Sizwe closed with:
“Our system must be fairer, clearer, and more competitive, not in conflict — but in courage.”
They titled it: “The Students’ Charter of Hope.”
On the final day, the mayor returned. The students stood straight, their posture showing pride.
“You’ve written a beautiful document,” she said. “One day, some of you may serve in our legislative halls.”
“And I’ll build them,” said Zinzi. “I want to be an architect.”
“And I’ll protect them,” said Sizwe. “Like a vulture guarding its hill.”
They laughed.
“You function well as a team,” the mayor said. “And that’s the most powerful kind of constitution.”
As they left, the cracked charter remained in its case — but now, it had new meaning. It was no longer just a broken page. It was a lesson.
A reminder that even fractures can lead to better foundations.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Where does the story take place? Describe the setting in your own words.
What did the students find wrong with the charter in the glass case?
Why was the stamp on the charter surprising to the students?
What does Mrs. Nkosi mean when she says the council had 'a fracture in trust'?
How did the students investigate the mystery? What groups did they form?
What did the architect want, and why did the old mayor disagree?
How did the students present their findings on presentation day?
What comparatives and superlatives did the students use in their presentation?
What did the mayor ask the students to write? What did they call it?
What do you think the title 'The Cracked Charter' means — both literally and figuratively?
Think and Discuss
Discuss these questions with a partner, your family, or your AI tutor:
Nomusa says, 'Fear of expansion is not a reason to silence people.' Do you agree? Why or why not?
The mayor tells the students, 'You function well as a team, and that is the most powerful kind of constitution.' What does she mean?
If you could add one rule to 'The Students' Charter of Hope', what would it be?
The story says 'even fractures can lead to better foundations.' Can you think of a time when something difficult led to something good?
Vocabulary Spotlight
Find these words in the story and write the sentence they appear in. Then explain what each word means using context clues:
charter
fracture
lobby
legislative
collaborative
artifacts
architect
constitution
mission
tradition
posture
vulture
Writing Extension
Choose one of these writing tasks:
Write your own youth charter with 5 rules you think are important for young people.
Write a letter to the mayor of your town suggesting one change you would like to see.
Imagine you are Zinzi, Nomusa, or Sizwe. Write a diary entry about the day you discovered the mystery of the cracked charter.
