paragraphs: Grammar  —  Defining Relative Clauses
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Unit 4 Grammar
What Are Defining Relative Clauses?

A defining relative clause is a group of words that gives us essential information about a noun. It tells us exactly which person, place or thing we are talking about. Without the clause, the sentence would be unclear or incomplete.
Look at this example:
"The explorer who made the remarkable discovery shared it with his team."

The words "who made the remarkable discovery" form a defining relative clause. They tell us which explorer we mean. If we remove them, the sentence becomes: "The explorer shared it with his team" — and we do not know which explorer.
Why Does This Matter?

Defining relative clauses make your writing clearer and more detailed. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can combine them into one smooth sentence that gives the reader all the information at once. This is especially important in storytelling, report writing and explanations.
The Relative Pronouns: Who, Which, That
A defining relative clause always starts with a relative pronoun. There are three main ones to learn:
Who
Use who when talking about people.
The girl who found the spring saved the village.
The teacher who loves science teaches us every day.
The student who finished the test early helped others.
Which
Use which when talking about things (not people).
The book which was on the table is mine.
The spring which lay beneath the dunes was hidden for centuries.
The movie which won the award was fantastic.
That
Use that for people or things. It is the most flexible relative pronoun and can often replace who or which.
The cat that chased the mouse ran up the tree.
The path that Amina followed was safe.
The storm that delayed us led to an amazing discovery.
How to Build a Defining Relative Clause
Follow these four steps:
Identify the noun you want to describe. Example: "the explorer".
Choose the right relative pronoun: who for people, which for things, that for either.
Add a clause that gives essential information about that noun. Example: "who made the remarkable discovery".
Place the clause right after the noun. Example: "The explorer who made the remarkable discovery shared it with his team."
Practice: Combine Two Sentences
You can use a defining relative clause to join two short sentences into one:
Short sentences: The dog barked all night. It belongs to my neighbour.
Combined: The dog that barked all night belongs to my neighbour.
Short sentences: The teacher told us the story. She is very kind.
Combined: The teacher who told us the story is very kind.
The No-Comma Rule
This is the most important rule to remember:
Defining relative clauses do NOT use commas.
The information inside a defining relative clause is essential — it tells us which person or thing we mean. Because it is essential, we do not separate it from the rest of the sentence with commas.
Correct vs Incorrect
CORRECT: The boy who is wearing a red hat is my brother.
INCORRECT: The boy, who is wearing a red hat, is my brother.

(The incorrect version, with commas, is actually a different type of clause called a non-defining relative clause. You will learn about that later. For now, remember: no commas in defining relative clauses.)
Memory Trick: The 3 W’s
Remember these three words:
Who → for people
Which → for things
That → for either (people or things)

Think of it like picking the right key for the right door. If the door is labelled "people", use who. If it is labelled "things", use which. If you are not sure, that is like a master key — it works on both doors!
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Adding Commas
Wrong: The boy, who is wearing a red hat, is my brother.
Right: The boy who is wearing a red hat is my brother.
Fix: Remember that defining relative clauses give essential information. No commas needed.
Mistake 2: Using the Wrong Pronoun
Wrong: The book who I read was interesting.
Right: The book which I read was interesting. (Or: The book that I read...)
Fix: Ask yourself: Am I talking about a person or a thing? Use who for people, which for things.
Mistake 3: Putting the Clause in the Wrong Place
Wrong: Who made the discovery the explorer shared the map.
Right: The explorer who made the discovery shared the map.
Fix: The relative clause must come immediately after the noun it describes.
Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blank
Choose the correct relative pronoun (who, which or that) for each sentence.
The dog ______ barked all night kept everyone awake.
The singer ______ won the contest is very talented.
The game ______ we played yesterday was exciting.
The woman ______ lives next door is a doctor.
The cake ______ my mother baked was delicious.
The children ______ arrived early got the best seats.
The story ______ the teacher told was about a brave explorer.
The car ______ is parked outside belongs to my uncle.
Exercise 2: Combine the Sentences
Join each pair of sentences using a defining relative clause.
The boy won the race. He trains every morning.
The house is very old. It stands on the hill.
The teacher is strict but fair. She teaches us maths.
The river flows through our town. It is very wide.
The man helped us find the way. He was wearing a blue jacket.
The legend teaches us about patience. It was told by the elders.
Exercise 3: Spot the Mistake
Each sentence below has a mistake. Find it and write the correct version.
The girl, who scored the goal, celebrated with her team.
The book who I borrowed was very interesting.
Made the discovery the scientist won an award.
The cat which it caught the mouse was very fast.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Sentences
Write five sentences of your own. Each sentence must contain a defining relative clause. Use each of these relative pronouns at least once: who, which, that.
Try to use vocabulary words from Unit 4 in your sentences (e.g., remarkable, mysterious, reliable, hasty, dreadful, grateful).
Exercise 5: Story Sentences
Go back to the story "The Hasty Route" (in your Story file). Find and copy out three sentences that contain defining relative clauses. Underline the clause in each one.
Answer Key
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blank
that (or which)
who (or that)
that (or which)
who (or that)
which (or that)
who (or that)
that (or which)
which (or that)
Exercise 2: Combine the Sentences
The boy who trains every morning won the race.
The house that stands on the hill is very old.
The teacher who teaches us maths is strict but fair.
The river which flows through our town is very wide.
The man who was wearing a blue jacket helped us find the way.
The legend that was told by the elders teaches us about patience.
Exercise 3: Spot the Mistake
Remove the commas: The girl who scored the goal celebrated with her team.
Change "who" to "which" or "that": The book which I borrowed was very interesting.
Move the clause after the noun: The scientist who made the discovery won an award.
Remove the extra "it": The cat which caught the mouse was very fast.
Summary

A defining relative clause gives essential information about a noun. Use who for people, which for things, and that for either. Place the clause right after the noun, and do not use commas. Keep practising, and soon these clauses will flow naturally in your writing!

paragraphs: Unit 4: Telling Timeless Tales
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

In this unit you will explore myths and legends — the timeless tales that people have told for hundreds and even thousands of years. Myths are ancient stories that explain why things in nature happen (such as why the sun rises or why the sea is salty). They often involve gods, spirits, or supernatural beings. Legends are stories loosely based on real events or real people from history, but with unlikely or exaggerated details added over time. Both myths and legends have been passed down through oral tradition, meaning people told them aloud long before anyone wrote them down.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain the difference between a myth and a legend.
Identify the key features of classic tales: oral tradition, timeless settings, moral or explanatory purpose, and larger-than-life characters.
Use defining relative clauses to add essential information to your sentences.
Use the present continuous tense to talk about future plans and arrangements.
Compare things using comparative adjectives (e.g., more reliable, hotter, wiser).
Identify and use opposites (antonyms) such as dreadful/delightful and miserable/prosperous.
Match verbs with nouns correctly in collocations (e.g., make a decision, face an outcome).
Write your own short legend with a clear beginning, middle and end.
Read, discuss and retell myths and legends with expression and detail.
Key Vocabulary
Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find all 25 vocabulary words in your separate Vocabulary file, but these are the ones to learn first.
Open your Vocabulary file to study the full list of 25 words with definitions, examples and practice activities.
Reading: The Legend of the Whispering Dunes
Read the following legend carefully. It is set in the deserts of East Africa. As you read, look for features of a legend: a hero, a quest, unlikely events and a lesson at the end.

Long ago, on the edge of the great Ogaden desert, there lived a girl named Amina who was known for her remarkable memory. The elders said she could remember every word of every story she had ever heard. One dry season, the wells that supplied the village began to fail, one after another. The phenomenon puzzled everyone. Where had the water gone?

Amina remembered a legend her grandmother had told her. It spoke of a hidden spring that lay beneath the Whispering Dunes — sand hills that made a low humming sound when the wind blew across them. The spring, the story said, would reveal itself only to someone who was patient and reliable, someone who could follow the sound without making a hasty choice.
"I will go," Amina told the elders. Her cousin Yusuf, who was anxious but brave, joined her. Together they set off on a quest across the burning sand.

By the second day, the heat was dreadful. Yusuf spotted a pool of blue water shimmering in the distance. "Let us run to it!" he cried. But Amina shook her head. "That is a mirage — a trick of the light. The spring we seek is the one that whispers. Listen." Yusuf was reluctant to pass up the blue shimmer, but he trusted Amina.

On the third morning, Amina heard a faint hum. The dunes ahead were singing. She walked slowly, placing each foot with care, following the sound. At last she reached a low hollow between two tall dunes. The sand there was dark and damp. Amina knelt and dug with her hands. Cool, clear water bubbled up — the hidden spring!
Yusuf was grateful beyond words. They filled every goatskin bag and hurried back to the village. The people were delightful in their celebrations, and the elders called Amina's deed honorable.

From that day, the village marked the path to the Whispering Dunes with stones. And the elders added a new lesson to the old legend: "A reliable heart that listens carefully will always find what a hasty foot runs past."
The legend of the Whispering Dunes is still told today, around fires on cool desert evenings, reminding every generation that patience and trust are worth more than speed.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage to find the evidence.
Why was Amina well known in her village?
What phenomenon puzzled the villagers at the start of the story?
What was the condition for the hidden spring to reveal itself?
Why did Yusuf want to run towards the shimmering water? Why did Amina stop him?
How did Amina find the real spring?
What lesson did the elders add to the legend at the end?
Deeper Thinking
Is this story a myth or a legend? Give two reasons for your answer.
What character qualities does Amina show? List at least three adjectives and support each with evidence from the text.
Why do you think stories like this were told aloud before being written down?
Think of a natural phenomenon you have experienced (wind, rain, thunder, drought). How might an ancient community have explained it through a story?
Myths and Legends: What Is the Difference?
Myths and legends are both types of classic tales, but they are not the same. Let us look at the differences and the things they share.
Features of a Myth
Explains a natural phenomenon (why volcanoes erupt, why seasons change, etc.).
Involves gods, spirits or supernatural beings.
Is not based on fact — it is entirely imaginary.
Often has a moral or a warning for people.
Set in a timeless, unspecific past ("Long, long ago...").
Features of a Legend
Is loosely based on history or real events, but exaggerated over time.
Features a hero or heroine who goes on a quest or performs brave deeds.
Includes unlikely or exaggerated events that may not have really happened.
Often teaches a lesson about courage, loyalty, or wisdom.
May name a real place or a real historical period.
What They Share
Both come from the oral tradition — told aloud before being written.
Both use classic openings like "Long ago..." or "In ancient times...".
Both have larger-than-life characters who do extraordinary things.
Both carry a moral or message for the listener.
Both use vivid, descriptive language to bring the story to life.

When you read a classic tale, ask yourself: Does it explain a natural event? (If yes, it is probably a myth.) Does it feature a human hero on a quest? (If yes, it is probably a legend.) Some stories mix features of both!
Grammar Focus: Defining Relative Clauses
A defining relative clause gives essential information about a noun. Without it, we would not know which person, place or thing the sentence is about. Defining relative clauses do not use commas.
Relative Pronouns
who — for people. Example: The girl who found the spring saved the village.
which — for things. Example: The spring which lay beneath the dunes was hidden.
that — for people or things. Example: The path that Amina followed was safe.
How to Build a Defining Relative Clause
Find the noun you want to describe (e.g., the explorer).
Choose the right relative pronoun: who for people, which for things, that for either.
Add a clause that gives essential information (e.g., who made the discovery).
Put the clause right after the noun: The explorer who made the discovery shared the map.
Important Rule
A defining relative clause has no commas around it. The information inside it is essential — if you remove it, the meaning of the sentence changes or becomes unclear.
Correct: The boy who lives next door is my friend.
This tells us which boy we mean. If we remove "who lives next door", we do not know which boy the sentence is about.
Memory Trick: The 3 W’s
Remember: Who for people, Which for things, That for either. Think of it as picking the right key for the right door!
Your separate Grammar file has a full lesson with more examples, exercises and an answer key. Work through it after this section.
Grammar Practice
Exercise 1: Choose the Right Relative Pronoun
Fill in each blank with who, which or that.
The teacher ______ told us the legend was very animated.
The book ______ I borrowed from the library is about ancient myths.
The traveller ______ crossed the desert alone was brave.
The well ______ they found had carvings on its stones.
The children ______ listened carefully remembered the whole story.
The storm ______ delayed us turned out to be a blessing.
Exercise 2: Combine the Sentences
Join each pair of sentences into one sentence using a defining relative clause.

The girl saved the village. She found the hidden spring.
(Hint: The girl who ...)

The map was old. It showed the route to the dunes.
(Hint: The map that / which ...)

The elders told many stories. They were the wisest people in the village.
(Hint: The elders who ...)

The legend teaches patience. It has been told for generations.
(Hint: The legend that ...)
Exercise 3: Write Your Own
Write three sentences of your own, each containing a defining relative clause. Use each of these relative pronouns once: who, which, that.
Answer Key: Exercise 1
who
which (or that)
who (or that)
which (or that)
who (or that)
that (or which)
Answer Key: Exercise 2 (Sample Answers)
The girl who found the hidden spring saved the village.
The map that showed the route to the dunes was old.
The elders who were the wisest people in the village told many stories.
The legend that has been told for generations teaches patience.
Writing Workshop: Write Your Own Legend

Now it is your turn to become a storyteller! You are going to write your own short legend. A legend is a story that is loosely based on real events or places, features a hero or heroine, includes unlikely or exaggerated events, and teaches a lesson.
Step 1: Plan Your Legend
Before you start writing, answer these planning questions:
Setting: Where and when does your legend take place? (Think of a real place you know — a desert, a coast, a mountain, a city.)
Hero/Heroine: Who is the main character? What makes them special or admirable?
Quest or Problem: What challenge does the hero face? What are they searching for or trying to achieve?
Unlikely Event: What surprising, exaggerated or unlikely thing happens during the story?
Lesson/Moral: What message or wisdom does the legend teach?
Step 2: Organise Your Story
Use this simple structure:
Beginning: Introduce the setting, the hero, and the problem or quest. Start with a classic opening like "Long ago..." or "In the days when..."
Middle: Show the hero facing challenges. Include dialogue, description and at least one unlikely event. Use defining relative clauses to add detail.
End: Resolve the quest. Show what the hero learned. State the lesson or moral.
Step 3: Write Your First Draft
Write between 250 and 400 words. Try to include:
At least two defining relative clauses (using who, which or that).
At least five vocabulary words from this unit (e.g., hasty, quest, remarkable, reliable, dreadful, grateful).
Vivid descriptive language — use adjectives and adverbs to paint pictures.
At least two lines of dialogue between characters.
A clear moral or lesson at the end.
Step 4: Edit and Improve
Read your legend out loud. Check for these things:
Does every sentence make sense?
Have you used standard English (correct grammar, spelling, punctuation)?
Are your defining relative clauses in the right place, with no commas around them?
Does your dialogue use speech marks correctly?
Is there a clear beginning, middle and end?
Does the legend teach a lesson?
Writing Model
Here is a short example to show you the kind of legend you might write:
The Shepherd Who Tamed the Wind
Long ago, in the highlands near Harar, there lived a shepherd named Daahir who was reliable and kind. Every morning he led his flock to the green meadows that lay beyond the rocky ridge.
One season, a dreadful wind began to blow without stopping. The grass dried, the wells shrank, and the animals grew thin. The elders said it was hopeless, but Daahir refused to give up.

He set off on a quest to find the cave where the wind was born. After three days, he reached a cave that hummed and roared. Inside, he found a bird with silver feathers. "Why do you blow so hard?" he asked.
"I am lonely," the bird replied. "No one visits me."
Daahir promised to visit the bird every full moon. In return, the bird calmed the wind. The grass grew green again, and the village was grateful beyond words.
The elders called Daahir honorable, and they added this lesson to their stories: "A kind word can calm the fiercest storm."
Notice how this model uses a classic opening, a hero on a quest, an unlikely event (a talking bird), dialogue, defining relative clauses, vocabulary words, and a moral at the end.
Extra Grammar: Present Continuous for Future Plans
When we talk about something that is already planned or arranged for the future, we often use the present continuous tense (am/is/are + verb-ing) with a future time expression.
Examples
We're documenting the site tomorrow.
They're presenting the report next week.
I'm visiting the museum on Saturday.
Practice
Rewrite each sentence using the present continuous for future plans.
We plan to explore the caves next Friday. → We ______ the caves next Friday.
She plans to meet the scientist on Monday. → She ______ the scientist on Monday.
They plan to travel to the coast this weekend. → They ______ to the coast this weekend.
Answers
We are exploring the caves next Friday.
She is meeting the scientist on Monday.
They are travelling to the coast this weekend.
Extra Grammar: Comparatives and Opposites
Comparative Adjectives

We use comparative adjectives to compare two things. For short adjectives (one syllable), add -er (e.g., hot → hotter). For longer adjectives (two or more syllables), put more before the adjective (e.g., reliable → more reliable).
Route A was more comfortable than Route B.
The second day was hotter than the first.
Fatoumata's plan was wiser than Idris's plan.
Opposites (Antonyms)
Opposites are words with completely different meanings. Knowing opposites helps you express ideas more clearly.
Practice: Opposites
Write the opposite of each word.
dreadful → ______
miserable → ______
hasty → ______
reluctant → ______
grateful → ______
Answers
delightful
prosperous
careful
eager
irritable (opposite in context)
Listening and Speaking
Activity 1: Retell a Legend
Choose one of the legends you have read in this unit ("The Legend of the Whispering Dunes", "The Hasty Route" from your Story file, or your own legend). Practise retelling it aloud.
When you retell the legend, try to:
Use a classic opening ("Long ago..." or "In ancient times...").
Change your voice for different characters.
Use expression and body language to make the story exciting.
Include at least one defining relative clause when you speak.
End with the moral or lesson of the story.
Record yourself if you can, then listen back. Did you speak clearly? Did you use expression? Ask your AI tutor for feedback.
Activity 2: Myth or Legend?
Your AI tutor will tell you a short classic tale. Your job is to decide: is it a myth or a legend? Explain your answer using the features you have learned.
Activity 3: Group Discussion (or Partner Talk)
Discuss these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor:
Why do you think people create legends about real places and real people?
What makes a story last for hundreds of years?
If you could create a legend about your own town or city, what would it be about?
Do you think myths are just made-up stories, or do they teach us something important?
Activity 4: Dramatic Reading
Pick a scene from "The Hasty Route" (your Story file) that has dialogue between two or more characters. Read it aloud with a partner or record yourself doing both voices. Focus on:
Using different tones for different characters (e.g., Fatoumata is calm and thoughtful; Idris is energetic and sometimes hasty).
Pausing at commas and full stops for natural rhythm.
Adding feeling to words like "dreadful", "grateful" and "remarkable".
Extra Practice: Matching Verbs with Nouns
In English, some verbs work best with specific nouns. These are called collocations. Using the right verb-noun pair makes your English sound natural.
Common Collocations from This Unit
Practice: Match the Verb to the Noun
Draw a line or write the correct verb next to each noun.
______ a justifiable decision (make / do / take)
______ a dreadful outcome (face / give / put)
______ a delightful experience (share / throw / bring)
______ a remarkable discovery (find / make / do)
______ a profitable result (achieve / run / play)
Answers
make a justifiable decision
face a dreadful outcome
share a delightful experience
make a remarkable discovery
achieve a profitable result
Check Your Learning
Use this checklist to see how well you have understood Unit 4. Tick each statement that you can do confidently. If you cannot tick something, go back and review that section.
I can explain the difference between a myth and a legend.
I can list at least three features of classic tales (oral tradition, timeless settings, larger-than-life characters, moral purpose).
I can use defining relative clauses with who, which and that correctly.
I know that defining relative clauses have no commas around them.
I can use the present continuous tense to talk about future plans.
I can form comparative adjectives for both short and long adjectives.
I can identify opposites (antonyms) for key vocabulary words.
I can match verbs with nouns in common collocations.
I have written my own legend with a hero, a quest, an unlikely event and a moral.
I can retell a myth or legend aloud with expression and detail.
I have used at least ten vocabulary words from this unit in my own writing or speaking.
Well Done!

You have completed Unit 4: Telling Timeless Tales. You have explored the world of myths and legends, learned to use defining relative clauses, expanded your vocabulary, and written your own legend. These are timeless skills — just like the tales you have been reading. Keep telling stories, because every great writer starts as a great storyteller.

paragraphs: The Hasty Route
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Unit 4 Story
About the Story
Theme: Exploration, teamwork and discovery
Genre: Adventure fiction with character growth
Setting: A desert expedition in Mauritania
Moral: Thoughtful leadership and dependable teamwork matter more than speed in reaching a goal
The Hasty Route

In the hot, golden deserts of Mauritania, a team of young explorers prepared for a journey across the Adrar Plateau. They were led by twelve-year-old Fatoumata, a careful thinker, and her cousin Idris, who was brave — but often made hasty decisions.

“This is a map of the route we’re taking,” Fatoumata said as she showed the group their plan. “We’re documenting sand-dwelling animals and ancient wells. We’re also testing our new water-purifying backpack.”
Idris waved a hand. “We should skip the first stop. It’s slower. Let’s head straight to the mountain site.”
Fatoumata looked at him. “That’s a reasonable idea if the weather holds. But the guide said sandstorms are common today.”
“I’m not reluctant to take risks,” Idris said forcefully. “Let’s move quickly before it gets hotter!”
The group hesitated. Fatoumata studied the clouds. “We’re documenting the full route tomorrow. Today’s walk is only a check.”

By noon, the sun was high, and the wind picked up. The group had reached a small ridge where the air grew cooler and surprisingly pleasant. They ate lunch under a large rock, sharing stories and laughing.
“I’m grateful we packed shade cloths,” said Rami, the team’s youngest.
“I’m grateful I listened to Fatoumata,” added Yusef. “This stop is cooler than the other side.”
Suddenly, a loud sound rolled across the dunes — wind, fast and heavy.
“It’s a sandstorm!” shouted Idris.
They scrambled to cover their supplies and crouch under the cloths. The sand hit them like needles.
“It’s dreadful,” coughed Rami. “We should’ve stayed back.”
“No,” said Fatoumata. “We just need to wait it out.”
After twenty minutes, the wind died down. The world around them looked different. Their footprints were gone, and so was the second map — blown away.
“We’re off-track now,” said Idris, brushing sand off his pack. “Maybe this is my fault.”
Fatoumata didn’t blame him. “It was a justifiable mistake. You wanted to save time. But now we need to be reliable and fix it.”
They checked their compass and followed shadows to rejoin the path.
Suddenly, they noticed a round stone structure ahead.
“What’s that?” Rami asked.
Fatoumata smiled. “It’s an ancient well. We found it!”
The group approached slowly. Around the well were carvings of animals, maps, and a mysterious pattern that glowed slightly in the sun.
Fatoumata knelt down. “These are symbols used by early Berber traders. This is a remarkable find!”
“Do you think they camped here?” Idris asked.
“Possibly,” Fatoumata said. “There’s evidence of firestones and crushed herbs. Maybe it was once a stop on a trading route.”
Yusef leaned forward. “We should collect a soil sample. That could help us prove how old this well is.”
Rami scribbled notes. “This is becoming more than a school project — it feels like a real expedition.”
Fatoumata grinned. “It is. And we’re documenting history.”
The group took photos, sketched the site, and filtered some well water.
“Let’s label this site: ‘Desert Point 4,’” said Fatoumata. “We’re documenting this tomorrow, right after we revisit the southern path.”
Rami looked curious. “We’re returning?”
“Yes,” Fatoumata said. “We’re presenting the full trail report to the science club next week.”
“I’m still feeling a bit anxious about our return walk,” said Idris.
“I’ll lead that part,” said Yusef. “It’ll be more pleasant now that we know the path.”
Back at camp that night, they compared the two paths. Fatoumata wrote:
“Route A was more comfortable, less dangerous, and full of ancient clues. Route B was shorter but hotter and riskier. The dreadful wind made it miserable.”
Idris added to the log:
“I was too forceful in my choice. Fatoumata’s slower plan was wiser. Her idea to explore carefully led to a profitable find.”
Later, around the fire, they sipped warm tea. Rami asked, “Do you think we’ll get chosen to present at the regional fair?”
“I think we’ve got a good chance,” Fatoumata replied. “Our site is unique, and we worked well together.”
Idris nodded. “We disagreed, but we kept moving. That’s what made it honorable.”
The next day, the team began preparing their presentation.
“We’re writing about the explorers who found Desert Point 4,” said Fatoumata.
“And the decisions that taught them patience,” Idris added.
They printed photos, diagrams, and their annotated map. The report included a defining clause:
“The storm that delayed us led to a well which hadn’t been recorded before.”
At the school fair, judges asked, “Was the storm a mistake?”
“No,” Fatoumata replied. “It was part of the journey.”
“And would you do it again?” they asked.
“Only with this team,” she smiled. “They’re the most faithful, most honorable crew imaginable.”
Vocabulary Spotlight
The story uses all 25 vocabulary words from Unit 4. Here are the ones to pay special attention to:
Grammar in the Story
Defining Relative Clauses
Find these examples in the story:
"The storm that delayed us led to a well which hadn’t been recorded before."
"...the explorers who found Desert Point 4..."
"...the decisions that taught them patience..."
Present Continuous for Future Plans
The characters use the present continuous to talk about things already arranged:
"We’re documenting the full route tomorrow."
"We’re presenting the full trail report next week."
"We’re writing about the explorers who found Desert Point 4."
Comparatives and Opposites
Look at how the story compares Route A and Route B:
Route A was more comfortable and less dangerous than Route B.
Opposites used: dreadful / pleasant, miserable / delightful, hasty / thoughtful, forceful / gentle.
Reflection Questions
After reading the story, think about these questions and write your answers in full sentences.
Comprehension
Where does the story take place? Describe the setting.
Who are the two main characters, and how are they different?
Why did Idris want to skip the first stop?
What happened during the sandstorm?
What did the group discover after the storm?
Why was the discovery at the well considered remarkable?
Character and Theme
How does Fatoumata show that she is a good leader? Give two examples from the story.
How does Idris change during the story? What does he learn?
The moral of the story is: "Thoughtful leadership and dependable teamwork matter more than speed in reaching a goal." Do you agree? Why or why not?
Which character do you relate to more — Fatoumata or Idris? Explain your answer.
Language and Writing
Find three examples of vocabulary words used in dialogue. Write them down.
Find the defining relative clause in the report the team wrote. Copy it out.
How does the author show the difference between Route A and Route B? What language techniques are used?
If you could add one more scene to this story, what would happen and why?

Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the 25 important words for Unit 4. They all appear in your story, "The Hasty Route". Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, then do the quick practice at the end of the group. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
How to learn a hard word: break it into syllables, look for a smaller word you already know inside it, and use the other words in the sentence (the context) to guess the meaning before you check.
Group 1: Feelings and Reactions
Practise These Words
Think of a time you felt anxious. Write one sentence about it.
What is the difference between being furious and being irritable? Explain in your own words.
Write a sentence using both grateful and miserable as opposites.
Group 2: Describing Quality and Character
Practise These Words
Name something you find delightful and something you find dreadful. Write one sentence for each.
Use the word mysterious to describe a place you have seen or imagined.
What is the difference between pleasant and glorious? Which is stronger?
Group 3: Behaviour and Strength
Practise These Words
Describe someone you know who is reliable. What do they do that shows this?
Can happiness be contagious? Write two sentences explaining your answer.
Write a sentence using faithful and honorable together.
Group 4: Possibility and Judgement
Practise These Words
What is the difference between justifiable and reasonable? Write a sentence for each.
Think of something that is intolerable to you. Write a sentence explaining why.
If a decision leads to a profitable result, is it always a good decision? Explain.
Vocabulary Activities
Activity 1: Word Sort
Sort the 25 vocabulary words into these categories. Some words might fit in more than one category — that is fine, just explain your reason.
Positive feelings or qualities (e.g., grateful, delightful, ...)
Negative feelings or qualities (e.g., dreadful, miserable, ...)
Character and behaviour (e.g., reliable, faithful, ...)
Judgement and possibility (e.g., justifiable, reasonable, ...)
Activity 2: Opposites Match
Match each word on the left with its opposite on the right.
Answers: 1-c, 2-a, 3-b, 4-d, 5-e
Activity 3: Fill in the Story
Complete this short paragraph using words from the vocabulary list.

The explorers set off on a ______ (1) morning, feeling ______ (2) about the journey ahead. When a ______ (3) storm hit, they became ______ (4). But their leader was ______ (5) and calm. She made a ______ (6) decision to shelter under a rock. After the storm, they made a ______ (7) discovery that made the whole trip ______ (8).
Suggested answers: (1) pleasant, (2) anxious, (3) dreadful, (4) miserable, (5) reliable, (6) reasonable, (7) remarkable, (8) profitable
Activity 4: Vocabulary in Sentences
Choose any ten words from the full list. Write one original sentence for each word. Try to connect your sentences to the theme of myths, legends and adventure.
Activity 5: Word Detective
Many of the vocabulary words share common suffixes (word endings). Look at these suffixes and sort the words:
-ful (meaning "full of"): grateful, dreadful, delightful, forceful, faithful
-ous (meaning "having the quality of"): furious, mysterious, glorious, contagious, anxious, prosperous
-able / -ible (meaning "can be"): imaginable, justifiable, profitable, reasonable, intolerable, reliable, honorable, miserable, remarkable
-ant (meaning "tending to"): pleasant, reluctant, significant
Look at each suffix group. Can you think of one more word that ends with the same suffix? Write your new words and their meanings.
Full Word List at a Glance

paragraphs: Grammar — Unit 5: Tell Me How
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Zero Conditional

This grammar file focuses on the Zero Conditional — the main grammar target for Unit 5. Read the explanation, study the examples, complete the exercises, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
What is the Zero Conditional?

The zero conditional is a type of conditional sentence that we use to talk about things that are always true. These include scientific facts, rules of nature, and general truths that happen every time a certain condition is met.
We call it the “zero” conditional because there is zero chance that the result will not happen. If the condition is true, the result is always true.
Why Does It Matter?
The zero conditional is especially useful for:
Instructional writing: When you write steps and explain what happens if you follow them.
Scientific explanations: When you describe cause and effect in nature.
Everyday facts: When you state something that is always true.
Rules and warnings: When you explain what happens if someone does (or does not do) something.
How to Form the Zero Conditional
The Pattern
Important rules to remember:
Both clauses use the present simple tense.
You can put the if-clause first or the main clause first. When the if-clause comes first, put a comma after it. When the main clause comes first, you do not need a comma.
You can use when instead of if with the same meaning: When you heat water, it boils.
Reversed Order
These two sentences mean the same thing:
If you mix red and blue, you get purple. (if-clause first, comma needed)
You get purple if you mix red and blue. (main clause first, no comma)
Examples
Scientific Facts
If you heat ice, it melts.
If you drop something, it falls.
If water reaches 100 degrees Celsius, it boils.
If the temperature drops below zero, water freezes.
If you mix vinegar and baking soda, it fizzes.
Everyday Life
If you press the button, the light turns on.
If you do not eat, you feel hungry.
If it rains, the ground gets wet.
If you practise every day, you improve.
If you add sugar to tea, it tastes sweet.
Instructions and Procedures
If you stir the mixture too quickly, it splashes.
If you leave bread in the sun, it dries out.
If you follow the steps carefully, you get good results.
If the jar is not clean, the experiment fails.
If you use warm water, the salt dissolves faster.
From the Story
Can you find these zero conditional sentences in the story “The Memory Wall”?
“If you combine honesty with emotion, it’ll be worth reading.”
“If you feel something important, write it.”
“If we combine stories and voices, we grow stronger.”
“If you navigate a hard feeling, that’s a big thing.”
“If you listen carefully, people say things that stay with you.”
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Using 'will' in the zero conditional
Incorrect: If you heat water, it will boil.
Correct: If you heat water, it boils.

Explanation: The zero conditional talks about facts that are always true. We do not need 'will' because there is no uncertainty. Using 'will' changes the sentence to the first conditional, which is about possible future events, not general truths.
Mistake 2: Using the past tense
Incorrect: If you heated water, it boils.
Correct: If you heat water, it boils.
Explanation: The zero conditional uses the present simple in both parts. Do not use the past tense.
Mistake 3: Forgetting the comma
Incorrect: If you drop a glass it breaks.
Correct: If you drop a glass, it breaks.
Explanation: When the if-clause comes first, always put a comma before the main clause.
Mistake 4: Confusing zero and first conditional
Zero conditional (always true): If you heat water, it boils.
First conditional (possible future): If you heat the water, it will be ready for tea.
Tip: Ask yourself: Is this always true, or is it about one specific situation in the future? If it is always true, use the zero conditional (present simple in both parts).
Memory Tip
Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets:
If you ______ (mix) flour and water, you ______ (make) dough.
If it ______ (rain) heavily, the river ______ (flood).
If you ______ (not sleep) enough, you ______ (feel) tired.
If the alarm ______ (go) off, we ______ (leave) the building.
If you ______ (combine) sugar and butter, the mixture ______ (become) smooth.
If you ______ (not wear) a coat, you ______ (get) cold.
If we ______ (work) together, we ______ (finish) faster.
If you ______ (press) this button, the door ______ (open).
Exercise 2: Match the Halves
Draw a line to match each if-clause with the correct main clause:
If you water the plants,  ...  a) it melts.
If you heat ice,  ...  b) they grow.
If the sun shines,  ...  c) you get purple.
If you mix red and blue,  ...  d) the room gets bright.
Exercise 3: Rewrite with the Zero Conditional
Rewrite each sentence as a zero conditional:
Water boils at 100 degrees. → If you heat water to 100 degrees, ______.
Plants die without water. → If you ______, ______.
Metal expands when it gets hot. → If ______, ______.
People get sunburnt without sunscreen. → If ______, ______.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own
Write five zero conditional sentences of your own. Try to write about different topics: nature, cooking, school, health, and daily life.
Exercise 5: Spot the Errors
Each sentence has one mistake. Rewrite it correctly:
If you will add salt to water, it dissolves.
If water freezed, it becomes ice.
If you touched fire it burns you.
If you mix green and yellow you will get a lighter shade.
If you doesn't study, you fails the test.
Exercise 6: Zero or First Conditional?
Read each sentence. Write ZC if it is a zero conditional (always true) or FC if it is a first conditional (possible future):
If it rains tomorrow, I will take an umbrella.
If it rains, the ground gets wet.
If you study hard, you will pass the exam.
If you heat metal, it expands.
If we leave now, we will arrive on time.
Answer Key
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
mix / make
rains / floods
do not sleep / feel
goes / leave
combine / becomes
do not wear / get
work / finish
press / opens
Exercise 2: Match the Halves
If you water the plants, they grow. (b)
If you heat ice, it melts. (a)
If the sun shines, the room gets bright. (d)
If you mix red and blue, you get purple. (c)
Exercise 3: Rewrite
If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.
If you do not water plants, they die.
If metal gets hot, it expands.
If people do not use sunscreen, they get sunburnt.
Exercise 5: Spot the Errors
If you add salt to water, it dissolves. (Remove 'will'.)
If water freezes, it becomes ice. (freezes, not freezed.)
If you touch fire, it burns you. (touch, not touched; add comma.)
If you mix green and yellow, you get a lighter shade. (Remove 'will'.)
If you don't study, you fail the test. (don't, not doesn't; fail, not fails.)
Exercise 6: Zero or First?
1. FC   2. ZC   3. FC   4. ZC   5. FC
tables:
📝  Structure
If + subject + present simple verb, subject + present simple verb.

If-clause (condition) + Main clause (result)
If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.
If the sun sets, it gets dark.

💡  How to Remember
Zero = always. If the condition happens, the result ALWAYS follows.
Both halves use the PRESENT SIMPLE.
Think of it as a law of nature: If the sun rises, a new day begins.
Quick formula: If + present simple, present simple = ALWAYS TRUE.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers?
Can you explain the difference between the zero and first conditional again?
Can you give me five more zero conditional sentences to practise?
Can you help me find zero conditional sentences in the story?

paragraphs: Unit 5: Tell Me How
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit, you will explore the world of instructional and procedural writing. Procedural texts are everywhere around us. They tell us how to cook a meal, how to play a game, how to build something, and how to get from one place to another. Every time you follow a recipe, read a science experiment, or look at the instructions for a board game, you are reading a procedural text. By the end of this unit, you will be able to read, understand, and write your own clear and well-organised instructions. You will also learn an important grammar pattern called the zero conditional, which is used to describe things that are always true. This unit will strengthen your reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills, all through the lens of instructions and how things work.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand procedural and instructional texts, identifying their key features and structure.
Recognise and use imperative verbs (command words) such as cut, stir, place, and measure in your own writing.
Identify and use time connectives (first, next, then, after that, finally) to sequence steps clearly.
Understand the purpose and layout of a procedural text, including titles, materials lists, numbered steps, and safety tips.
Use the zero conditional (If + present simple, present simple) to express facts and general truths.
Write a complete set of instructions with a clear title, a materials or equipment list, numbered steps, and helpful tips.
Give and follow spoken instructions clearly and accurately in pair and group activities.
Use prepositions of position (in, on, under, beside, between, above) to describe where things are placed.
Evaluate and improve instructional writing by checking for clarity, correct sequencing, and appropriate vocabulary.
Use new vocabulary words accurately in both speech and writing, including words such as combine, indicate, navigate, and emphasize.
Key Vocabulary

The words below will appear throughout this unit. Study them carefully so that you can recognise them in the reading passage, use them in your writing, and feel confident saying them aloud. For each word, read the definition and the example sentence. Try to use at least five of these words in your own sentences before you move on.
Reading: How to Make Traditional Somali Bariis

The passage below is a procedural text. A procedural text gives you step-by-step instructions on how to do or make something. As you read, pay close attention to the structure of the text: the title, the introduction, the materials list, the numbered steps, and the tips at the end. Also notice the imperative verbs (command words) at the start of many sentences, and the time connectives that link the steps in order. Read the passage at least twice before answering the questions that follow.
How to Make Traditional Somali Bariis (Spiced Rice)

Bariis is one of the most beloved dishes in Somali cooking. It is a fragrant, golden-coloured rice that is seasoned with a blend of spices and often served alongside meat, vegetables, or banana. Families across Somalia and the wider East African region have been making bariis for many generations, and the recipe has been passed down from grandmothers to mothers to daughters. Learning how to make bariis is not just about cooking a meal. It is about connecting with your heritage and sharing something special with the people you love. The instructions below will guide you through the process step by step, so that even if this is your first time in the kitchen, you can create a delicious dish that your whole family will enjoy.
What You Will Need (Ingredients and Equipment)

Before you begin cooking, make sure you have gathered all of the following items. Having everything ready before you start is an important part of following any set of instructions. In cooking, this preparation step is called mise en place, a French phrase that means putting everything in its place.
2 cups of basmati rice
3 cups of water
3 tablespoons of vegetable oil or ghee
1 small onion, finely chopped
2 cloves of garlic, crushed
1 teaspoon of cumin
1 teaspoon of cardamom
Half a teaspoon of turmeric (this gives the rice its golden colour)
Half a teaspoon of cinnamon
1 teaspoon of salt (or to taste)
A medium-sized cooking pot with a lid
A wooden spoon for stirring
A sieve or colander for rinsing the rice
Steps

Rinse the rice. Place the two cups of basmati rice into a sieve or colander. Hold it under cold running water and gently swirl the rice with your fingers. Continue rinsing until the water runs clear. This removes the extra starch and helps the rice cook into separate, fluffy grains rather than a sticky lump. Set the rinsed rice aside in a bowl.

Soak the rice. Transfer the rinsed rice into a bowl and cover it with cold water. Allow it to soak for at least fifteen minutes. Soaking softens the grains so that they cook evenly and do not break apart. While the rice is soaking, you can prepare the other ingredients.

Heat the oil and cook the onion. Place the cooking pot on the stove over a medium heat. Add three tablespoons of vegetable oil or ghee. When the oil is hot, add the chopped onion. Stir the onion with a wooden spoon until it becomes soft and slightly golden, which usually takes about three to four minutes. Be careful not to let the onion burn, as this will give the rice a bitter taste.

Add the garlic and spices. Next, add the crushed garlic to the pot and stir for about thirty seconds until you can smell its fragrance. Then add the cumin, cardamom, turmeric, cinnamon, and salt. Stir everything together for one minute so that the spices combine with the oil and release their flavour. The mixture in the pot should look golden and smell wonderful.

Add the rice. Drain the soaked rice and add it to the pot. Stir gently so that every grain of rice is coated with the spiced oil. Continue stirring for about two minutes. This step is important because it helps each grain absorb the spice flavours before the water is added.

Pour in the water. Carefully pour three cups of water into the pot. Stir once to make sure the rice is spread evenly across the bottom of the pot. Turn the heat up to high and bring the water to a boil.

Cover and simmer. When the water begins to boil, reduce the heat to the lowest setting. Place the lid tightly on the pot. Allow the rice to simmer for about eighteen to twenty minutes. Do not lift the lid during this time, because the steam inside the pot is what cooks the rice. If you lift the lid, the steam escapes and the rice may not cook properly.

Rest and fluff. After twenty minutes, turn off the heat but leave the lid on the pot for another five minutes. This resting period allows the rice to finish steaming. Finally, remove the lid and use a fork to gently fluff the rice, separating the grains. Your bariis is now ready to serve.
Helpful Tips
If you prefer a richer flavour, use ghee instead of vegetable oil.
You can add a few whole cloves or a small stick of cinnamon to the pot for extra aroma.
Bariis is traditionally served on a large communal plate. Place the rice in the centre and arrange the meat or vegetables around it.
Always ask an adult for help when using the stove or handling hot pots and pans.
If the rice sticks to the bottom of the pot, the heat was too high. Next time, reduce the heat a little more during the simmering step.
Understanding the Text

Now that you have read the passage carefully, it is time to check your understanding. The questions below are divided into three levels. Literal questions ask you to find information that is stated directly in the text. Inferential questions ask you to think about what the text suggests or implies, even if it is not stated directly. Evaluative questions ask you to give your own opinion or make a judgement about the text. Answer each question in full sentences.
Level 1: Literal Questions (Finding Information in the Text)
What is bariis?
How many cups of rice does this recipe require?
Why do you rinse the rice before cooking it?
How long should the rice soak in water?
What spices are used in this recipe? List at least four.
What should you do after the water begins to boil?
How long does the rice need to simmer with the lid on?
Level 2: Inferential Questions (Reading Between the Lines)
Why do you think the passage says you should not lift the lid while the rice is simmering? What might happen if you do?
The passage says that learning to make bariis is about more than just cooking a meal. What do you think the author means by this?
Why do you think the instructions tell you to gather all your ingredients before you start cooking? What problems could arise if you do not?
The passage mentions that bariis has been passed down from generation to generation. What does this suggest about the importance of food in Somali culture?
Level 3: Evaluative Questions (Giving Your Opinion)
Do you think these instructions are easy to follow? Give reasons for your answer, referring to specific features of the text.
If you were writing these instructions for someone who has never cooked before, what extra information would you add?
Why is it important that instructional texts are written in a clear, step-by-step order? What might happen if the steps were jumbled up?
Text Features Analysis

Procedural texts have special features that make them different from stories or information texts. Go back to the reading passage and complete the following tasks. These will help you understand the tools that writers use when they write instructions.

Imperative verbs: Find and write down at least eight imperative verbs (command words) from the passage. Remember, an imperative verb tells you to do something. Examples include rinse, place, and stir. Write each verb and the sentence it appears in.

Time connectives: Find and list at least five time connectives from the passage. Time connectives are words or phrases that show the order of steps, such as first, next, then, after that, when, while, and finally. Write each one and explain where it appears in the instructions.

Structure: Draw a simple diagram or list showing the structure of this procedural text. Your diagram should include: the title, the introduction, the materials list, the numbered steps, and the tips section. Next to each part, write one sentence explaining its purpose.
Audience and purpose: Who do you think this text was written for? How can you tell? Think about the level of detail, the language used, and the safety reminders.
Tone and style: Is this text written in a formal or informal style? Find two examples from the text to support your answer.
Grammar Focus: The Zero Conditional

In this section, you will learn about an important grammar pattern called the zero conditional. The zero conditional is especially useful when you are writing instructions, because instructions often describe what happens as a result of doing something. Understanding this pattern will make your procedural writing clearer and more precise.
What Is the Zero Conditional?

The zero conditional is a sentence structure we use to talk about things that are always true. These are facts, scientific truths, or results that happen every single time a certain condition is met. When we use the zero conditional, we are not talking about possibilities or things that might happen. We are talking about things that definitely and reliably happen whenever the condition occurs. Think of the zero conditional as describing the rules of how the world works.

For example, think about what happens when you heat water. Every single time you heat water to one hundred degrees Celsius, it boils. This is not sometimes true or occasionally true. It is always true. That is why we use the zero conditional to describe it: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils.
The Pattern
The zero conditional follows a simple and consistent pattern. Both parts of the sentence use the present simple tense. Here is the formula:
If + subject + present simple verb, subject + present simple verb.

The first part of the sentence (the if-clause) describes the condition. The second part (the main clause or result clause) describes what always happens when that condition is true. You can also reverse the order of the two parts. When the if-clause comes second, you do not need a comma.
Here are examples of both word orders:
If you mix red and blue, you get purple. (if-clause first)
You get purple if you mix red and blue. (if-clause second, no comma needed)
Examples from Everyday Life

Let us look at many examples so that the pattern becomes completely clear. Notice how every example describes something that is always true, and notice how both parts of the sentence use the present simple tense.
If you heat ice, it melts.
If the sun sets, it gets dark.
If you do not water a plant, it dies.
If you mix yellow and blue, you get green.
If you touch fire, it burns your skin.
If it rains heavily, the streets flood.
If you add salt to food, it tastes saltier.
If you press this button, the machine starts.
If you study hard, you learn more.
If you drop a glass, it breaks.
Culturally Relevant Examples
Here are some zero conditional sentences that relate to your own experiences and culture. As you read them, think about whether each one describes something that is always true.
If you fast during Ramadan, you do not eat or drink during daylight hours.
If the adhan sounds, Muslims know it is time to pray.
If you add turmeric to rice, the rice turns golden.
If the monsoon rains come, the farms in the countryside get the water they need.
If you greet an elder respectfully, they feel honoured.
If you combine cumin and cardamom, the food smells fragrant.
If you read the Quran every day, your recitation improves.
If you share food with your neighbours, the community bonds grow stronger.
Zero Conditional vs. First Conditional

It is important to understand how the zero conditional is different from the first conditional, because learners sometimes mix them up. The key difference is about certainty. The zero conditional describes things that are always true and always happen. The first conditional describes things that are possible or likely but not guaranteed.
Compare these two sentences:
Zero conditional: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils. (This always happens. It is a scientific fact.)
First conditional: If you study tonight, you will do well on the test tomorrow. (This is possible and likely, but it is not guaranteed every single time.)

Notice the grammar difference as well. In the zero conditional, both parts use the present simple. In the first conditional, the if-clause uses the present simple, but the main clause uses will + base verb.
Here are more pairs so you can see the difference clearly:
Zero: If you drop an egg, it breaks. First: If you drop that egg, it will make a mess on the floor.
Zero: If plants do not get sunlight, they die. First: If we do not water the garden today, the flowers will probably wilt.
Zero: If you mix vinegar and baking soda, it fizzes. First: If you try this experiment, you will see something interesting.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Learners often make a few common errors when using the zero conditional. Read through these mistakes carefully so that you can avoid them in your own writing.

Mistake: Using will in the zero conditional. Incorrect: If you heat ice, it will melt. Correct: If you heat ice, it melts. Remember, the zero conditional uses present simple in both clauses because we are stating a fact, not a prediction.
Mistake: Using the past tense in the if-clause. Incorrect: If you heated water, it boils. Correct: If you heat water, it boils. Both parts must be in the present simple.

Mistake: Confusing zero and first conditional. If you are describing a general truth or fact that is always true, use the zero conditional (present simple in both parts). If you are describing a specific future possibility, use the first conditional (present simple + will).
Mistake: Forgetting the comma when the if-clause comes first. Incorrect: If you add too much salt the food tastes bad. Correct: If you add too much salt, the food tastes bad.
Grammar Practice

Now it is time to practise what you have learned about the zero conditional. Work through each exercise carefully. When you have finished, check your answers using the answer key at the end of each exercise. If you get any answers wrong, go back and re-read the grammar section above before trying again.
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence using the correct present simple form of the verb in brackets. Remember, zero conditional sentences use the present simple tense in both parts.
If you __________ (heat) butter, it __________ (melt).
If it __________ (rain), the ground __________ (get) wet.
If you __________ (not water) plants, they __________ (die).
If you __________ (mix) flour and water, you __________ (get) dough.
If the temperature __________ (drop) below zero, water __________ (freeze).
If you __________ (add) lemon to tea, it __________ (change) colour.
If a person __________ (not sleep) enough, they __________ (feel) tired.
If you __________ (press) the green button, the door __________ (open).
Answer Key for Exercise 1:
heat / melts
rains / gets
do not water / die
mix / get
drops / freezes
add / changes
does not sleep / feel
press / opens
Exercise 2: Match the Halves
Draw a line to match each if-clause on the left with the correct result clause on the right. Write out the complete sentences in your notebook.
If-clauses:
If you combine sugar and water,
If the alarm rings,
If you read every day,
If metal gets very hot,
If you do not eat breakfast,
Result clauses:
a) you feel hungry before lunch.
b) it expands.
c) your vocabulary improves.
d) everyone leaves the building.
e) you get syrup.
Answer Key for Exercise 2:
If you combine sugar and water, you get syrup. (e)
If the alarm rings, everyone leaves the building. (d)
If you read every day, your vocabulary improves. (c)
If metal gets very hot, it expands. (b)
If you do not eat breakfast, you feel hungry before lunch. (a)
Exercise 3: Spot the Errors
Each sentence below contains a mistake. Find the mistake, explain what is wrong, and rewrite the sentence correctly.
If you will drop a ball, it bounces.
If you heated water, it boils.
If it rain, the roads get slippery.
If you mix red and yellow you get orange.
If plants does not get light, they die.
Answer Key for Exercise 3:
Error: 'will drop' should be 'drop' (zero conditional uses present simple). Correct: If you drop a ball, it bounces.
Error: 'heated' is past tense; it should be 'heat' (present simple). Correct: If you heat water, it boils.
Error: 'rain' should be 'rains' (third person singular). Correct: If it rains, the roads get slippery.
Error: Missing comma after the if-clause. Correct: If you mix red and yellow, you get orange.
Error: 'does not get' should agree with the plural subject 'plants'; correct form is 'do not get'. Correct: If plants do not get light, they die.
Exercise 4: Rewrite Using the Zero Conditional
Rewrite each fact below as a zero conditional sentence. The first one has been done for you as an example.
Example: Fact: Heating chocolate causes it to melt. Zero conditional: If you heat chocolate, it melts.
Fact: Mixing blue and yellow paint produces green paint.
Fact: Not brushing your teeth causes cavities.
Fact: Adding too much chilli to food makes it very spicy.
Fact: Exercising regularly keeps your body healthy.
Fact: Leaving milk outside the fridge for too long causes it to go sour.
Answer Key for Exercise 4:
If you mix blue and yellow paint, you get green paint.
If you do not brush your teeth, you get cavities.
If you add too much chilli to food, it becomes very spicy.
If you exercise regularly, your body stays healthy.
If you leave milk outside the fridge for too long, it goes sour.
Exercise 5: Write Your Own

Write five of your own zero conditional sentences. Try to make them about topics that interest you or that relate to your daily life. At least two of your sentences should be about cooking, food, or household tasks. Check that both parts of each sentence use the present simple tense. When you have finished, swap your sentences with a partner and check each other's work, or ask your AI tutor to review them.
Writing Workshop: Write Your Own Instructions

In this section, you will plan and write your own procedural text. You have studied the features of instructional writing in the reading passage, and you have practised the zero conditional in the grammar section. Now it is time to bring all of these skills together and create your own set of clear, well-organised instructions. This is one of the most important tasks in this unit, so take your time and work through each stage carefully.
Step 1: Choose Your Topic

Select one of the following topics for your instructional writing. Choose the topic that you know the most about or that interests you the most. If you have a different idea for a set of instructions, you may discuss it with your teacher or AI tutor before starting.
How to Make a Traditional Somali Drink (such as shaah cadays, spiced tea with milk). Write clear instructions so that someone who has never made this drink before could follow your steps and succeed.

How to Organise and Set Up a Classroom Library Display. Write instructions for a group of students who want to create an attractive book display in their classroom. Include steps for sorting books, labelling sections, and arranging the display.

How to Prepare for and Lead a Morning Assembly at School. Write instructions for a student who has been asked to lead the school assembly for the first time. Include steps for planning, practising, and presenting.
Step 2: Plan Your Writing

Before you start writing, fill in the planning frame below. Good writers always plan before they write. Planning helps you organise your thoughts, decide on the order of your steps, and make sure you do not forget any important details.
Planning Frame:
Title: What will you call your instructions? (Write a clear, descriptive title.)
Purpose: Why would someone need these instructions? Who is your audience?
Materials or equipment: List everything the reader will need before they start.
Steps: Write a brief note for each step in the correct order. Aim for between six and ten steps.
Tips or warnings: Are there any helpful tips, safety warnings, or common mistakes you should mention?
Time connectives: Which time connectives will you use to link your steps? Write at least five (for example: first, next, then, after that, once, when, while, finally).
Step 3: Study the Model Opening Paragraph

Before you begin drafting, read this model opening paragraph. Notice how it introduces the topic, explains why the instructions are useful, and gives the reader an idea of what to expect. Your own opening paragraph should do the same things.

Model opening paragraph: Shaah cadays is a warm, sweet, and fragrant tea that is enjoyed in Somali homes every day. Whether it is served in the morning to start the day or in the evening when guests arrive, this spiced tea brings people together. Making shaah cadays is simple, but there are a few important steps that will help you get the flavour just right. In the instructions below, you will learn exactly how to prepare this beloved drink. You will need just a few basic ingredients, a small pot, and about fifteen minutes of your time. By the end, you will have a steaming cup of perfectly spiced tea to share with your family.
Step 4: Write Your First Draft
Using your completed planning frame, write your full set of instructions. Follow this structure to make sure your text is well organised and includes all of the key features of procedural writing.
Title: Write a clear title that tells the reader exactly what the instructions are for.
Opening paragraph: Introduce your topic. Explain what the reader will learn, why it is useful, and what they should expect.
Materials or equipment list: Use bullet points to list everything the reader needs.
Numbered steps: Write each step in order using imperative verbs at the start (such as gather, place, stir, arrange, practise). Use time connectives to link the steps together.
Tips or warnings section: Add any helpful advice, safety reminders, or notes about common mistakes at the end.
Closing sentence: End with an encouraging sentence that tells the reader they have completed the task.
Step 5: Check Your Work

When you have finished your first draft, use the checklist below to review your writing. Tick each item to make sure you have included it. If you have missed something, go back and add it before writing your final copy.
My instructions have a clear and descriptive title.
I have written an opening paragraph that introduces the topic and explains its purpose.
I have included a materials or equipment list.
My steps are numbered and in the correct order.
I have used imperative verbs at the start of my steps.
I have used at least five different time connectives to link my steps.
I have used at least three vocabulary words from this unit.
I have included a tips or warnings section.
My sentences are clear and easy to understand.
I have checked my spelling, punctuation, and grammar.
I have written a closing sentence that encourages the reader.
I have written at least one zero conditional sentence in my tips section (for example: If you add too much sugar, the tea becomes too sweet).
Step 6: Peer Feedback

Swap your instructions with a partner. Read their instructions carefully and then give them helpful feedback using the guide below. Remember, good feedback is kind, specific, and helpful. It tells the writer what they did well and what they could improve.
Peer Feedback Guide:
Clarity: Were the instructions easy to follow? Could you actually do the task by following these steps? If any step was confusing, explain which one and why.
Structure: Did the writer include all the key features: a title, an introduction, a materials list, numbered steps, and tips? If anything was missing, say what it was.
Language: Did the writer use imperative verbs and time connectives? Give examples of ones you noticed.
Vocabulary: Did the writer use any of the vocabulary words from this unit? Which ones?
Suggestions: Write one specific suggestion for how the writer could improve their instructions.
After receiving feedback from your partner, revise your instructions. Make improvements based on their suggestions. Then write or type your final copy neatly.
Listening and Speaking Activities

Good communication is not only about writing. Being able to give and follow spoken instructions is a skill that you will use every day of your life, whether you are explaining a game to a friend, telling someone how to get to a place, or teaching a younger sibling how to do something. The activities below will help you practise giving and following instructions using your speaking and listening skills.
Activity 1: The Instruction Game (Pairs)

Work with a partner. One person is the instructor and the other is the follower. The instructor thinks of a simple task, such as how to make a paper aeroplane, how to draw a specific shape, or how to arrange objects on a desk. Without showing the follower what the end result should look like, the instructor gives step-by-step spoken instructions. The follower must listen carefully and carry out each step exactly as described. When the task is complete, compare the follower's result with what the instructor intended. Then switch roles and try again with a different task.
Discussion questions after the game:
Were the instructions clear enough? What made them easy or hard to follow?
Did the instructor use imperative verbs and time connectives? Give examples.
What would the instructor do differently next time to make the instructions clearer?
How did the follower feel when a step was confusing? What did they do?
Activity 2: Explain a Process (Individual Presentation)

Choose a process that you know well. It could be how to perform wudu (the Islamic washing ritual before prayer), how to make a simple snack, how to braid hair, how to look after a pet, or how to play your favourite game. Prepare a short spoken presentation of two to three minutes in which you explain the process step by step to your classmates or family members. Use clear imperative verbs and time connectives as you speak. You may use small prompt cards with key words to help you remember, but do not read from a full script. After your presentation, ask your audience if they have any questions and whether your instructions were clear enough to follow.
Presentation tips:
Speak slowly and clearly so that your audience can follow each step.
Use your hands to demonstrate actions where possible.
Make eye contact with your audience to keep them engaged.
Use time connectives such as first, next, then, and finally to signal each new step.
End by asking your audience: 'Is there any step you would like me to explain again?'
Activity 3: Interview About Daily Routines (Pairs)

Work with a partner. Take turns interviewing each other about your daily routines. The interviewer should ask questions like: How do you get ready for school in the morning? What steps do you follow when you prepare for bed at night? How do you organise your school bag? The person being interviewed should answer using time connectives and clear sequencing. The interviewer should take notes and then summarise their partner's routine back to them using reporting language, for example: 'You said that first you wake up at six o'clock, then you wash your face, and after that you eat breakfast.' Check whether the summary is accurate.

Challenge extension: After the interviews, write a short paragraph summarising your partner's routine. Use at least three time connectives and two zero conditional sentences. For example: 'If Amina does not set her alarm, she wakes up late.'
Activity 4: Group Instruction Writing (Small Groups)
Work in groups of three or four. Your group will create a set of spoken instructions for a task that the rest of the class will try to follow. Choose one of these tasks or come up with your own:
How to tidy and organise a messy classroom in five minutes
How to set up a simple obstacle course in the school yard
How to plan and prepare for a class party or celebration

In your group, discuss and agree on the steps. Assign one group member to be the presenter. The presenter will read or speak the instructions aloud while the rest of the class tries to follow them. After the activity, the class should give feedback on whether the instructions were clear, whether any steps were missing, and what could be improved. This activity helps you practise collaborative planning, clear communication, and giving constructive feedback.
Check Your Learning

You have now completed all the sections of Unit 5. Before you move on, take a moment to reflect on what you have learned. Read each statement below and decide honestly whether you can do it confidently, whether you can do it but need more practice, or whether you cannot do it yet. There is no wrong answer here. This is for your own benefit, to help you identify the areas where you are strong and the areas where you might need to review the material again. Be honest with yourself, because that is how you grow as a learner.
Self-Assessment: I Can...
For each statement, write Yes, I can do this confidently, I can do this but need more practice, or I need to study this again.
I can read a procedural text and identify its key features, including the title, materials list, numbered steps, and tips.
I can explain the difference between a procedural text and a narrative or information text.
I can identify and use imperative verbs (command words) in my own instructional writing.
I can use at least five different time connectives to sequence steps in the correct order.
I can explain what the zero conditional is and give at least three examples of my own.
I can tell the difference between the zero conditional and the first conditional.
I can write a complete set of instructions with a clear structure, appropriate vocabulary, and correct grammar.
I can use prepositions of position (in, on, under, beside, between, above) to describe where things are placed.
I can give spoken instructions that are clear, well-sequenced, and easy for a listener to follow.
I can listen to spoken instructions and carry them out accurately.
I can use the vocabulary words from this unit correctly in sentences.
I can evaluate a set of instructions and suggest ways to improve them.
I can give helpful, kind, and specific feedback on a partner's writing.

Well done for completing Unit 5! You have learned about the structure and language of procedural writing, you have practised using the zero conditional, and you have written your own set of instructions. These are skills that will help you not only in English lessons but in many other subjects and in your everyday life. Remember, the best way to improve is to keep practising. Try writing instructions for different tasks at home, explain processes to your family members, and challenge yourself to use the vocabulary and grammar patterns from this unit as often as you can. Keep up the excellent work, and may Allah bless your learning journey.

We combine rice and water in the pot before cooking.
To show or point out something.
The recipe will indicate how much salt you need.

You must navigate the steps carefully to get the right result.

The teacher will emphasize the safety rules before the experiment.

Good instructions communicate each step clearly.
To help someone feel confident or to support them to do something.
I encourage you to try this recipe at home with your family.

If you do not understand a step, inquire by asking your teacher.
To come out or appear from somewhere.
The cake will emerge from the oven golden and warm.
