A group of people born around the same time.
This recipe has been passed down from generation to generation.
A way to go from one place to another, or a section of a text.
Read the passage carefully before answering the questions.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Choose any three words from the vocabulary table above.
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you give me two more example sentences for each of these words?'
Then try writing your own sentence for each word and ask the tutor to check them.

💡  Did You Know?
The word bariis comes from the Somali language and simply means rice.
Basmati rice originally comes from South Asia, but it has been a staple in Somali cooking for centuries due to ancient trade routes between East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and India.
In many Somali households, bariis is served at lunch time and is the main meal of the day.

⭐  Top Tip
When you identify imperative verbs, look at the very start of each instruction step.
Most imperative verbs in procedural texts come at the beginning of a sentence because they are telling you what action to take.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you explain the difference between the zero conditional and the first conditional?'
Then ask: 'Can you give me five zero conditional sentences about cooking or food?'
Challenge: Ask the tutor to give you a mixed quiz with both zero and first conditional sentences, and see if you can tell which is which.

⭐  Top Tip
Here is a quick memory trick for the zero conditional:
Think of it as the 'Always Rule.' If you can replace the word 'if' with 'every time' and the sentence still makes sense, then it is a zero conditional.
Example: Every time you heat ice, it melts. This works, so it is a zero conditional.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
After completing Exercise 5, share your five sentences with your AI tutor.
Ask: 'Are these correct zero conditional sentences? Please check the grammar and tell me if any need fixing.'
If the tutor suggests changes, rewrite the sentences and ask for feedback again.

💡  Did You Know?
Professional recipe writers and technical manual authors always get other people to test their instructions before publishing them.
This process is called user testing. The tester follows the instructions exactly as written, and if they get confused or make a mistake, the writer knows that step needs to be rewritten more clearly.
Your peer feedback activity is a form of user testing!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are practising alone, you can do these speaking activities with your AI tutor.
For Activity 2, tell the tutor: 'I am going to explain how to [your chosen process]. Please listen and then tell me if my instructions were clear and in the right order.'
For Activity 3, ask the tutor: 'Can you interview me about my daily morning routine? Ask me questions and then summarise what I said.'

paragraphs: Story — Unit 5: Tell Me How
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Memory Wall”
The Memory Wall

At Umoja Primary School in Nairobi, Year 5 students returned from lunch buzzing with energy. Something was different. Near the library, a long piece of brown paper had been hung on the wall with the words:
“If you remember it, write it.”
Below, there were small passages, each written in a different student’s handwriting. One read:
“The time I got lost and found again at school.”
Another:
“When my cousin taught me how to ride a bike.”
“Who made this?” asked Malik, scratching his head.
“I don’t know,” said Laila, standing near the fringe of the growing group. “But it’s interesting.”
Their teacher, Miss Tendo, entered and smiled. “This is our Memory Wall. You’ll all contribute stories this week.”
“What kind of stories?” asked Malik.
“Anything that made you think, feel, or learn,” she said. “Your memories will help us understand our emotions — and our generation’s voice.”
Malik was nervous. “What if I can’t remember anything exciting?”
“If you combine honesty with emotion, it’ll be worth reading,” Miss Tendo said. “Start with a moment that surprised you or changed how you think.”
Laila whispered, “That’s hard. I’d rather draw.”
“You can combine images and words,” said Miss Tendo. “Draw and write. Communicate your way.”
That evening, Malik sat at his desk. He thought about his grandfather. The memory came in small pieces — just like the way his grandpa’s voice would slowly fade when he fell asleep telling stories.
Malik began to write:
“One day, Grandpa told me a story about a boy who was once a slave. He couldn’t read, but he remembered everything he heard. Grandpa said, ‘That boy’s memory was a gift — and so is yours.’”
He stopped. Was that memory good enough?
He reread his lines. Then added:
“I didn’t inhale deeply. I just kept listening until the words stayed with me.”
The next day, Miss Tendo led a group discussion.
“If you feel something important, write it,” she said. “If we combine stories and voices, we grow stronger.”
Laila raised her hand. “I want to include a quote from my grandmother.”
“What’s the quote?” Miss Tendo asked.
Laila smiled. “She said, ‘You don’t need permission to shine.’”
“That’s beautiful,” the teacher replied. “We should emphasize voices like that.”
“Some people might assume stories are only about big things,” said another student.
“But if you navigate a hard feeling, that’s a big thing,” Miss Tendo said.
After lunch, students took turns adding to the wall. Some paused or hesitated.
“Don’t hesitate, Malik,” said Laila. “Yours is good. Honest.”
He smiled and walked over. He pasted his passage next to Laila’s drawing.
Another student pointed at it and said, “Did your grandfather really say that?”
“He did,” Malik replied.
“That was wise,” the student said. “It made me think about my uncle, who tells stories too.”
That afternoon, Miss Tendo held a reflection circle.
“What emerged from our stories?” she asked.
“We’re not so different,” said Laila. “Everyone has a memory that made them feel scared, happy, or proud.”
Malik added, “If you listen carefully, people say things that stay with you. Even if the words fade, the meaning remains.”
Miss Tendo nodded. “That’s the power of communication.”
On Friday, the final day of the Memory Wall, Miss Tendo invited a local author to read student work aloud.
He read Laila’s drawing-story and Malik’s memory. He said, “I’m touched. These words are better than many books I’ve read.”
He paused. “Who wrote this one about the boy remembering everything he hears?”
Malik raised his hand. “It’s mine.”
“Did you know that generations ago, stories were passed only by memory? You’ve honored that tradition.”
Malik felt his heart race — but it was not fear. It was pride.
At home that night, Malik’s little brother asked, “What’s a slave?”
Malik thought carefully. “A slave is someone who was forced to work without freedom. But they still had thoughts. Stories. Hope.”
“Like Grandpa’s story?”
“Yes,” Malik said, smiling. “And now I wrote it down so it won’t be forgotten.”
The next week, the Memory Wall was taken down — but not thrown away. Instead, it was turned into a book.
“Your work will be shared with next year’s students,” said Miss Tendo. “You helped build something real.”
“I didn’t expect that,” said Laila.
“You believed in each other,” said Miss Tendo. “You united, didn’t you?”
“Definitely,” said Malik. “We didn’t exaggerate. We just told the truth.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences:
What was the Memory Wall? What was written on it when the students first saw it?
What did Miss Tendo ask the students to contribute?
Why was Malik nervous at first?
What memory did Malik choose to write about?
What was the quote from Laila’s grandmother?
What does Miss Tendo mean when she says, “If you navigate a hard feeling, that’s a big thing”?
What did the visiting author say about Malik’s writing?
How did Malik explain the word “slave” to his little brother?
What happened to the Memory Wall at the end of the story?
What is the main message or moral of this story?
Think and Discuss
Talk about these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor:
Have you ever written about a personal memory? How did it feel to share it?
Laila’s grandmother said, “You don’t need permission to shine.” What do you think that means?
Miss Tendo says that combining stories and voices makes us stronger. Do you agree? Why?
Malik says, “Even if the words fade, the meaning remains.” Can you think of a time when someone said something that stayed with you?
In the story, the students united through sharing. How can sharing our experiences help build a community?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 5 vocabulary words and write the sentence they appear in:
fade
slave
inhale
combine
unite
quote
assume
emphasize
communicate
hesitate
emerge
fringe
exaggerate
passage
generation
navigate
encourage
Grammar in the Story
Find examples of these grammar features in the story:
Find one example of the zero conditional (if + present simple, present simple).
Find one example of a tag question (a statement with a short question at the end).
Find one example of reporting language (telling what someone said without using their exact words).
Find two emphatic adverbs (words like definitely, clearly, truly).
Now You Try: Creative Response
Your Memory: Write a short passage (five to eight sentences) about a personal memory that made you think, feel, or learn. Follow the style of Malik or Laila.

Instructions for a Memory Wall: Imagine your class wants to create a Memory Wall. Write a set of clear instructions for how to set one up. Include a title, a materials list, numbered steps, and at least one tip.
Letter to Malik: Write a short letter to Malik (four to six sentences). Tell him what you thought about his writing and share something about your own family stories.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 6: A Different Type of Story
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Connectives and Conjunctions

This file teaches you all about connectives and conjunctions — the words that glue your ideas together. Work through each section, try the practice exercises, and then check your answers at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Section 1: What Are Connectives and Conjunctions?

A connective is any word or phrase that links ideas in your writing. A conjunction is a specific type of connective that joins words, phrases, or clauses within a sentence. People often use the two words to mean the same thing.
Why do they matter? Without connectives, your writing would be a list of short, choppy sentences. Connectives make your ideas flow and help the reader follow your thinking.
Without connectives:
"The rule was unfair. The students protested. The rule was changed."
With connectives:
"The rule was unfair, so the students protested, and eventually it was changed."
See how the second version reads more smoothly? That is the power of connectives.
Section 2: Coordinating Conjunctions (FANBOYS)
Coordinating conjunctions join two ideas of equal importance. The easiest way to remember them is the acronym FANBOYS:
Punctuation Rule
When a FANBOYS conjunction joins two complete sentences, put a comma before it:
"The trial began , and the room grew quiet." (two complete ideas — comma needed)
"Tendo read and shared the letters." (one subject, two actions — no comma needed)
Practice 1 — FANBOYS
Choose the best FANBOYS conjunction to fill each gap.
I wanted to help, ______ I did not know what to do.
You can come with us, ______ you can stay at home.
The students were tired, ______ they kept working.
She studied every day, ______ she wanted to pass the exam.
He did not read the notice, ______ did he ask about it.
They found old files, ______ many of them were illegible.
The rule was changed, ______ the students had spoken up.
Section 3: Subordinating Conjunctions

Subordinating conjunctions join a main clause (the main idea) with a subordinate clause (extra information that depends on the main idea). The subordinate clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence.
Common Subordinating Conjunctions
Punctuation Rule
When the subordinate clause comes first, put a comma after it:
"Because the rule was unfair , they protested." (subordinate clause first — comma needed)
"They protested because the rule was unfair." (main clause first — no comma needed)
Practice 2 — Subordinating Conjunctions
Fill in each blank with the best subordinating conjunction.
______ the students found the letters, they felt heartbroken.
Tendo spoke firmly ______ she believed in justice.
______ it rained heavily, the students kept working by window light.
They could not read the documents ______ the ink had faded.
______ you notice unfairness, you should speak up.
Musa searched the files ______ he found the folder marked 'Unheard Voices'.
______ the debate began, the headteacher sat in silence.
The law remained ______ brave students challenged it.
Section 4: Connective Adverbs (Linking Words Between Sentences)
Connective adverbs are words that link separate sentences rather than clauses within one sentence. They are usually followed by a comma.
Practice 3 — Connective Adverbs
Choose the best connective adverb to link these sentence pairs.
The students worked hard all week. ______, their debate was a great success.
The old files were full of stamps and long paragraphs. ______, some sections were illegible.
Asha believed the law was not meant to harm anyone. ______, Tendo showed that it did.
______, the power went out during the rainstorm.
The headteacher was impressed by the students. ______, she asked them to present at assembly.
Musa organised the timeline. ______, Tendo prepared her closing speech.
Section 5: Time Connectives for Storytelling
When you retell a story or write one of your own, time connectives help the reader follow the order of events:
First / Firstly — the start of the sequence
Then / Next / After that — what comes in the middle
Meanwhile / At the same time — things happening together
Later / Eventually — after some time has passed
Finally / In the end — the last event
Practice 4 — Retell in Order
Put these events from the story in order. Then rewrite them as a paragraph using time connectives (first, then, next, after that, finally).
The students held a school-wide debate.
The children did a role-play trial in the courthouse.
Tendo wrote in her journal about what she had learned.
The students found letters in a folder marked 'Unheard Voices'.
The headteacher praised the students and read their slogan.
Section 6: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Starting a sentence with 'And' or 'But'
In formal writing, avoid starting sentences with coordinating conjunctions. Use a connective adverb instead:
Informal: "And they found more letters." → Better: "Furthermore, they found more letters."
Informal: "But it was still unfair." → Better: "However, it was still unfair."
(Note: In creative writing and stories, starting with 'And' or 'But' is acceptable for effect.)
Mistake 2: Using 'because' at the start without finishing the thought
Wrong: "Because it rained." (This is not a complete sentence.)
Right: "Because it rained, they worked inside." or "They worked inside because it rained."
Mistake 3: Mixing up 'but' and 'so'
but = contrast (two different ideas): "The law helped, but it was incomplete."
so = result (one idea causes the other): "The law was incomplete, so they challenged it."
Mistake 4: Missing commas
Remember: use a comma before FANBOYS when joining two complete sentences, and use a comma after a subordinate clause that comes first.
Answer Key
Practice 1 — FANBOYS
1. but   2. or   3. yet   4. for   5. nor   6. but (or and)   7. for
Practice 2 — Subordinating Conjunctions
1. When / After   2. because   3. Although / Even though   4. because   5. If / When   6. until   7. Before   8. until
Practice 3 — Connective Adverbs
1. As a result / Therefore   2. Furthermore / In addition   3. However   4. Unfortunately   5. Furthermore / As a result   6. Meanwhile
Practice 4 — Retell in Order
Correct order: 2, 4, 1, 5, 3

Example paragraph: First, the children did a role-play trial in the courthouse. Then, the students found letters in a folder marked 'Unheard Voices'. After that, the students held a school-wide debate. Next, the headteacher praised the students and read their slogan. Finally, Tendo wrote in her journal about what she had learned.

Tendo prepared her speech. Meanwhile, Musa organised the files.
Unfortunately, many students were unaware of the rule.
Fortunately, the students decided to speak up.
The rule caused disadvantage. In addition, it showed disregard for fairness.
Shows what happened because of something
People protested. As a result, the law was reviewed.

🧩  Quick Memory Trick
Think of conjunctions as GLUE for your sentences. FANBOYS glue equal ideas side by side. Subordinating conjunctions glue a small idea onto a big idea. Connective adverbs glue two separate sentences together across the gap.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers and explain any I got wrong?
Can you give me more sentences to practise with?
What is the difference between 'although' and 'however'?
Can you help me use connectives in my own writing?

paragraphs: Unit 6: A Different Type of Story
Year 5 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

In this unit you will explore the many different types of stories that writers create. Every story belongs to a particular genre, which is the French word for type or kind. By the end of this unit you will understand several genres, recognise their special features, and be able to choose the right genre for your own writing. You will also strengthen your grammar by learning how to connect ideas smoothly using connectives and conjunctions.
Identify and describe at least five different story genres: adventure, mystery, fable, legend, and realistic fiction
Explain the key features that make each genre different from the others
Read a passage that introduces multiple genres and answer questions about it at different thinking levels
Use coordinating conjunctions (the FANBOYS words) to join ideas of equal importance
Use subordinating conjunctions such as because, although, when, if, and while to show how ideas relate
Use connective adverbs such as however, therefore, and meanwhile to link sentences smoothly
Plan and write the opening of a story in a genre of your choice, using a planning frame
Take part in speaking and listening activities including a genre detective game and a book recommendation talk
Evaluate your own writing and a partner's writing using a peer-feedback checklist
Reflect on your learning by completing a set of self-assessment statements
Key Vocabulary
Before you begin reading, study the words below. These words will appear throughout the unit. Make sure you understand each one so that you can follow the texts and use the words in your own writing.
Try to use at least five of these words in your writing tasks later in the unit. The more you practise, the more naturally these words will come to you.
Reading: A World of Stories

Read the following passage carefully. It is set in a school library where a librarian is speaking to a group of Year Five learners about different types of stories. As you read, think about the features of each genre that the librarian describes.
The Library Tour

"Welcome, everyone, to our school library," said Mrs Farah, spreading her arms wide. She stood in front of five colourful bookshelves, each one decorated with a different banner. "Today I am going to take you on a special tour. We are not going to walk through the library. Instead, we are going to travel through the world of story genres. Each of these five shelves holds a different type of story, and by the end of our tour you will know exactly which shelf to visit when you want a particular kind of reading experience."

She walked to the first shelf, which was decorated with a bright orange banner showing a compass and a map. "This is our adventure shelf," she said. "Adventure stories take characters on exciting journeys. The characters often face dangerous situations, solve problems, and discover new places. Think of a story where a young sailor must cross the Red Sea during a terrible storm, or where a group of friends travel through the Somali highlands searching for a hidden waterfall. In adventure stories, the setting changes often, and the plot is full of action. The main character is usually brave, although they might feel afraid at first. What makes adventure stories special is that they keep you turning the pages because you always want to know what happens next."

Mrs Farah moved to the second shelf. Its banner was dark blue, covered with magnifying glasses and question marks. "This is the mystery shelf," she explained. "In a mystery, something puzzling happens at the beginning of the story, and the characters spend the rest of the story trying to work it out. Perhaps a valuable book has gone missing from the school library, or a strange light appears on the beach every Thursday night. Mystery stories are built on suspense. The writer gives you clues along the way, but also includes false clues to keep you guessing. A good mystery makes you feel like a detective yourself, and when the answer is finally revealed, everything suddenly makes sense."

The third shelf had a green banner with pictures of animals wearing human clothes. "Here we have our fable shelf," said Mrs Farah with a smile. "Fables are short stories, often with animals as the main characters. The animals talk, think, and behave like people. Every fable teaches a moral, which is a lesson about how to live well. You may know the fable of the lion and the mouse, which teaches that even the smallest creature can help the mightiest. In Somali tradition, we have many fables too. There is the story of the clever fox who outsmarts the hyena by using patience rather than strength. Fables have been told for thousands of years because their lessons never grow old."

She then pointed to the fourth shelf, which had a golden banner decorated with stars and crescent moons. "This is our legend shelf," she continued. "Legends are traditional stories that have been passed down through generations. They are often set in the distant past and may involve heroes who perform extraordinary deeds. Some legends explain how things in the natural world came to be. For example, there are legends about how certain rivers or mountains were formed. Legends are different from fables because they do not always have a clear moral. Instead, they celebrate courage, wisdom, and the history of a community. When you read a legend, you are connecting with the stories your grandparents and great-grandparents told around the fire."

Finally, Mrs Farah reached the fifth shelf. Its banner was a calm sky blue, showing pictures of everyday objects like schoolbags, bicycles, and houses. "The last genre I want to show you is realistic fiction," she said. "These stories are made up, but they feel as though they could really happen. The characters are ordinary people dealing with ordinary situations, such as moving to a new neighbourhood, making a difficult decision, or learning to get along with a sibling. Realistic fiction helps us understand other people's feelings and experiences. It is like looking through a window into someone else's life. Although there are no dragons or magical powers, realistic fiction can be just as powerful as any other genre because it speaks directly to our own lives."

Mrs Farah looked at the class and smiled. "Now you know the five shelves," she said. "Remember, a good reader tries many genres. You might discover that you love mysteries one month and legends the next. The most important thing is to keep reading, keep exploring, and keep letting stories take you to new places, whether those places are across the ocean or just across the street."
Understanding the Text

Answer the following questions about the passage you have just read. The questions are arranged in three levels. Level 1 questions check that you understood the basic facts. Level 2 questions ask you to think more deeply. Level 3 questions challenge you to analyse, evaluate, or create.
Level 1 — Finding Information
Where is the passage set? Name the specific place.
How many bookshelves does Mrs Farah show the class?
What colour is the banner on the adventure shelf?
According to Mrs Farah, what do all fables teach?
Name two everyday objects shown on the realistic fiction banner.
Level 2 — Thinking Deeper
Why does Mrs Farah say that mystery stories are built on suspense? Use evidence from the passage to support your answer.
In your own words, explain the difference between a fable and a legend as Mrs Farah describes them.
Mrs Farah says that adventure stories keep you turning the pages. What features of adventure stories create this effect?
Why do you think the writer chose a librarian as the character who explains the genres? What does this tell us about the importance of libraries?
Level 3 — Analysing and Evaluating
Mrs Farah compares realistic fiction to looking through a window into someone else's life. Do you think this is a good comparison? Explain why or why not, giving reasons.
If you were adding a sixth shelf to the library for a genre Mrs Farah did not mention, which genre would you choose and why? Describe the banner you would design for it.
Think about a story you have read or heard before. Which of the five genres does it belong to? Give at least two reasons for your answer.
Text Feature Analysis
The passage uses direct speech throughout. Find one example of a reporting clause (such as 'she said' or 'she explained') and explain what it tells the reader about how the character is speaking.
Look at the way the passage is organised. Why do you think the writer described each genre in a separate paragraph? How does this structure help the reader?
Grammar Focus: Connectives and Conjunctions

When you write stories, you need to link your ideas together so that your reader can follow your thinking. The words that do this linking are called connectives or conjunctions. In this section you will learn three types: coordinating conjunctions, subordinating conjunctions, and connective adverbs. Understanding all three types will give you a powerful set of tools for building clear, interesting sentences.
1. Coordinating Conjunctions (FANBOYS)

Coordinating conjunctions join two parts of a sentence that are equally important. You can remember them using the word FANBOYS, which stands for For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So. Each of these words does a slightly different job.
For gives a reason. It works like 'because' but sounds more formal. Example: The travellers stopped to rest, for they had walked since dawn.
And adds information or joins similar ideas. Example: Amina packed her bag and set off on the long journey.
Nor joins two negative ideas. Example: The explorer had no map, nor did she have a compass.
But shows a contrast or an opposite idea. Example: The path was narrow, but the children walked bravely.
Or presents a choice between two possibilities. Example: You can read a mystery or you can try an adventure story.
Yet shows a surprising contrast, similar to 'but'. Example: The fable was very short, yet its moral stayed with me for days.
So shows a result or consequence. Example: It was getting dark, so they decided to set up camp.

When you use a coordinating conjunction to join two complete sentences, place a comma before the conjunction. Example: The detective found a clue, and she wrote it in her notebook. If you are joining just two words or phrases, you do not need a comma. Example: The detective found a clue and a footprint.
2. Subordinating Conjunctions

Subordinating conjunctions join a main clause (the most important idea) with a subordinate clause (an idea that depends on the main clause for its meaning). The subordinate clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence. Here are some of the most useful subordinating conjunctions and what they do.
Because gives a reason. Example: The boy ran home quickly because it was starting to rain.
Although shows something unexpected. Example: Although the journey was long, the family enjoyed every moment.
When tells us the time something happened. Example: When the sun set behind the hills, the village grew quiet.
If sets a condition. Example: If you read every day, your vocabulary will grow stronger.
Unless means 'if not'. Example: We will not finish the story unless we read two more chapters tonight.
While means 'during the time that'. Example: While the children listened to the legend, they imagined the ancient hero's face.
Until means 'up to the point when'. Example: The mystery remained unsolved until the final chapter.
After tells us what happened next. Example: After the fable ended, the teacher asked the class to discuss the moral.
Before tells us what happened first. Example: Before you choose a genre, think about what kind of mood you want to create.
Since can mean 'because' or 'from the time that'. Example: Since she loved animals, she always chose fables to read.

Notice that when the subordinate clause comes first, you place a comma after it. When the main clause comes first, you usually do not need a comma. Compare these two sentences: Although it rained, they played outside. They played outside although it rained.
3. Connective Adverbs

Connective adverbs are words or short phrases that link one sentence to the next. They help your reader understand the relationship between your ideas. Unlike conjunctions, connective adverbs usually appear at the start of a new sentence and are followed by a comma.
However shows a contrast. Example: The legend seemed simple. However, it carried a deep message about courage.
Therefore shows a result. Example: The clue pointed to the garden. Therefore, the detective searched every bush.
Meanwhile shows something happening at the same time. Example: The hero climbed the mountain. Meanwhile, the villagers waited anxiously below.
Furthermore adds extra information. Example: Fables teach moral lessons. Furthermore, they are enjoyable to read aloud.
Consequently shows a result, similar to 'therefore'. Example: The traveller ignored the warning. Consequently, he found himself lost in the forest.
In addition adds another point. Example: The mystery had an exciting plot. In addition, the characters were well developed.
On the other hand introduces a different viewpoint. Example: Some readers prefer adventure stories. On the other hand, others enjoy the calmness of realistic fiction.

Using a variety of connectives in your writing shows your reader that you can organise your ideas clearly. Try to avoid using 'and' and 'but' over and over again. Instead, choose from the full range of coordinating conjunctions, subordinating conjunctions, and connective adverbs that you have learned.
Grammar Practice
Complete the exercises below. Each exercise focuses on a different aspect of connectives and conjunctions. Write your answers in your exercise book, then check them against the answer keys provided.
Exercise 1 — Choose the Right FANBOYS Conjunction
Copy each sentence and fill in the blank with the correct coordinating conjunction from the FANBOYS list. Think about the meaning of each option before you choose.
The hero wanted to cross the river, _______ there was no bridge. (Choose: but / and)
Would you like to read a mystery _______ a legend tonight? (Choose: or / yet)
The fable was short, _______ it taught an important lesson. (Choose: yet / for)
Fatima finished her homework, _______ she went outside to play. (Choose: so / nor)
The children packed food _______ water for the long walk. (Choose: and / but)
Answer Key — Exercise 1
but
or
yet
so
and
Exercise 2 — Complete with a Subordinating Conjunction
Fill in each blank with a suitable subordinating conjunction from the box: because, although, when, if, unless, while, until, after, before, since.
_______ you enjoy exciting journeys, you should try an adventure story.
The detective did not give up _______ she found the missing jewel.
_______ the storm was fierce, the sailors kept the boat steady.
The children listened carefully _______ Mrs Farah told the legend.
You will not understand the moral _______ you read the whole fable.
_______ the story ended, everyone clapped.
He chose a mystery _______ he loved solving puzzles.
Answer Key — Exercise 2
Note: some questions have more than one acceptable answer. The answers below are the most straightforward choices.
If
until
Although
while / when
unless
After / When
because / since
Exercise 3 — Connective Adverbs
Rewrite each pair of sentences as two sentences linked by the connective adverb given in brackets. Remember to use correct punctuation.
The setting of the story was a small village. The characters felt very real. (Furthermore)
Yusuf preferred legends. His sister always chose mysteries. (On the other hand)
The plot was confusing at first. Everything made sense at the end. (However)
The adventurer reached the top of the cliff. The rest of the group prepared a meal at the bottom. (Meanwhile)
Answer Key — Exercise 3
The setting of the story was a small village. Furthermore, the characters felt very real.
Yusuf preferred legends. On the other hand, his sister always chose mysteries.
The plot was confusing at first. However, everything made sense at the end.
The adventurer reached the top of the cliff. Meanwhile, the rest of the group prepared a meal at the bottom.
Exercise 4 — Spot the Mistake
Each sentence below contains a mistake with the connective or conjunction. Rewrite the sentence correctly and explain what was wrong.
Although the book was exciting and I could not stop reading. (Hint: is this a complete sentence?)
The mystery was solved, the detective found the final clue. (Hint: what is missing between the two clauses?)
However the legend was very old it was still interesting. (Hint: where should the punctuation go?)
She wanted to read a fable. Because she liked animal stories. (Hint: can the second part stand alone as a sentence?)
Answer Key — Exercise 4
Although the book was exciting, I could not stop reading. (The subordinate clause needs a main clause to follow it, joined by a comma.)
The mystery was solved because the detective found the final clue. OR: The mystery was solved, so the detective celebrated. (A conjunction or connective adverb is needed between two main clauses.)
The legend was very old. However, it was still interesting. ('However' needs a comma after it and works best at the start of a new sentence.)
She wanted to read a fable because she liked animal stories. (The 'because' clause is subordinate and should be joined to the main clause, not written as a separate sentence.)
Exercise 5 — Write Your Own

Write five sentences about a story you have read or a story you would like to write. Use a different type of connective in each sentence. Try to include at least one coordinating conjunction, one subordinating conjunction, and one connective adverb. Underline the connective in each sentence.
Writing Workshop: Write a Story Opening

Now it is time to put everything you have learned into practice. In this workshop you will plan and write the opening of a story in a genre of your choice. A good story opening does three important things: it introduces the main character, it establishes the setting, and it hooks the reader so that they want to keep reading. You will write between one and two paragraphs, which should be roughly 100 to 150 words.
Step 1 — Choose Your Genre
Decide which genre you want to write in. Think about the features of each one that you learned from Mrs Farah's library tour. Use the descriptions below to help you decide.
Adventure: Your character goes on an exciting journey. The setting changes. There is danger or a challenge to overcome.
Mystery: Something puzzling happens. Your character must find clues. You build suspense by keeping the answer hidden.
Fable: Your main characters are animals who talk and behave like humans. The story is short and teaches a moral lesson.
Legend: Your story is set in the distant past. The main character performs a brave or extraordinary deed. The story feels traditional.
Realistic fiction: Your character is an ordinary person in an everyday situation. The events could really happen. The emotions feel true to life.
Step 2 — Plan Your Opening
Use the planning frame below to organise your ideas before you start writing. You do not need to write in full sentences at this stage. Short notes are fine.
Genre: Write the genre you have chosen.
Main character: Write the character's name, age, and one or two details about their personality or appearance.
Setting: Where and when does the story take place? Think about the time of day, the weather, and the surroundings.
Opening hook: What happens in the very first sentence to grab the reader's attention? This might be an exciting event, a mysterious question, or an interesting description.
Connectives I will use: List at least three connectives from your toolkit that you plan to include.
Step 3 — Read the Model Paragraph
Below is an example of a story opening in the mystery genre. Study it carefully and notice how the writer uses connectives, describes the setting, and builds suspense.
Model Opening (Mystery):

The old wooden door of the school library creaked open, although nobody had pushed it. Amina froze at her desk. She had been the only person in the room since the final bell rang, and she was certain that the caretaker had already locked the main corridor. However, there it was: the door, wide open, letting in a draught that made the pages of her book flutter. She stood up slowly because she did not want to make a sound. On the floor, just inside the doorway, lay a small folded note. While the rest of the school was silent and empty, someone had been here. Amina picked up the note, unfolded it, and read three words that made her heart beat faster: 'Check shelf seven.'

Notice the following techniques in the model opening. First, the writer begins with an unusual event, the door opening by itself, which immediately makes the reader curious. Second, the setting is described through small details: the old wooden door, the draught, the fluttering pages. Third, the writer uses several connectives. The word although introduces an unexpected detail. The word and links two ideas about the corridor. However signals a surprising contrast. Because explains why Amina moves slowly. While contrasts the silence of the school with the mysterious visitor. These connectives make the writing flow smoothly and help the reader follow the story.
Step 4 — Write Your Opening

Now write your own story opening. Use your planning frame from Step 2. Aim for one to two paragraphs and about 100 to 150 words. Remember to include at least three different connectives. Write in the past tense. Focus on creating a strong first sentence that will hook your reader.
Step 5 — Self-Check with the Writing Checklist
Before you share your work, check it against the list below. Put a tick next to each point that you have achieved.
My opening is in the genre I chose, and it shows the features of that genre.
I have introduced my main character with at least one detail about them.
I have described the setting so the reader can picture where the story takes place.
My first sentence is interesting and makes the reader want to continue.
I have used at least three different connectives (a mix of coordinating, subordinating, and connective adverbs).
I have written in the past tense throughout.
I have checked my spelling and punctuation carefully.
My sentences are varied in length: some short for impact, some longer for detail.
Step 6 — Peer Feedback
Exchange your story opening with a partner. Read their work carefully and answer the following questions in writing. Be kind and specific in your feedback. Good feedback helps a writer improve.
Which genre has your partner written in? How can you tell?
What is the strongest part of the opening? Tell your partner what you liked most.
Can you identify at least two connectives they used? Write them down.
Is there anything that confused you or that you think could be clearer? Suggest one improvement.
Does the opening make you want to read more of the story? Explain why or why not.

After receiving feedback from your partner, read their comments thoughtfully. Decide which suggestions you agree with and revise your opening. Remember, even professional writers revise their work many times before they are satisfied.
Listening and Speaking Activities

Good readers are also good speakers and good listeners. The activities below give you opportunities to practise these skills while exploring story genres. You can do these activities with a family member, a classmate, or a study partner.
Activity 1 — Genre Detective Game

In this game, one person describes a story without saying which genre it belongs to. The listener must guess the genre by paying close attention to the clues. Here is how to play. First, the speaker thinks of a story they know, whether it is a book they have read, a film they have watched, or a story someone told them. Second, the speaker describes the story in three or four sentences, mentioning details about the characters, the setting, and the plot, but never naming the genre. Third, the listener guesses the genre and explains which clues helped them decide. Fourth, swap roles and play again. Try to play at least four rounds so that you cover different genres. After each round, discuss whether the clues were clear enough or whether the speaker needed to include more genre-specific features.
Activity 2 — Book Recommendation Talk

Prepare a short talk of about one to two minutes in which you recommend a story to a friend. Your talk should include the following points. Name the story and its author if you know it. Tell your listener which genre the story belongs to and why. Describe the main character and the setting in a few sentences without giving away the ending. Explain why you enjoyed the story and why you think your listener would enjoy it too. Use at least two connectives to link your ideas, for example, 'Although the beginning is slow, the story becomes very exciting in the middle.' Practise your talk before you deliver it. Speak clearly, make eye contact, and try to show enthusiasm for the story you are recommending.
Activity 3 — Group Story Starter

This is a collaborative storytelling activity. You need at least two people, but three or four is even better. First, the group agrees on a genre. Second, the first person says one or two opening sentences that fit the genre. For example, if the group chooses adventure, the first person might say: 'The boat rocked dangerously as the storm rolled in from the east.' Third, the next person continues the story by adding one or two more sentences, using at least one connective. Fourth, keep going around the group, each person adding to the story. Try to keep the story in the same genre throughout. After five or six turns, end the story together by agreeing on a final sentence. Afterwards, discuss how well the group kept to the genre. Did anyone accidentally shift into a different genre? What connectives did the group use? This activity helps you think on your feet and practise using connectives in spoken language.
Activity 4 — Dramatic Reading

Choose a short passage from a story, either the passage about Mrs Farah's library tour from this unit or a passage from another story you enjoy. Practise reading it aloud with expression. Think about the following points as you prepare. First, vary your pace. Read exciting parts a little faster and thoughtful or suspenseful parts more slowly. Second, change your volume. If a character whispers, lower your voice. If a character shouts, raise it. Third, use pauses. A short pause before an important word or after a dramatic event creates suspense and lets the listener absorb what has happened. Fourth, if the passage includes direct speech, try to give each character a slightly different voice or tone. Perform your dramatic reading for a family member or partner. After your performance, ask your listener which part they found most interesting or dramatic and why.
Check Your Learning

Now that you have completed this unit, read each statement below and decide honestly whether you can do it. If you can, put a tick. If you are not sure, go back and review the relevant section before moving on. Being honest with yourself is the best way to make progress.
I can name and describe at least five different story genres: adventure, mystery, fable, legend, and realistic fiction.
I can identify which genre a story belongs to by looking at its features, such as the type of characters, the setting, and the plot.
I can explain what a moral is and give an example from a fable.
I can explain the difference between a legend and a fable.
I can use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) correctly to join two ideas of equal importance.
I can use subordinating conjunctions such as because, although, when, if, unless, while, until, after, before, and since to link a main clause with a subordinate clause.
I can use connective adverbs such as however, therefore, meanwhile, and furthermore to link sentences and show the relationship between ideas.
I can punctuate sentences correctly when using conjunctions and connective adverbs, including placing commas in the right positions.
I can plan a story opening using a planning frame that includes genre, character, setting, and opening hook.
I can write a story opening in a genre of my choice that includes a clear character, a described setting, and a hook for the reader.
I can give constructive peer feedback on a partner's writing using specific comments and suggestions.
I can participate in speaking and listening activities, such as a genre detective game, a book recommendation talk, and a dramatic reading, using clear speech and appropriate expression.
tables: a type or category of story, music, or art
My favourite genre is adventure because I love exciting journeys.
an exciting experience or a story about one
The adventure took the characters across deserts and mountains.
a story that involves solving a puzzle or crime
In the mystery, the detective followed clues to find the missing necklace.
a short story with animals that teaches a moral lesson
The fable about the tortoise and the hare teaches us that slow and steady wins.
a traditional story from the past that may or may not be true
The legend of Dhegdheer is a well-known story in Somali culture.
a made-up story that could happen in real life
The realistic fiction story was about a girl starting at a new school.
a word or phrase that links parts of a sentence or text
The connective 'however' showed that the second idea was different.
a joining word such as and, but, or, so
Use a conjunction to combine two short sentences into one.
a lesson about right and wrong taught by a story
The moral of the fable was that honesty is always the best choice.
the main events of a story arranged in order
The plot of the mystery kept me guessing until the very last page.
the time and place where a story happens
The setting of the legend was a village beside the Indian Ocean long ago.
a feeling of excitement or worry about what will happen next
The writer built suspense by not revealing the answer until the final chapter.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between a legend and a myth?
Give me an example of a fable from East African tradition and tell me its moral.
What makes realistic fiction different from a true story or autobiography?

📖  Did You Know?
The word 'genre' comes from the French language and originally meant 'kind' or 'type'. It entered English in the 1700s.
Somali oral tradition is one of the richest in the world. For centuries, stories, poems, and legends were memorised and passed down by word of mouth before they were ever written down.

💡  Top Tip
Keep a 'connectives toolkit' in the back of your exercise book. Write down every new connective you learn, sorted into three columns: coordinating, subordinating, and connective adverbs. When you write a story or essay, open your toolkit and challenge yourself to use at least one from each column.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my five sentences from Exercise 5 and tell me if I used the connectives correctly?
Give me three more subordinating conjunctions that I have not learned yet and show me how to use them.

💡  Top Tip
When giving feedback, use the 'sandwich' method: start with something positive, then give your suggestion for improvement, and finish with another positive comment. This makes your partner feel encouraged while still helping them grow as a writer.

📖  Did You Know?
In Somali culture, storytelling and poetry performances have always been a form of art and entertainment. Skilled storytellers, known as sheeko-xariir, could hold audiences spellbound for hours with their voices alone, using changes in pace, tone, and volume to bring stories to life.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
I am not sure about some of my Check Your Learning answers. Can you quiz me on the five genres to help me check my understanding?
Can you give me a short passage and ask me to identify which connectives are used and what type each one is?

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 6: A Different Type of Story
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Case of the Incomplete Rule”
The Case of the Incomplete Rule

The morning air in Bukoba town was heavy. Clouds hovered like thoughts waiting to fall. Inside the small red-brick courthouse, children from Liberty Primary School took their seats. They were here for a role-play activity — part of their civic education week.
Tendo adjusted her papers. “I’m the lawyer,” she whispered to Musa, who played the judge. “Let’s make this trial meaningful.”
“It already is,” Musa said. “We’re discussing a real law that was recently dismantled — one that caused a lot of discrimination.”
As the fake trial began, the classroom grew quiet. A teacher read from an old dusty passage:
“This rule denied students with a disability from attending top schools unless they passed a different exam.”
Gasps filled the room.
Tendo stood up. “This is not just unfair. It’s injustice,” she said firmly. “It shows clear disregard for fairness.”
“Objection!” shouted Asha, the pretend prosecutor. “This law was not created to harm anyone!”
“But it did,” Tendo replied. “Ask any student who felt unwelcome, uneasy, or ignored.”
After the trial, the students gathered in the library. “We need to understand how these rules were made,” said Musa.
They opened old files from the education office. Some were illegible, written in faded ink. Others were full of stamps and long paragraphs.
“Look at this one,” said Asha. “It’s full of crossed-out sections. It’s clearly incomplete.”
Musa pointed to a word. “And this part says the rule was reviewed during a time of civil unrest.”
They flipped pages, curious and focused. But Tendo stopped at a folder marked ‘Unheard Voices’.
Inside were letters from students across the country. One line read:
“I feel like I’m being punished for a condition I can’t change.”
“That’s heartbreaking,” whispered Asha.
Tendo sat down slowly. “These students never had a chance to speak in court, did they?”
Musa shook his head. “They wrote letters instead of giving speeches.”
Asha added, “Maybe because they thought no one would listen.”
“We’re listening now,” said Tendo, her voice firm. “And that matters.”
The group grew quiet again, not from sadness, but from understanding.
They weren’t just reading — they were witnessing something that should have never been forgotten.
“That rule didn’t just block schools,” Musa said finally. “It blocked hope.”
Later that day, the teacher led a group discussion.
“Why do you think this law lasted so long?” she asked.
“Because people were unaware of its effects,” said Musa.
“Because some were too uncertain to speak,” added Asha.
Tendo raised her hand. “And some felt incapable of changing the system.”
“That’s a powerful reflection,” the teacher said. “And what helped change it?”
“Voices,” said Tendo. “Letters. Stories.”
The next day, it rained hard. Power flickered. But in the back of the school, the students worked by window light. They were making a timeline of events.
“This part shows when the law was passed,” said Musa.
“And this is when protests began,” added Asha.
Tendo drew a scale of justice. “This symbolises what we must protect — even when things feel monotonous or slow.”
Asha grinned. “Your drawing is as clear as a gavel’s knock.”
“And your timeline is as navigable as a city map,” said Tendo.
At the end of the week, they held a school-wide debate.
Tendo opened her notes. “This law caused disadvantage,” she said. “It may have been legal, but it wasn’t just.”
Asha stood. “Unfortunately,” she began, “we didn’t fix it sooner.”
A pause.
“Fortunately,” she added, “we’re speaking now.”
The headteacher, who watched silently, stood up. “You children have reminded us of something important,” she said. “Old laws are not always right. Some must be questioned. Some must be changed.”
She looked at the board and read their slogan:
“Unfairness, once noticed, must be challenged.”
Then she nodded. “A good rule is one that welcomes everyone. And a good generation is one that makes room for all.”
Later that evening, Tendo wrote in her journal:
“The court was pretend, but the feeling was real. We weren’t actors — we were citizens. If we don’t speak, silence wins. But if we speak together, even an invincible law can fall.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Where is the story set? What activity were the children doing?
What was the old rule about? Why was it unfair?
What does Tendo mean when she says the rule shows "clear disregard for fairness"?
What did the students find in the folder marked 'Unheard Voices'?
Why does Musa say the rule "blocked hope"?
How did the students use evidence to support their arguments in the debate?
What does the headteacher mean when she says, "A good generation is one that makes room for all"?
What genre does this story belong to? Give two reasons for your answer.
Think and Discuss
Have you ever seen a rule that you thought was unfair? What did you do about it, or what could you do?
Tendo says, "If we don't speak, silence wins." Do you agree? Why or why not?
The children in the story are around your age. Do you think young people can really help change unfair rules? Explain your thinking.
How does this story connect to the Islamic value of standing up for justice (adl)?
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the story. Write the sentence it appears in, then write your own sentence using the same word.
discrimination
injustice
disregard
incomplete
illegible
unrest
unaware
uncertain
incapable
invincible
disadvantage
disability
monotonous
navigable
unfortunately
Language Features
The story uses several language features. Find an example of each:
A simile (a comparison using 'like' or 'as').
An example of direct speech with correct punctuation.
A sentence that uses the conjunction but to show contrast.
A sentence that uses because to give a reason.
A sentence written in the past continuous tense (was/were + -ing).
Creative Response
Choose one of these tasks:
Write a journal entry as if you are Tendo, Musa, or Asha on the evening after the debate. What are you thinking and feeling? (About 100–150 words.)
Write a letter to the headteacher explaining why you think the students' project was important. Use at least three connectives. (About 100–150 words.)
Design a poster with the slogan "Unfairness, once noticed, must be challenged." Include three reasons why speaking up matters.
tables:

Genre: Courtroom civic drama (realistic fiction).
Theme: Law, legacy, and the power of truth.
Setting: Bukoba, Tanzania — a school and a small courthouse.
Moral: Even unfair rules can be rewritten when brave voices speak.
Read it slowly. When you meet a word from your Vocabulary list, pause and say it out loud!

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 6: A Different Type of Story
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 6. They are grouped by their prefix or word ending so you can see how English builds new words from smaller parts. Learn a few words each day: read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then do the practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you pronounce the words correctly.
Group 1: Words with the Prefix un- (meaning 'not' or 'the opposite of')
The prefix un- is added to the beginning of a word to make it mean the opposite. For example, happy → unhappy means 'not happy'.
Practise Group 1
Cover the definitions. Can you remember what each un- word means?
Write three sentences of your own using different un- words from the table.
What does the prefix un- do to a word? Explain in your own words.
Group 2: Words with the Prefix dis- (meaning 'not', 'opposite of', or 'remove')
The prefix dis- works like un-. It makes a word mean the opposite or removes the original meaning. For example, agree → disagree means 'not agree'.
Practise Group 2
Look at the words dissatisfied, disadvantage, disability, disregard, discrimination, and dismantle. What base word can you find inside each one?
Write a sentence using discrimination that shows you understand what it means.
What is the difference between dissatisfied and uneasy? Both describe negative feelings.
Group 3: Words with the Prefix in- (meaning 'not')
The prefix in- also means 'not'. Before some letters it changes to im-, il-, or ir- (e.g., impossible, illegal, irregular), but in this unit we focus on in-.
Practise Group 3
Remove the prefix in- from each word. What is the base word? (e.g., incomplete → complete)
Use injustice and invincible in the same short paragraph (2–3 sentences).
Why do you think the prefix in- sometimes changes to im-? (Hint: try saying 'inpossible' — is it easy to say?)
Group 4: Words with Special Endings (-ible / -able / -ous)
Some English words end with -ible, -able, or -ous. These endings turn a word into an adjective (a describing word). Knowing these endings helps you read and spell longer words.
-ible and -able mean 'able to be' or 'can be': navigable = can be navigated, legible = can be read.
-ous means 'full of' or 'having the quality of': miraculous = full of miracles.
Practise Group 4
What is the opposite of illegible? (Hint: remove the prefix il-.)
Think of two more words that end in -ous (e.g., famous, dangerous). Write a sentence for each.
Think of two more words that end in -able (e.g., comfortable, reasonable). Write a sentence for each.
Use monotonous in a sentence about something in your daily life.
Summary: How Prefixes and Suffixes Work
A prefix is a group of letters added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning. A suffix is a group of letters added to the end of a word.
Vocabulary Activities
Activity 1 — Prefix Sorting
Sort all 25 vocabulary words into three groups based on their prefix. Which group has the most words?
Words with -ible / -able / -ous endings: _______________
Activity 2 — Fill in the Prefix
Add the correct prefix (un-, dis-, or in-) to complete each word.
The puzzle was ______complete, so we could not finish it.
Many people were ______aware of the problem.
She felt ______satisfied with the unfair decision.
They showed ______regard for the feelings of others.
The old writing was ______legible because the ink had faded.
He felt ______capable of speaking in front of the whole school.
The journey into the forest was ______familiar and exciting.
______fortunately, the game was cancelled because of rain.
Activity 3 — Match the Word to the Meaning
Draw a line to match each word to its correct definition.
unrest          —          treating someone unfairly
discrimination  —          unable to be defeated
invincible      —          very boring and always the same
monotonous      —          unhappiness that may lead to protest
navigable       —          able to be travelled through
Activity 4 — Use It in a Story
Write a short paragraph (5–7 sentences) about a character who faces an unfair situation. Use at least six vocabulary words from this unit. Underline the vocabulary words you use.
Activity 5 — Word Families
For each word below, write the base word and then any other forms you can think of:
discrimination → base word: ______ / other forms: ______
unfortunately → base word: ______ / other forms: ______
invincible → base word: ______ / other forms: ______
navigable → base word: ______ / other forms: ______
incomplete → base word: ______ / other forms: ______
Answer Key
Activity 2 — Fill in the Prefix
1. in (incomplete)   2. un (unaware)   3. dis (dissatisfied)   4. dis (disregard)   5. il (illegible)   6. in (incapable)   7. un (unfamiliar)   8. Un (Unfortunately)
Activity 3 — Match the Word to the Meaning

unrest = unhappiness that may lead to protest  |  discrimination = treating someone unfairly  |  invincible = unable to be defeated  |  monotonous = very boring and always the same  |  navigable = able to be travelled through
Activity 5 — Word Families (examples)

discrimination → discriminate, discriminating  |  unfortunately → fortunate, fortune, fortunately  |  invincible → vincible (rare), invincibility  |  navigable → navigate, navigation, navigator  |  incomplete → complete, completely, completion
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, spell it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!
unrest □   uneasy □   unexpected □   unsuccessful □   unfamiliar □   uncertain □   unaware □   unconscious □   unique □   unfortunately □
dissatisfied □   disadvantage □   disability □   disregard □   discrimination □   dismantle □
incomplete □   injustice □   incapable □   incompetent □   invincible □
illegible □   navigable □   miraculous □   monotonous □
tables: A situation where people are unhappy and may protest or cause trouble.
There was unrest in the city because people wanted to change the rules.
Feeling worried or uncomfortable about something.
I felt uneasy when I heard a strange noise in the dark.
Something that happens suddenly and is not planned.
The rain was unexpected, so we got wet without our umbrellas.
Not achieving the desired result or goal.
He tried to climb the tree, but he was unsuccessful and fell down.
Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before.
The new school was unfamiliar to me, so I felt a little nervous.
I felt uncertain about which game to play.
She was unaware that her friend needed help.
Not awake and not aware of what is happening.
After falling off his bike, he was unconscious and needed help.
One of a kind; very special.
My grandma has a unique necklace that no one else has.
Used to say that something is sad or disappointing.
Unfortunately, it started to rain during our picnic.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you test me on the un- words?
Can you give me more words that begin with un-?
Can you help me use 'unfortunately' in a sentence?
Not happy or pleased with something.
She was dissatisfied with her test score because she wanted to do better.
A situation that makes something harder or less good.
Not having a bike is a disadvantage when my friends ride to school.
A condition that makes it harder for someone to do certain things.
Some people with a disability use a wheelchair to help them move around.
To ignore something or not pay attention to it.
If you disregard the rules, you might get in trouble.
Treating someone unfairly because of who they are.
It is wrong to show discrimination against someone just because they are different.
To take something apart piece by piece.
We need to dismantle the old playground equipment before we can build the new one.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between dis- and un-?
Can you help me spell 'discrimination' correctly?
Not finished or not having all the parts.
My homework is incomplete because I forgot to answer one question.
When someone is treated unfairly or not given what they deserve.
It is an injustice when some children do not have enough food to eat.
She is incapable of lifting the heavy box by herself.
Not able to do something well or correctly.
He felt incompetent when he could not solve the maths problem.
Unable to be defeated or overcome; very strong.
The superhero felt invincible when he saved the city from danger.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you give me examples of words that use im-, il-, or ir- instead of in-?
Can you test me on the in- words?
Hard to read or not clear enough to understand.
My teacher said my handwriting was illegible, so I need to write more neatly.
Able to be travelled on or through, especially by boats.
The river is navigable, so boats can easily go up and down it.
Very special or amazing; seems impossible.
The way the injured bird flew again was miraculous.
Very boring because it is always the same.
The teacher's voice was monotonous, and it made it hard to pay attention.

Can you help me understand the difference between -ible and -able?
Can you give me a spelling test on these long words?
