paragraphs: Grammar — Unit 8: Share Your Views
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Six grammar mini-lessons

This file has six grammar mini-lessons for Unit 8. Do not try to learn them all in one day! Read one lesson, try the practice, and check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it.
Lesson 1: The Present Perfect Tense
What it means and why it matters

The present perfect tense is a special way to talk about actions that happened in the past but are still important now. It connects the past with the present. We use it to talk about experiences, achievements, and situations that continue. For example, "She has studied philosophy and physics" means she studied those subjects before, and that knowledge is still relevant today.
How to form it
subject + have / has + past participle
Use have with I, you, we, they.
Use has with he, she, it.
Regular verbs: add -ed (play → played, visit → visited).
Irregular verbs: learn by heart (go → gone, see → seen, eat → eaten, write → written).
Examples:
"I have visited the museum."
"He has eaten lunch."
"They have travelled to many countries."
"The temple has stood for centuries."
"She has studied philosophy and physics."
Important rule
Do not use the present perfect with a specific past time word like "yesterday" or "last week". Instead, use the simple past:
Wrong: "I have seen him yesterday."
Right: "I saw him yesterday."
Right: "I have seen him before." (no specific time)
Common mistakes and fixes
Mistake: "He have finished."  Fix: "He has finished." (use has with he/she/it)
Mistake: "She has went to the store."  Fix: "She has gone to the store." (correct past participle)
Mistake: "I have see a bird."  Fix: "I have seen a bird." (past participle, not base verb)
Memory tip
Think of the present perfect as a bridge between the past and the present. "Have/has + past participle" connects what happened before with what matters now!
Practice 1
Complete the sentences using the present perfect.
They ________ (eat) lunch already.
I ________ (finish) my homework.
She ________ (visit) the zoo three times.
We ________ (see) that film before.
The temple ________ (stand) for centuries.
He ________ (write) a persuasive letter.
Lesson 2: Using "For" and "Since" with the Present Perfect
What it means and why it matters
"For" and "since" help us talk about how long something has been happening. Understanding these words helps you express time clearly and precisely.
How to use them
For + a period of time (how long):
"I have lived here for five years."
"We have studied English for three months."
"They have known each other for a long time."
Since + a specific point in time (when it started):
"I have lived here since 2020."
"She has played football since she was six."
"He has studied maths since last month."
Common mistakes and fixes
Mistake: "I have worked here for 2020."  Fix: "....since 2020." (specific time = since)
Mistake: "I have been happy since two years."  Fix: "....for two years." (duration = for)
Memory tip
Think of for as a ruler that measures time (how long). Think of since as a pin on a timeline that marks where something began.
Practice 2
Fill in the gap with for or since.
I have been a fan of this team ______ three years.
She has lived in Nairobi ______ 2021.
We have known each other ______ a very long time.
He has studied Arabic ______ last September.
They have practised the song ______ two weeks.
Lesson 3: Adverbs of Degree
What it means and why it matters
Adverbs of degree tell us how much or to what extent something is true. They make your descriptions stronger and clearer. In persuasive writing, they help you emphasise your point.
Common adverbs of degree
very — "The ceremony was very beautiful."
quite — "He was quite precise in his speech."
really — "The film was really exciting!"
truly — "The ceremony was truly magnificent."
extremely — "She is extremely talented."
How to use them
Place the adverb of degree before the adjective or adverb it modifies:
"He is very tall." (before adjective "tall")
"She runs really fast." (before adverb "fast")
Common mistakes and fixes
Mistake: "He is very unique."  Fix: "He is truly unique." (unique already means one-of-a-kind)
Mistake: "He is very really nice."  Fix: Choose one: "He is really nice." or "He is very nice."
Memory tip
Think of adverbs of degree as "power boosters" for your adjectives. They make your descriptions stronger!
Practice 3
Complete the sentences with the best adverb of degree from the box: very, quite, really, extremely.
The temple was ________ magnificent.
She is ________ smart for her age.
I am ________ excited about the trip.
The homework was ________ easy.
Lesson 4: Adjective Order
What it means and why it matters

When we use more than one adjective to describe a noun, the order matters. If we get it wrong, the sentence sounds strange. English has a natural order for adjectives that native speakers follow without thinking about it.
The correct order
Follow this order when using multiple adjectives:
Quantity (one, two, three)
Opinion (beautiful, interesting, ugly)
Size (big, small, tall)
Age (old, young, ancient, new)
Shape (round, square, flat)
Colour (red, blue, golden)
Origin (Ethiopian, Somali, British)
Material (wooden, metal, stone)
Purpose (cooking, sleeping, writing)
Example: "a beautiful ancient golden statue" (opinion → age → colour)
More examples
"A lovely small blue ceramic vase." (opinion → size → colour → material)
"Three exciting new science books." (quantity → opinion → age → purpose)
"An old round wooden table." (age → shape → material)
Memory tip
Remember the first letters: Q-O-S-A-S-C-O-M-P (Quantity, Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Colour, Origin, Material, Purpose). Make up a silly sentence to remember it!
Practice 4
Put the adjectives in the correct order.
I saw a ________ cat. (black, fluffy, small)
She bought a ________ dress. (beautiful, red, long)
They visited a ________ museum. (fascinating, old, local)
We found a ________ stone. (round, ancient, grey)
Lesson 5: Similes
What it means and why it matters
A simile compares two different things using the words like or as. Similes make your writing more vivid and interesting. They help the reader picture exactly what you mean.
How to create a simile
Choose two different things to compare.
Think about what they have in common.
Connect them with like or as.
Examples:
"The prayer echoed like a telegraph." (both send messages over distance)
"Her smile was like sunshine on a rainy day." (both bring warmth and happiness)
"The wind howled like a frightened wolf." (both make loud, wild sounds)
"His heart raced like a drum during a parade." (both beat fast and loudly)
Common mistakes and fixes
Mistake: Using "than" instead of "like": "The cat is fast than a car."  Fix: "The cat is fast like a car."
Mistake: Comparing things that have nothing in common: "The cat is like a banana."  Fix: Choose things that share a quality.
Memory tip
Similes = "like" or "as" for vivid comparisons. If you can picture it, it works!
Practice 5
Complete these similes with your own ideas:
The clouds floated in the sky like ________.
She was as brave as ________.
His laughter rang out like ________.
The old building stood like ________.
Now change these sentences into similes:
The car was very fast. → The car was like a ________.
The night was dark. → The night was as dark as ________.
Lesson 6: Linking Words for Sequencing
What it means and why it matters

Linking words for sequencing show the order of events. They help your reader follow your story or argument step by step. This is especially important in persuasive writing, where you need to present your reasons in a clear order.
Common sequencing words
First / Firstly — to introduce the first point.
Next / Then / After that — to continue the sequence.
Finally / Lastly / In conclusion — to wrap up.
In addition / Furthermore / Moreover — to add more information.
Examples from the story
"First, they gathered."
"Then, the ceremony began."
"Finally, they celebrated the triumph."
Common mistakes and fixes
Mistake: Using linking words in the wrong order ("Finally" before "First").
Mistake: Forgetting to connect ideas. Fix: Make sure each sentence relates to the one before it.
Memory tip
Remember: First, Next, Last — like the beginning, middle, and end of a story!
Practice 6
Fill in the blanks with the correct linking word (First, Then, Finally):
________, we gathered our research materials.
________, we analysed the evidence carefully.
________, we presented our findings to the class.
Answer Key
Practice 1: Present Perfect
have eaten
have finished
has visited
have seen
has stood
has written
Practice 2: For / Since
for
since
for
since
for
Practice 3: Adverbs of Degree
Suggested answers (other correct answers are possible):
truly / really
quite
very / really
extremely / quite
Practice 4: Adjective Order
a small fluffy black cat
a beautiful long red dress
a fascinating old local museum
an ancient round grey stone
Practice 5: Similes
Answers will vary — check that each simile uses "like" or "as" and that the comparison makes sense.
Practice 6: Linking Words
First
Then
Finally
Unit 8: Share Your Views
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you will learn how people share their views and try to persuade others. Persuasive writing and speaking are everywhere: in posters that ask you to join an event, in letters to the headteacher asking for something new, in film reviews that recommend a movie, and even in the conversations you have with your friends every day. By the end of this unit, you will know how to tell the difference between a fact and an opinion, how to use powerful language to persuade an audience, and how to structure a piece of writing that makes people sit up and listen.

You will also explore a wonderful story called "The Temple of One Step", set in the highlands of Ethiopia. It is about two young students, Amin and Safiya, who investigate an ancient temple and discover that real power comes not from climbing high, but from thinking deeply. As you read, you will meet all twenty-five vocabulary words for this unit and see the grammar patterns in action.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Recognise and write persuasive texts such as posters, letters, and reviews.
Tell the difference between a fact (something that can be proved) and an opinion (what someone thinks or feels).
Use comparative and superlative adjectives to make your arguments stronger.
Write in both formal and informal registers, choosing the right tone for the audience.
Use the present perfect tense to talk about experiences: "I have visited the museum."
Use modal verbs such as should, must, and could to express opinions and recommendations.
Plan and write a persuasive poster, a formal letter, and a film review.
Present your views clearly in speech, using evidence and expressive language.
Key Vocabulary
Here are some of the most important words for this unit. You will find all twenty-five words in your separate Vocabulary file, but these are the ones you will meet most often in the lesson:
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day until you know all twenty-five.
Work through the grammar lessons in the Grammar file. Do one at a time, not all at once!
Read the Story file, "The Temple of One Step", and answer the questions.
Work through the sections below: Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Self-Assessment at the end to see what you have mastered.
Reading: A Persuasive Text
Save Our School Garden!
Read this persuasive poster carefully. It was written by Year 5 students at a school in Mogadishu.
Save Our School Garden!
Dear Students, Teachers, and Parents,

Have you ever walked past the empty patch of land behind the science block? Right now it is just dust and stones. But imagine if it were a beautiful, green garden full of vegetables, herbs, and flowers. We believe our school deserves a garden, and we need your help to make it happen.
Why do we need a school garden?

First, a garden teaches us important life skills. We would learn how to plant seeds, water crops, and care for living things. These are skills that many of our grandparents use every day, and they are just as valuable now as they were in ancient times. Second, growing our own food is healthier and cheaper than buying everything from the market. Fresh tomatoes, onions, and herbs could be used in the school kitchen, saving money and giving us more nutritious meals. Third, a garden makes the school more magnificent. Instead of a dusty, empty space, we would have a central area full of life and colour where students can sit, read, and enjoy nature.
What do the experts say?

According to a recent study, students who work in school gardens score higher marks in science. A physician from the National Health Board has also stated that children who eat fresh vegetables have stronger bodies and better concentration. This is not just our opinion — it is supported by evidence.
What can you do?

We are asking every class to donate a small bag of soil or a packet of seeds. Teachers, please give us one hour each week for garden time. Parents, if you have tools or experience with farming, we would love your help. Together, we can turn that empty patch into something we are all proud of.

The school garden is not just a nice idea — it is the best idea for our school this year. It will promote teamwork, teach us about the cycle of growth, and give us a triumph we can share. Let us take the first step today!
Join us at the Garden Launch on Friday at 2 p.m. behind the science block. Bring seeds, bring your friends, and bring your energy!
Written by the Year 5 Garden Committee
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
What is the empty patch of land behind the science block currently used for?
Give three reasons the writer says the school needs a garden.
What evidence does the writer use to support the idea that gardens are good for students?
What does the writer ask each class to donate?
Find one fact and one opinion in the text. Write them down and explain how you can tell the difference.
What is the "call to action" at the end of the poster? Why is it important in persuasive writing?
Language Analysis
Look at the text again and find examples of each persuasive technique:
Rhetorical question — a question asked to make the reader think, not to get a real answer. (Hint: look at the first paragraph.)
Emotive language — words chosen to make the reader feel something. Find two examples.
Expert opinion — when the writer mentions what a doctor, scientist, or other authority says.
Superlative adjective — a word that means "the most" (e.g. best, greatest, most important). Find one.
Modal verb — a verb like should, must, could, or would. Find three examples in the text.
Fact vs Opinion
One of the most important skills in this unit is telling facts from opinions.

A fact is something that can be checked and proved. For example: "A physician from the National Health Board stated that children who eat fresh vegetables have stronger bodies." You could check this by reading the study.

An opinion is what someone believes or feels. For example: "The school garden is the best idea for our school this year." Another person might disagree — they might think a new library is the best idea.
When you read a persuasive text, always ask yourself: "Can this be proved, or is it what someone thinks?"
Practice: Fact or Opinion?
Write F for fact or O for opinion.
The school has an empty patch of land behind the science block.
A garden would make the school more magnificent.
Students who work in gardens score higher marks in science.
The garden is the best idea for our school this year.
Fresh tomatoes and herbs can be used in cooking.
Everyone should help build the garden.
Answers: 1. F   2. O   3. F   4. O   5. F   6. O
Grammar Focus: Present Perfect Tense
The present perfect tense is used throughout this unit. It connects the past with the present — it shows that something happened before now and is still important or relevant today.
How to Form It
subject + have / has + past participle
Use have with I, you, we, they:  "I have visited the museum."
Use has with he, she, it:  "She has written a letter."
Regular verbs: add -ed (visit → visited, play → played).
Irregular verbs: learn by heart (see → seen, go → gone, write → written).
When to Use It
To talk about experiences: "I have explored an ancient temple."
To talk about achievements: "We have finished the poster."
To talk about something that started in the past and continues now: "She has lived here for five years."
With for (a duration): "They have studied for three hours."
With since (a starting point): "He has known her since 2020."
Common Mistakes
Mistake: "I have see the film."
Fix: Use the past participle, not the base verb: "I have seen the film."
Mistake: "He have finished."
Fix: Use has with he, she, it: "He has finished."
Mistake: "I have visited the zoo yesterday."
Fix: Do not use the present perfect with a specific past time. Say: "I visited the zoo yesterday." (simple past) or "I have visited the zoo." (present perfect, no time word)
Memory Tip
Think of the present perfect as a bridge: one end is in the past, the other end reaches to right now. The word have or has is the bridge.
Grammar Practice
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct form of have/has + the past participle.
I ______ (read) three books this month.
She ______ (visit) the ancient temple twice.
They ______ (study) philosophy for two years.
He ______ (write) a persuasive letter to the headteacher.
We ______ (see) the ceremony at the town hall.
The geographer ______ (travel) across the hemisphere.
Answers: 1. have read   2. has visited   3. have studied   4. has written   5. have seen   6. has travelled
Exercise 2: For or Since?
Fill in the gap with for or since.
I have lived in this city ______ seven years.
She has been a student ______ September.
They have known each other ______ a very long time.
He has played football ______ he was six years old.
We have studied English ______ three months.
Answers: 1. for   2. since   3. for   4. since   5. for
Exercise 3: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence has one mistake. Find it and write the correct version.
She have finished her homework.
I have went to the market.
He has see the new film.
They has eaten lunch.
We have visited the mosque yesterday.
Answers: 1. She has finished   2. I have gone   3. He has seen   4. They have eaten   5. We visited the mosque yesterday (simple past, not present perfect)
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Sentences
Write four sentences using the present perfect. Use the verbs in brackets.
(explore) — Write about a place you have explored.
(learn) — Write about something you have learned this year.
(help) — Write about someone you have helped.
(read) — Write about a book you have read.
Writing Workshop: Persuasive Writing
Now it is your turn to write a persuasive text! In this workshop, you will plan and write your own persuasive piece step by step.
Understanding Persuasive Writing
A persuasive text tries to convince the reader to think or do something. Good persuasive writing uses a clear structure:
Introduction — State your opinion clearly. Grab the reader's attention.
Reasons and evidence — Give at least three reasons with facts and examples.
Counter-argument — Think about what someone who disagrees might say, and explain why your view is still stronger.
Conclusion — Repeat your opinion and end with a powerful call to action.
Persuasive Language Toolkit
Use these language tools to make your writing more persuasive:
Rhetorical questions: "Don't you think every child deserves a safe place to play?"
Modal verbs: should, must, could, would, ought to.
Comparative adjectives: better, safer, more exciting, more important.
Superlative adjectives: the best, the safest, the most important.
Emotive language: words that make the reader feel something — imagine, wonderful, terrible, urgent.
Evidence and facts: statistics, expert opinions, real examples.
Repetition: repeating a key word or phrase for emphasis.
Call to action: telling the reader exactly what to do — "Join us!", "Sign up today!"
Formal vs Informal Register
The register is how formal or informal your writing is. You choose based on your audience.
Informal register (writing to a friend or classmate):
Use contractions: can't, won't, I'll, we're.
Use first person: I, we, my.
Use friendly language: "Hey, you should totally come!"
Formal register (writing to a headteacher, a newspaper, or a public audience):
Avoid contractions: cannot, will not, I will, we are.
Use third person where possible: "Students would benefit..."
Use polite, professional language: "I am writing to request..."
Writing Task 1: A Persuasive Poster
Design a poster to persuade students at your school to do one of these things:
Join a reading club.
Take part in a community clean-up day.
Eat healthier lunches.
Help younger students with their homework.
Your poster should include:
A bold, catchy title.
At least two reasons why people should take part.
A rhetorical question to make the reader think.
At least one comparative or superlative adjective.
A clear call to action (what to do, when, and where).
Writing Task 2: A Formal Letter

Write a formal letter to the headteacher of your school asking for something you think the school needs. It could be a new library corner, more sports equipment, a school garden, or anything else you feel strongly about.
Your letter should follow this structure:
Your address and the date (top right).
Dear [Headteacher's name], (formal greeting).
Paragraph 1: Say what you are writing about and what you want.
Paragraph 2: Give two or three reasons, using facts and evidence.
Paragraph 3: Explain what you would like the headteacher to do next.
Yours sincerely, and your name.
Remember to use formal register: no contractions, polite language, and clear reasons.
Writing Task 3: A Film or Book Review
Write a review of a film you have watched or a book you have read. Your review should:
Name the film or book, the genre, and the director or author.
Give a short summary of the plot (no spoilers!).
Share your opinion: What did you like? What could be better?
Use comparative adjectives to compare it with another film or book.
Give a rating out of five stars and a recommendation.
Listening & Speaking
Speaking Task 1: Present Your Views
Choose a topic you feel strongly about. It could be one of these or your own idea:
"Every school should have a garden."
"Children should be allowed to choose their own books to read."
"Homework is more helpful than harmful."
"Sport is just as important as studying."
Prepare a short speech (about one minute) to share your opinion. Use this structure:
State your opinion clearly: "I believe that..."
Give two or three reasons with evidence.
End with a strong closing statement or call to action.
Record yourself and listen back. Check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use persuasive language? Did you include evidence?
Speaking Task 2: Persuade a Partner
Work with your AI tutor or a family member. One person argues for the topic, and the other argues against it. Each person speaks for thirty seconds. Then swap sides!
This is called a debate. A good debater listens carefully, uses evidence, and stays polite even when they disagree.
Listening Task: Spot the Technique
Ask your AI tutor to read aloud a short persuasive text. As you listen, write down:
One fact you heard.
One opinion you heard.
One modal verb (should, must, could).
One rhetorical question (if there is one).
Oral Review
Give a spoken review of a film, a book, or a place you have visited. Use note cards to help you remember your key points. Try to use:
At least one comparative adjective ("It was more exciting than...").
At least one superlative adjective ("It was the best...").
An adverb of degree (quite, truly, extremely, really).
The present perfect ("I have watched this film twice.").
Check Your Learning
Activity 1: Persuasive Techniques Hunt
Read the "Save Our School Garden" text again. Copy and complete this table:
Activity 2: Comparative and Superlative
Complete the table with the correct comparative and superlative forms.
Answers: 1. better, best   2. more exciting, most exciting   3. scarier, scariest   4. worse, worst   5. more important, most important
Activity 3: Register Check
Rewrite each informal sentence in formal register.
"We can't wait for the garden to be ready!"
"You guys should totally come to the event."
"It's gonna be the best thing ever."
"I reckon we need more books in the library."

Possible answers: 1. We are very eager for the garden to be ready. 2. We strongly encourage all students to attend the event. 3. It will be an outstanding occasion. 4. I believe we require more books in the library.
Activity 4: Vocabulary in Context
Choose the correct word to complete each sentence.
The lawyer presented strong ______ (evidence / ceremony) in court.
Her speech was very ______ (persuasive / physical) and changed many minds.
The ______ (physician / geographer) gave the patient medicine.
The discovery of the ancient temple was a great ______ (phase / triumph).
We need to ______ (promote / descend) healthy eating in schools.
Answers: 1. evidence   2. persuasive   3. physician   4. triumph   5. promote
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I don't understand. Can you explain it in a different way?"
Practise speaking: "Can you listen to my speech and help me improve it?"
Get feedback: "Can you check my persuasive letter and tell me what to fix?"
Ask for more practice: "Can you give me more fact-or-opinion questions?"
Prepare for your teacher: "What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Self-Assessment: Can I Do It?
Tick "Yes" or "Not yet". For every "Not yet", ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can tell the difference between a fact and an opinion.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can identify persuasive techniques in a text.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use comparative and superlative adjectives.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write in formal and informal register.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use the present perfect tense correctly.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can plan and write a persuasive poster, letter, or review.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can present my views clearly in speech.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use modal verbs to express opinions.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 9
In Unit 9, you will continue to build on the skills you have learned here.
Make sure you know all twenty-five vocabulary words from your Word Wall.
Practise writing in both formal and informal register — the more you write, the easier it gets.
Tell your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 9? Can you get me ready?"
Well done! You have worked hard to share your views and persuade others. Keep reading, keep writing, and keep thinking critically about the world around you.

paragraphs: Story — Unit 8: Share Your Views
Year 5 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Temple of One Step”
The Temple of One Step
In the highlands of northern Ethiopia, surrounded by acacia trees and winding trails, sat a small town called Lomi. It had no airport, no paved roads — but it did have a legend.
“The temple stands without steps,” the villagers often said. “No stairs, no ramps — just walls. Yet people reached it.”
Year 5 students from the Lomi Academy had heard this story every year. But this time, their teacher, Miss Rahmat, decided to turn the tale into an investigation.
“You will research the old faith site,” she said. “Your goal is to find evidence. Ask questions. Use your intelligence. You might find more than you expect.”
The class split into teams. Amin and Safiya formed Team Sandalwood, named after the tree growing near the old river path.
As they hiked to the temple’s central courtyard, they passed broken stones and ancient tools. Birds circled above.
“It’s quiet,” whispered Amin.
“Too quiet,” said Safiya. “Like a ceremony ended but didn’t clean up.”
They reached the site. The temple stood magnificent and tall — walls covered in symbols, the doors carved with spirals.
“Miss Rahmat said this temple has stood here for centuries,” said Amin. “And there’s no staircase?”
“Not one,” said Safiya. “But people went inside. That’s the mystery.”
They circled the structure. On one side, a tunnel opened between rocks.
“This could be the entrance,” Amin said.
“It might also be a trap for goats,” Safiya warned.
They crawled through anyway. Inside was cool, with a soft echo. The floor was rough, but the walls had hand-painted drawings — evidence of belief systems, myths, and planets.
“There’s a cycle here,” Safiya pointed. “See? Sun, moon, storm, harvest. Then repeat.”
Amin added, “That’s how early people understood time.”
They took notes and photos.
At the back, they saw a phrase carved in two languages:
“Power is not in height. One step is a phase.”
Back at school, they shared findings.
“This wasn’t a temple of climbing,” said Safiya. “It was about thought. Intelligence. They believed each stage of life was a phase.”
Amin added, “They connected faith to physics and the stars.”
“But how do we know they believed this?” asked another student.
Safiya held up a sketch. “Because they left precise markings. The same ones appear in our history books — used in the old judicial scrolls.”
“Why judicial?” asked Miss Rahmat.
“Because justice was part of belief,” said Safiya. “Citizenship meant knowing the stars, the land, and the law.”
Miss Rahmat smiled. “You’ve used your minds well. But your job’s not over.”
She held up an envelope. “Inside is a poem written by a physician from 200 years ago. He walked the same hills. Translate it. Present what you learn.”
The group met under a tree the next day.
Safiya read aloud:

“I have walked in search of shape,
Faith a wheel I cannot break.
My heart is not innocent of doubt,
But it has stood the physical ache.”
“What does that mean?” asked Amin.
“That belief isn’t perfect,” Safiya replied. “It’s not easy. It changes — but endures.”

Over the next week, the class created a timeline of ideas. They connected philosophy, physics, and old stories. They mapped how ideas travelled across the hemisphere, through trade, war, and whispers.
“We’ve studied for seven days,” Amin said. “And I’ve never thought this deeply.”
“I’ve never worked since last year with this much focus,” said Safiya.
“The spread of these ideas,” added Miss Rahmat, “was a triumph. Not by war, but by word.”
The students gasped. In that moment, a light breeze passed through the open windows. The mural in their classroom fluttered.
“It’s like the past is still here,” whispered Amin.
On presentation day, the class arranged stones and photos, words and sketches.
Safiya explained: “The temple was not about walls, but about thought. One step was not a staircase. It was a mindset.”
Amin said, “This descent into the earth was part of learning. Not climbing up, but down — into self, into story.”
Another student pointed at the map. “Ideas moved like a telegraph — quiet but fast. Across towns, across time.”
The audience clapped. A visiting geographer from the national museum nodded with interest.
After the presentation, the geographer said, “I’ve visited many ancient sites. But few students have ever explained the layers so clearly.”
He paused at a drawing.
“This simile,” he said. “‘The prayer echoed like a telegraph’ — who wrote that?”
“I did,” said Safiya, smiling.
“It’s quite elegant,” he replied. “Truly expressive.”
“Thanks,” she said. “It just… fit.”
That weekend, the team hiked back to the temple to place a small offering: a flat stone with their names etched in chalk.
“We’re not trying to own the temple,” said Amin.
“We’re recognising its voice,” added Safiya.
They stood quietly. A long ceremony had ended, but a new one had begun.
That evening, as they sat on the school roof, watching the stars, Amin asked, “Do you think we’ll forget this?”
“No,” Safiya said. “We’ve seen real things. Felt them. You don’t forget things that feel physical.”
She lay back, eyes on the sky. “Faith isn’t one thing. It’s many things held together by belief.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Where is the town of Lomi, and what is unusual about its famous temple?
What did Miss Rahmat ask the students to do, and what was their goal?
What did Amin and Safiya find inside the temple? Describe at least three things.
What was the phrase carved at the back of the temple? What do you think it means?
Why did Safiya say that citizenship meant knowing the stars, the land, and the law?
What did the physician’s poem talk about? Put it in your own words.
How did the class present their findings on presentation day?
What did the visiting geographer say about Safiya’s simile? Why did he like it?
Why did Amin and Safiya go back to the temple at the weekend?
What does Safiya mean when she says, “You don’t forget things that feel physical”?
Think and Discuss
The carved phrase says, "Power is not in height. One step is a phase." What lesson does this teach us about learning and growing?
Safiya says that belief "changes but endures." What do you think she means? Can you think of something in your own life that has changed but still stayed important?
The geographer says that few students have explained the layers of an ancient site so clearly. Why do you think Amin and Safiya were so good at it?
The story says ideas spread "not by war, but by word." Can you think of an example from your own life where words were more powerful than force?
If you could investigate an ancient site in your country, where would you go and what would you look for?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 8 vocabulary words in bold and write the sentence each one appears in:
intelligence
citizenship
ancient
magnificent
central
cycle
evidence
precise
ceremony
judicial
innocent
descent
philosophy
hemisphere
geographer
telegraph
physician
physics
triumph
phase
physical
Grammar in the Story
Find examples of these grammar patterns in the story and write them down:
Present perfect tense (have/has + past participle) — find at least three examples.
For/since — find one sentence with "for" and one with "since".
Adverbs of degree (quite, truly, exactly) — find two examples.
A simile (a comparison using "like" or "as") — find at least one.
Now You Try: Writing
Imagine you are Amin or Safiya. Write a persuasive speech (5–8 sentences) convincing your class to investigate an ancient site in your area. Use at least three persuasive techniques.
Write a short reflection (3–5 sentences) about what the story taught you. Use the present perfect at least once.
The physician wrote a poem about belief. Write your own short poem (4 lines) about something you believe in. Try to include a simile.

Group 1: People and Roles

My Word Wall

Vocabulary Practice Activities
Activity 1: Match the Word to the Meaning
Draw a line from each word to its meaning.
philosophy     a) a great victory
evidence       b) very old; from a long time ago
triumph        c) the study of big ideas about life
ancient        d) something that shows a claim is true
hemisphere     e) half of the Earth
Answers: 1–c   2–d   3–a   4–b   5–e
Activity 2: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word to complete each sentence.
The ________ (ceremony / cycle) of the seasons repeats every year.
The detective found ________ (evidence / descent) at the scene.
We learned about the ________ (judicial / physical) system in our civics class.
The ________ (physician / geographer) helped the sick child recover.
The ________ (ancient / recent) temple has stood for over a thousand years.
Answers: 1. cycle   2. evidence   3. judicial   4. physician   5. ancient
Activity 3: Word Families
Some of these words belong to the same family. Group the words that are connected:
physics, physical, physician, physiology, philosophy
Hint: Which words come from the Greek root meaning "nature" or "body"?
Answer: physics, physical, physician, and physiology all come from the Greek root "phys" meaning nature/body. Philosophy comes from "philo" (love) + "sophia" (wisdom).
Activity 4: Use It in a Story
Write a short paragraph (4–6 sentences) about a visit to a special place. Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit. Underline each vocabulary word you use.
